question: Across studies on ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, and their oxidation products, what range of melting points has been reported for these ultra-high-temperature ceramic borides, carbides, and oxides?
answer:
Across studies on ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, and their oxidation products, the reported range of melting points for these ultra-high-temperature ceramic borides, carbides, and oxides is as follows:

- ZrB2 has a melting point of 3245 ◦ C [7].
- HfB2 has a melting point of 3380 ◦ C [18].
- ZrC has a melting point of 3718 K (which is approximately 3445 ◦ C) [20].
- HfC has a melting point of 4228 K (which is approximately 4000 ◦ C) [20].
- The oxide of Zr, ZrO2, has a melting point of 2715 ◦ C [1].
- The oxide of Hf, HfO2, has a melting point of 2810 ◦ C [1].
- The oxide of Ta, Ta2O5, has a melting point of 1800 ◦ C [1].

Therefore, the range of melting points reported for these materials spans from 1800 ◦ C for Ta2O5 up to approximately 4000 ◦ C for HfC.

refractory carbides, nitrides and borides of Hf, Zr and Ta, etc, which are classified as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), have remarkably raised owing to the striking properties, like supernal melting points, high excellent thermal conductivity, moderate coefficient of thermal expansion and high strength and modulus [4 e 7]. Refractory carbides including HfC (3890 /C14 C), TaC (3880 /C14 C) and ZrC (3540 /C14 C) with the highest melting points among UHTCs were reported to have lower vapor pressures than those of nitrides and borides, which were considered promising candidate materials for TPS [8 e 11]. The corresponding solid oxidation products, HfO2 (2810 /C14 C) and ZrO2 (2715 /C14 C) have higher melting points than that of Ta2O5 (1800 /C14 C), which could be more stable in environments above 2000 /C14 C [12]. However, the implementation of pure HfC and ZrC is restricted due to the weak oxidation inhibition at intermediate temperatures, forming porous oxide scale and making it susceptible to the ablative environment [13,14].

Introduction
Borides, carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals such as ZrB2, HfB2, HfC, or HfN belong to a class of ceramics defined ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) with extremely high melting temperatures. These materials are of particular interest because of the unique property combination of high refractoriness, high-electrical and -thermal conductivity, chemical inertness against molten metals or nonbasic slags and good thermal shock resistance. 1,2 These properties make them attractive candidates for high-temperature applications where corrosion-wear-oxidation resistance are demanded. ZrB2 has several applications such as Hall-Heroult cell cathodes for electrochemical processing of aluminium, evaporation boats, crucibles for handling molten metals, thermowell tubes for steel refining, thermocouples sleeves for high-temperature uses, nozzles, plasma electrodes, or as dispersoid in metal and ceramic-matrix composites for heaters and igniters. In addition, ZrB 2 is a metallic conductor with electrical resistivity comparable with those of their parent metals. This permits to produce complex components at reduced shaping costs. HfB2, because of its higher costs of production, has found a limited number of applications, in wear-resistant coatings for instance. Similar to ZrB2, HfB2 has also been recently considered in ultra-high-temperature composites for aerospace applications (leading edge parts on re-entry vehicles), in conjuction with SiC, because of its excellent refractoriness. 3,4

1. Introduction
Refractory transition metal borides and carbides have extremely high melting points of over 3000 ◦ C and hence are referred to as ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). Even though they have been studied since the 1960s, there has been recent interest in these materials as potential candidates for thermal protection systems on hypersonic vehicles. A developmental history of UHTC materials can be found in Opeka et al. 1 and they have recently been reviewed by Paul et al. 2 The initial selection of UHTC materials was based on their melting temperatures, however oxidation temperature and the melting points of their oxides is, in fact, more critical. There are a number of materials with melting points over 3000 ◦ C, whose oxides also have melting points in excess of 2500 ◦ C, for example ZrB2, HfB2 and HfC. These materials are widely studied for high temperature

The desired properties may require the development of fibre reinforced UHTC composites to enable viable application development beyond 2500 ◦ C. For such hybrid materials, carbon fibre is the preferred choice owing to its high strength, ready availability and ability to be formed into complex shapes, 5 provided it can be protected from oxidation.

1. Introduction
Advanced materials offering a temperature capability significantly exceeding 2000 °C in extreme, highly corrosive environments are required for a range of aerospace and other applications where oxidation and/or erosion resistance is needed at very high temperatures [1]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are typically based on the borides, carbides and nitrides of refractory transition metals, including hafnium, zirconium and tantalum amongst others [1-3], not only offer extremely high melting points of over 3000 °C, but their oxides also typically have melting points over 2500 °C. Both hafnium and zirconium diborides, HfB2 and ZrB2, in particular have been extensively studied as innovative thermal protection systems (TPS) [4-6] and sharp leading edge components [7-9] for aerospace vehicles. They have typically been investigated as monolithic components; however they exhibit both poor thermal shock and oxidation resistance [10].
The use of fibre reinforcement can significantly improve the

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Even though borides are more extensively used for the ultra-high temperature applications, some carbides, such as the mono-carbides of Hf, Zr, Ta and Nb, are also classified as UHTCs. Unlike the borides, these carbides crystallize in cubic close packed structures. The more commonly used HfC, ZrC and TaC possess face centered cubic or the NaCl structure (Wuchina, 2007; Pierson, 1996; Pierrat, 2010) (Figure 2a). Usually the host metal atoms form cubic close packed structures with

Compared  to  the  borides, still  higher  melting points have been reported for the carbides, with some solid solution carbides like Ta x Hf 1-x Cy possessing melting points in excess of 4000 o C (Cotton, 2010; Wuchina, 2007). However, unlike the borides, some issues related to their oxidation resistance (Wuchina, 2007) restrict their applications and this will
be discussed in the following section. In contrast to the M-B, the published M-C phase diagrams (Figure 2b) reveal presence of considerably wider homogeneity range, which implies presence of non-stoichiometry in their compositions and related defects in their lattices. Also, the presence of the defects such as carbon vacancies  allows  tailoring  of  the  thermo-physical  and  thermo-mechanical  properties (Wuchina, 2007; Pierrat, 2010; Wuchina, 2004).

5.2. ZrB2 ceramics
The sintering temperature range for densification of ZrB2 is influenced by melting point of the silicides used as the sinter aids [34,134,136,137]. The densification temperature (1600 °C) is lower for ZrSi2-reinforced ZrB2 when compared to other silicides since the melting point of ZrSi2 is relatively low. Sciti et al. [112,134,149] extensively studied the use of MoSi2 sinter-additive for ZrB2/ HfB2. Their work revealed that achievement of near-theoretical sinter-density was possible for ZrB2 with the use of MoSi2 (3-20 vol %) via PS at 1900 °C, HP at 1800 °C and SPS at 1750 °C. The densification was assisted by transient liquid phase that formed due to the reaction between MoSi2 and surface oxide impurities on ZrB2, as was shown by Eq. (30). During hot pressing, the plastic deformation of silicides at high temperatures (> 800 °C) would also contributes to the densification of ZrB2 as the deformation forces soft silicide particles to fill in the voids left by ZrB2 particles [112,134].

Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

Introduction
Important previous studies reported that MI of the Zr-Ti alloy is effective for forming ZrC and TiC dual phase matrices in porous carbon materials [25] and C/C preforms [26]. Zr-Ti alloys are all-proportional solid solutions, where the melting point of the alloys with Ti contents of 0-80 at% varies within the range of 1540-1700 /C176 C, which is higher than the melting point of Si and lower than the melting point of Zr [27]. The mechanical properties and heat resistance of Si-free Cf/UHTCMCs also overcome the heat resistance limitation determined by the evaporation temperature of B2O3 and SiO2. Previous studies showed that Zr-Ti alloy infiltration is effective for improving the heat resistance of C/UHTCMCs [26]. However, the applicability of these materials at extreme temperatures above 2000 /C176 C is unknown.
Thus, the objective of this study is to evaluate the potential utility of C/UHTCMCs with Zr-Ti alloy infiltration at temperatures of 2000 /C176 C or above. To evaluate the applicability of these materials, three types of C/UHTCMCs fabricated using different alloy compositions are subjected to arc-wind tunnel testing. The heat flux is set to below and above 2000 /C176 C. The microstructure is characterized, and the degradation behavior is examined based on thermodynamic analysis.

3.2. Oxidation behavior of the SiC/(Hf0.2Ta0.8)C nanocomposites
To reveal the intrinsic reasons for the excellent oxidation resistance of  SiC/(Hf0.2Ta0.8)C  nanocomposite  materials in  the  medium to  high temperature  range,  XRD  characterization  was  carried  out  on  the oxidized, and the results are shown in Fig. 5. It can be observed that within this temperature range, the characteristic peaks of SiO2  are the most prominent. In addition, oxides such as Ta2O5, and Hf6Ta2O17 can also be detected. Based on previous studies [20], it is speculated that the high melting-point phases in the oxide scales (e.g., Hf6Ta2O17) act as frameworks, while the low melting-point phases (e.g., glassy phase SiO2) act as melt-filling phases, which jointly form the structurally favored oxide  scales,  thereby  slowing  down  the  oxidation  rate.  Interestingly, non-stoichiometric  Ta2O5-x appears  at  oxidation  temperature  higher than  1400 ◦ C  [40].  Studies  have  shown  that  the  Ta-O  system  has  a broadened  oxygen  solubility  at  temperatures  higher  than  1000 ◦ C, which allows for the generation of non-stoichiometric Ta2O5-x  in addition to the generation of thermodynamic TaO2 and Ta2O5 stable phases [41].
Firstly, from 1200 ◦ C to 1600 ◦ C, the intensity of the SiO2 reflection peaks undergo a first increase and then slightly decrease process. This is mainly because as the temperature rises, the continuous passive oxidation of SiC produce a dense SiO2 layer with low oxygen diffusion coefficient [17]. However, when the temperature reaches 1500 -1600 ◦ C, a severe reaction occurs between SiC and SiO2, leading to the consumption of the SiO2 protective scale and the generation of SiO and CO [42].

3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
Fig. 7 shows the 1800 ◦ C-flexural strength versus sintering temperature of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics with and without C dopant, compared with several typical ZrB2  and HfB2-based UHTCs. In the previous studies, in order to obtain superior high-temperature properties, some refractory materials have been considered to be added into the matrix. The most widely  used  refractory  phases  were  ZrC  [15,40]  and  SiC  [13,38].  In addition, high-temperature resistant phases such as WC [41 -43], B4C [44],  Mo2C  [45]  and  SiB6 [46]  were  also  considered.  For  superior strength at ultra-high temperatures, it is generally not recommended to employ sintering additives with low melting point. Consequently, the sintering temperature of these reported ZrB2-based UHTCs with superior high-temperature strength typically exceeds 1800 ◦ C, resulting in significant energy expenditure. In contrast, introducing sintering additives with low melting point into ZrB2 ceramics can significantly reduce the densification  temperature,  however  the  residual  lower  melting  point phases would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs (e.g., the strength of ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2 at 1800 ◦ C is only 30 MPa). In the current work, active carbon was added into the ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic exhibited excellent flexural strength (390 MPa) at 1800 ◦ C, surpassing that of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC [15,40], ZrB2-30 vol% SiC [38], HfB2-20 vol% SiB6  [46], and HfB2-5 vol% SiC-5 vol% WC [41]. 2. Crystal structure, bonding and phase diagram of transition metal borides
Both HfB and HfB2 phases have homogeneity range and HfB2 has the highest melting point (3380 °C) [57,59]. In Ta-B phase diagram, there are six boride phases, such as Ta2B, Ta3B2, TaB, Ta3B4, TaB2 and Ta5B6 [60]. Among these phases, only TaB and TaB2 are stable from room temperature (RT) up to their melting temperatures. The TaB2 phase, possessing a melting temperature of ~3200 °C, has a wide compositional range between 61 and 72 at.% B or 64 and 72 at.% B below the eutectic temperature of ~2055 °C [60]. 1. Introduction
Hafnium diboride (HfB2) is a refractory metal boride that exhibits excellent  thermal  and  mechanical  properties,  including  high  melting point  (Tm~3380 ◦ C),  high  hardness  (~28  GPa),  and  good  oxidation resistance  [1 -4].  These  properties  make  it  a  promising  material  for extreme environments, such as protective tiles on nose cones and leading  edges  of  re-entry  vehicles.  Despite  the  promising  combination  of properties,  monolith  HfB2  has  limited  applications  owing  to  its  poor sinterability.  The  high  melting  point  and  strong  covalent  bonding  of HfB2 pose  challenges  to  its  sintering  process,  impeding  complete densification  and  leading  to  significant  grain  growth  during  conventional sintering (T > 0.5Tm) [3].
To address these challenges, various sintering techniques, such as hot pressing  (HP)  [1,5 -7],  pressure-less  sintering  (PS)  [8],  reactive  hot pressing (RHP) [9], self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) [10], and spark plasma sintering (SPS) [11 -18], have been employed to enhance  the  densification  of  HfB2-based  composites.  Overall,  these studies demonstrate that SPS is an effective technique for consolidating HfB2 ceramics with high relative density (up to ~ 98 %) and homogeneous  microstructure  [3,4,15 -20]. 4. Discussion
La2O3 ( ∼ 2100 ◦ C) has a lower melting point than SiC ( ∼ 2800 ◦ C), ZrB2 ( ∼ 3200 ◦ C) and HfB2 ( ∼ 3400 ◦ C) which induces plastic deformation at lower temperatures. This has been observed in other ceramic systems such as Al2O3, which has a similar melting point ( ∼ 2100 ◦ C) to La2O3 and plastic deformation at 10 -3 s -1 in micron-sized Al2O3 could be obtained at 1400 ◦ C with stresses around 100 MPa. 51,52 Grain boundary chemistry is affected by thermal treatment which could be beneficial for the strength of UHTCs at high temperature. Hilmas and Fahrenholtz 53 reported an enhancement of up to 40% of the strength of HfB2-SiC ceramics at 1600 ◦ C using a thermal treatment in Ar of 10 h at 1550 ◦ C. This effect was attributed to a grain boundary chemistry change during the thermal treatment. Further investigation of this effect and strength data at higher temperatures is required to understand high temperature mechanical response of UHTCs. However, this field is challenging since some phenomena such as creep, have only been explored very recently, 12 and its effect on UHTCs properties at working temperature is unknown.

3.1. Phase analysis
The XRD pattern of HfB2 powder and SPS consolidated pellets from temperature 1750 -1950 ◦ C is shown in Fig. 1a . Notably, there is a slight peak shift of ~0.2 ◦ (as shown in Fig. 1b) towards left for H1800, H1850, H1900 and H1950, however, HfB2 phase is retained. This slight shift is attributed to presence of ~2 MPa tensile residual stresses (calculation provided  in  appendix  (equation  v  and  vi)  in  the  HfB ₂ phase  at  T ≥ 1800 ◦ C. During SPS cooling, differential contraction between the core and surface of HfB2 can induce tensile surface stresses, which is attributed to low thermal expansion (CTE~6.3 × 10 GLYPH<0> 6  1/ ◦ C) of HfB ₂ and rapid cooling  from  high  temperatures  [3].  Nevertheless,  the  XRD  pattern Fig.  1a  also  accentuates  the  presence  of  low-intensity  peaks  corresponding to the Hafnium Carbide (HfC) phase (~1 vol%), indicating its presence as an impurity in pristine powder as well as sintered pellets. The lattice parameters for hafnium diboride (HfB2) are determined to be a = b = 3.143 Å and c = 3.476 Å. In contrast, the lattice parameters for hafnium carbide (HfC) are, a = b = c = 4.637 Å. The presence of HfC was further confirmed by microstructural analysis using EDS as shown in Fig. 1c (the presence of HfC and HfB2  at all temperatures is shown in appendix, Fig. A3). The presence of HfC is ignored in studying the sintering kinetics in the present study.

question: What melting point values have been reported for ultra-high-temperature ceramics generally versus hafnium and zirconium diborides, carbides, and their oxides specifically?
answer: Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are generally defined as inorganic materials with melting temperatures at or above 3000 °C [11]. Specifically, hafnium diboride (HfB2) has a melting point of 3380 °C [57,59], while zirconium diboride (ZrB2) has a melting point of 3245 °C [4]. Hafnium carbide (HfC) has a melting point of approximately 3900 °C [8,9], and zirconium carbide (ZrC) has a melting point of 3205 °C [11]. The oxides of hafnium and zirconium, such as hafnia (HfO2) and zirconia (ZrO2), have melting points over 2500 °C [1].

1. Introduction
Advanced materials offering a temperature capability significantly exceeding 2000 °C in extreme, highly corrosive environments are required for a range of aerospace and other applications where oxidation and/or erosion resistance is needed at very high temperatures [1]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are typically based on the borides, carbides and nitrides of refractory transition metals, including hafnium, zirconium and tantalum amongst others [1-3], not only offer extremely high melting points of over 3000 °C, but their oxides also typically have melting points over 2500 °C. Both hafnium and zirconium diborides, HfB2 and ZrB2, in particular have been extensively studied as innovative thermal protection systems (TPS) [4-6] and sharp leading edge components [7-9] for aerospace vehicles. They have typically been investigated as monolithic components; however they exhibit both poor thermal shock and oxidation resistance [10].

1. Introduction
Ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are those with extremely high melting points that give them a potential advantage for use in thermal protection systems or refractory applications. In particular, zirconium and hafnium diborides and carbides have melting points in excess of 3000 ◦ C. 1 In addition, diborides have higher thermal conductivity than carbides which make them promising candidates in thermal protection systems for hypersonic applications. The combination of high temperature capability and high thermal conductivity is particularly beneficial. However, ZrB2 and HfB2 baseline UHTCs possess insufficient oxidation resistance for hypersonic flight environments, in which temperatures above 2000 ◦ C in highly oxidizing atmospheres are reached. Most research to date has focused on developing more oxidation-resistant UHTCs composites. 2-5 The best oxidation resistance has been found for UHTCs containing SiC, typically additions between 10 and

1. Introduction
Certain characteristics of ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) materials containing transition metal borides and carbides give them a potential advantage over more traditional materials for use in hypersonic applications. In particular, hafnium and zirconium diborides and carbides, as well as their oxides, hafnia and zirconia, have extremely high melting points, all in excess of 2500 ◦ C (4530 ◦ F). 1 While HfC and ZrC have higher melting points than HfB2 and ZrB2, the diborides have substantially higher thermal conductivities than the carbides. 2,3 This combination of high-temperature capability and high thermal conductivity makes HfB2 and ZrB2 particularly attractive for use in sharp wing leading edges (WLEs) and nose tips. 4 In practice, the high-temperature oxidation resistance of pure diboride materials is not sufficient for aerothermal flight environments. The best oxidation performance is found for monolithic materials hot-pressed from mixtures of ceramic powders containing a silica former as a minor component. 5,6 The most common compositions typically contain 10-30 vol.% SiC, 7-12 although other silica formers like MoSi2 and TaSi2 have also been investigated. 13-16
∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +1 650 859 2667; fax: +1 650 859 6196. E-mail address: jochen.marschall@sri.com (J. Marschall).
The design of high-performance hypersonic vehicles generally involves relatively sharp nose tips and WLEs. (An arcjet model of a concept UHTC WLE section is shown in Fig. 1.) While not an achievable design in practice, in the context of supersonic flow theory, a 'sharp' leading edge is one with zero radius of curvature. In a practical design context, 'sharp' usually refers to a leading-edge radius that is much smaller than the nose, wing, or vehicle length scale, while 'blunt' implies the converse.

Ceramics International
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are conventionally defined as inorganic materials with melting temperatures at or above 3000 ◦ C. Chemically, they are identified as carbides, borides, and nitrides of group IV and V transition metals [11 -13]. Among the various compositions,  the  diborides  of  zirconium  (ZrB2)  and  hafnium  (HfB2) retain desirable physical, mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties in addition to oxide phases that are thermally stable above 2500 ◦ C [38]. The  superior  melting  temperatures  for  Zr-  and  Hf-based  oxides  are depicted in Fig. 1. Based on these characteristics, UHTCs have garnered wide attention as enabling TPS for hypersonic air vehicles, especially for sharp leading-edge geometries used to reduce drag. During hypersonic flight, shockwaves that are generated on the leading-edge surface induce a corrosive plasma layer and significant aerothermal heating (~2000 ◦ C)  [39].  UHTCs  are  expected  to  address  the  thermal  and oxidation requirements for these conditions. However, exposure under simulated test environments has shown that the low toughness of these M. 1.1. General
Ultra-High Temperature Ceramic Matrix Composites (UHTCMCs) are a relatively new class of materials designed to withstand extreme thermal  environments,  particularly  in  aerospace,  nuclear,  and  miningrelated applications. They have been developed to overcome the limitations of traditional ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which, while resistant to high temperatures, are brittle and have low thermal shock resistance [1 -5].
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) generally consist of transition metal carbides, nitrides, and borides, with Group IV compounds (Ti, Zr, Hf) and TaC being the primary focus due to their exceptional melting points above 2000 ◦ C and stable oxides formed in situ at high temperatures  [1 -3].  These  materials  are  particularly  valued  in  environments where temperatures surpass 2000 ◦ C, intense heat fluxes, and highly  reactive  dissociated  gases  [6].  Zirconium  diboride  (ZrB2)  and hafnium diboride (HfB2) are commonly studied due to their superior oxidation  resistance  and  high  thermal  conductivity,  with  protective

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  have  gained  renewed attention  in  the  last  decades  due  to  their  potential  use  as  protective coatings on metal substrates in extreme environments [1 -5]. By definition, UHTCs are ceramic materials with melting points above 3000 ◦ C [6,7].  Therefore,  they  are  suitable  candidates  for  thermal  protection structures in gas-turbine engines or chemical plants, which can withstand  application  temperatures  exceeding  ~1600 ◦ C  and  aggressive environmental conditions [1,8 -11]. In addition to their high melting point,  UHTCs  combine  improved  structural  properties  such  as  high hardness,  excellent  chemical  stability,  high  Youngs  modulus,  high refractoriness, as well as high thermal and electrical conductivity [1, 8 -10,12]. UHTCs are typically binary compounds consisting of carbides, borides or nitrides of early transition metals (e.g. ZrB2, HfN, HfC or TaC) [7,8,12]. 1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are widely used in the field of thermal protection due to their exceptional melting point ( > 30 0 0 ° C), outstanding high-temperature performance, and excellent resistance to ablation [ 1-3 ]. UHTCs are primarily composed of transition metal carbides, borides, and nitrides [ 4 , 5 ]. Among these ceramics, carbides, and nitrides can be solutionized into carbon-nitride ceramics with superior properties due to the same NaCl face-centered cubic (FCC) structure [ 6 , 7 ].
Particularly, hafnium carbide (HfC), characterized by ultra-high temperature melting point ( ∼ 3900 ° C) [ 8 , 9 ], displays enhanced performance as nitrogen atoms is incorporated [ 10 , 11 ]. 1. Introduction
Generally,  Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  refer  to  ceramics with melting points above 3000 ◦ C, including carbides, nitrides and borides of transition metals such as Zr, Hf, and Ta [1 -3]. Known for their exceptionally high melting points, hardness, modulus and excellent high-temperature oxidation resistance, UHTCs have garnered significant attention in thermal protection applications [4]. Among them, HfC and TaC are extensively studied [5]. Ta and Hf, located in groups IVB and VB of  the  periodic  table,  have  similar  atomic  radii  due  to  lanthanide contraction, enabling unlimited mutual solubility to form Ta-Hf-C solid solutions [6]. Ta4HfC5  synthesized in a molar ratio of 4:1 of TaC:HfC exhibits  a  melting  point  approaching  4000 ◦ C  [7].  Due  to  the  high melting  point  and  superior  thermophysical  and  oxidation-resistant properties inherited from TaC and HfC, Ta4HfC5  holds promising applications in extreme high-temperature aerospace environments [8 -10].
To apply Ta4HfC5 in thermal protection materials, two fundamental

In Ta-B phase diagram, there are six boride phases, such as Ta2B, Ta3B2, TaB, Ta3B4, TaB2 and Ta5B6 [60]. Among these phases, only TaB and TaB2 are stable from room temperature (RT) up to their melting temperatures. The TaB2 phase, possessing a melting temperature of ~3200 °C, has a wide compositional range between 61 and 72 at.% B or 64 and 72 at.% B below the eutectic temperature of ~2055 °C [60]. 1. Introduction
Hafnium diboride (HfB2) is a refractory metal boride that exhibits excellent  thermal  and  mechanical  properties,  including  high  melting point  (Tm~3380 ◦ C),  high  hardness  (~28  GPa),  and  good  oxidation resistance  [1 -4].  These  properties  make  it  a  promising  material  for extreme environments, such as protective tiles on nose cones and leading  edges  of  re-entry  vehicles.  Despite  the  promising  combination  of properties,  monolith  HfB2  has  limited  applications  owing  to  its  poor sinterability.  The  high  melting  point  and  strong  covalent  bonding  of HfB2 pose  challenges  to  its  sintering  process,  impeding  complete densification  and  leading  to  significant  grain  growth  during  conventional sintering (T > 0.5Tm) [3].
To address these challenges, various sintering techniques, such as hot pressing  (HP)  [1,5 -7],  pressure-less  sintering  (PS)  [8],  reactive  hot pressing (RHP) [9], self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) [10], and spark plasma sintering (SPS) [11 -18], have been employed to enhance  the  densification  of  HfB2-based  composites.  Overall,  these studies demonstrate that SPS is an effective technique for consolidating HfB2 ceramics with high relative density (up to ~ 98 %) and homogeneous  microstructure  [3,4,15 -20]. 1. Introduction
Hafnium  diboride  (HfB2)  has  a  hexagonal  crystal  structure  (MB2 type, P6/mmm space group) consisting of B atoms layers in 2D graphitic rings and hexagonal close-packed Hf layer [1], which endows HfB2 with high melting point (3250 ◦ C), excellent chemical stability, high electrical and thermal conductivity, good thermal shock resistance and so on [2 -4].  Therefore,  HfB2 can  be  used  as  one  of  the  most  promising ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  applied  in  some  extreme  working conditions, such as in hypersonic vehicles, rocket thrusters, and other thermal protection components [5 -9]. However, strong covalent bond and low self-diffusion coefficient of HfB2 make the densifying HfB2 very difficult [10]. In addition, the oxides (such as B2O3, HfO2) on the surface of HfB2  powder further hinder its densification sintering [11 -13]. As a consequence, some additives such as SiC [14,15], B4C [16,17], WC [18, 19], MoSi2 [20 -22], etc.) through reacting with HfO2 to reduce the onset temperature of densification are employed to densify the HfB2 powder by traditional hot pressing [23]. However, the introduction of impurities from the surplus additives and the reaction product is bound to affect the property  of  the  material.  Besides,  in  order  to  achieve  the  densifying HfB2, the long dwell time (1 -2h) at high temperature (above 2100 ◦ C) inevitably results in the grain coarsening from initial powder grain size

1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is an ultra high temperature ceramic (UHTC) possessing high thermomechanical strength and chemical resistance. With a melting point of ∼ 3200 ◦ C it is one of the leading candidates for thermal protection systems in hypersonic flight and refractory materials such as crucibles for high temperature metals processing [1,2]. However, its densification is challenging even by the use of pressure and pulsed electric current/spark plasma sintering (SPS) methods.
Densification of ZrB2 powders can be improved by additives. However, the selection of the additive for refractory applications can be crucial to thermomechanical strength. Higher melting point metals such as Ir, W, Nb, and Mo are often selected in up to

5.2. ZrB2 ceramics
The sintering temperature range for densification of ZrB2 is influenced by melting point of the silicides used as the sinter aids [34,134,136,137]. The densification temperature (1600 °C) is lower for ZrSi2-reinforced ZrB2 when compared to other silicides since the melting point of ZrSi2 is relatively low. Sciti et al. [112,134,149] extensively studied the use of MoSi2 sinter-additive for ZrB2/ HfB2. Their work revealed that achievement of near-theoretical sinter-density was possible for ZrB2 with the use of MoSi2 (3-20 vol %) via PS at 1900 °C, HP at 1800 °C and SPS at 1750 °C. The densification was assisted by transient liquid phase that formed due to the reaction between MoSi2 and surface oxide impurities on ZrB2, as was shown by Eq. (30). During hot pressing, the plastic deformation of silicides at high temperatures (> 800 °C) would also contributes to the densification of ZrB2 as the deformation forces soft silicide particles to fill in the voids left by ZrB2 particles [112,134].

question: What volume-fraction ranges have been reported for sintering additives and silica-forming secondary phases in boride-based UHTC composites?
answer:
The volume-fraction ranges for sintering additives in boride-based UHTC composites have been reported as follows:

- For ZrSi2 as a sintering additive in ZrB2 composites, a volume fraction of 30 vol% has been used [1].
- For MoSi2 as a sintering additive in UHTC composites, a volume fraction of 15 vol% has been used [2].
- For SiC as a sintering additive in ZrB2 composites, volume fractions ranging from 10 to 20 vol% have been used [3].
- For SiC in ZrB2 composites, a volume fraction of 10 vol% has been used alongside 5 vol% of MeSi2 compounds [4][5].
- For Y2O3 as a sintering aid in ZrB2-SiC composites, a volume fraction of 5 vol% has been used [3].

Regarding silica-forming secondary phases, the following volume-fraction ranges have been reported:

- For SiC, which can form a silica phase upon oxidation, volume fractions ranging from 10 to 20 vol% have been used in ZrB2 composites [3].
- For SiC in ZrB2 composites, a volume fraction of 10 vol% has been used alongside 5 vol% of MeSi2 compounds [4][5].

The sources do not provide specific volume-fraction ranges for silica-forming secondary phases other than those mentioned above.

chunks.text:
4.1. Feasibility of EBS for UHTCs
Metal Boride UHTCs are unusual materials that possess ceramic-like properties such as high melting point, hardness, as well as metal-like properties such as high electrical and thermal conductivity. The UHTC material chosen for this research is ZrB2, which has an electrical and thermal conductivity of 1.08 × 10 7 S/m and 60 W/mK, respectively, and a melting temperature of 3245 ◦ C. With ZrSi2  as a sintering additive (melting point: 1625 ◦ C), it can be selectively melted, forming a liquid phase that binds the ZrB2  powder particles together, resulting in processing temperature around 1700 ◦ C that is easily attainable by the EBM machines.  Additionally,  ZrSi2  softens  around  800 ◦ C,  hence  a  liquid phase sintering process would most likely occur at temperatures less than 1700 ◦ C by an intergranular viscous ZrSi2 phase [11].
In the current study, a representative ZrB2 UHTC was mixed with 30 vol% of ZrSi2, which acts as a densification aid. It was determined by S. Guo  et  al.  [10]  that  in  the  traditional  UHTC  sintering  process,  the addition of ZrSi2  in the 30% volume fraction is critical in maintaining good  electrical  (11.9 × 10 6 S/m)  and  thermal  conductivities  (86.81 W/mK)  without  compromising  the  mechanical  behavior.  The  high electrical conductivity of the UHTC materials makes them compatible with the EBM process. The earlier work in our group [2] was the first of its  kind  to  demonstrate  the  feasibility  of  EBM  to  process  the  UHTC mixture  and  pioneer  EBS.  Although  only  1D  melt  tracks  have  been performed, interesting consolidation mechanisms have been reported. The current study will contribute more to the findings through experimentation and subsequent characterization of single-layer builds of the ZrB2 -30 vol% ZrSi2 powder feedstock.

Effect of Transition Metal Silicides
The extremely high melting point and the low self-diffusion coefficient make these ceramics very difficult to sinter to full density: temperatures above 2000°C and the application of pressure are necessary conditions. However these processing parameters lead to coarse microstructures, with mean grain size of the order of 20 μm and trapped porosity, all features which prevent the achievement of the full potential of the thermo-mechanical properties of UHTCs.
Several  activities  have  been  performed  in  order  to  decrease  the  severity  of  the processing conditions of UHTCs introducing sintering additives, such as metals, nitrides, carbides or silicides. In general the addition of such secondary phases does decrease the sintering temperature, but some additives have some drawbacks, especially during use at high temperature, owing to their softening and the following loss of integrity of the material.
In this chapter, composites based on borides and carbides of Zr, Hf and Ta were produced with addition of MoSi 2 or TaSi 2 . These silicides were selected as sintering aids owing to their high melting point (>2100°C), their ductility above 1000°C and their capability to increase the oxidation resistance.
The microstructure of fully dense hot pressed UHTCs containing 15 vol% of MoSi 2 or TaSi 2 ,  was  characterized by x-ray diffraction, scanning, and transmission electron microscopy.
Based on microstructural features detected by TEM, thermodynamical calculations, and the available phase diagrams, a densification mechanism for these composites is proposed.
The mechanical properties, namely hardness, fracture toughness, Young's modulus and flexural strength at room and high temperature, were measured and compared to the properties of other ultra-high temperature ceramics produced with other sintering additives. Further, the microstructural findings were used to furnish possible explanations for the excellent high temperature performances of these composites.

A B S T R A C T
strength and good thermal conductivity, the most studied UHTCs are the ZrB2-based composites [17 -19]. In order to improve the thermo-mechanical properties, oxidation resistance and final density, borides are usually combined with silicon-containing additives in small amount,  such  as  10 -20  vol%  of  SiC  [20,21].  In  fact,  the  oxidation resistance of pure ZrB2  is poor already at 1000 ◦ C, because of the formation of a porous and non-protective ZrO2 film, as well as the evaporation  of  B2O3.  The  introduction of  SiC  promotes  the  formation  of  a surficial glassy silica phase, which protects the material from oxidation up to 1600 ◦ C [22]. Other ceramic additives, such as Y2O3  or MoSi2 improve the densification of ZrB2-SiC composites by forming a liquid phase with the oxide impurities present on the surface of ZrB2 and SiC grains [23,24]. The presence of Y2O3 stabilizes the ZrO2 formed during oxidation in the tetragonal structure, leading to the formation of a more compact film that improves the oxidation resistance [25]. The presence of MoSi2 improves the oxidation resistance thanks to the formation of a SiO2 layer. In more detail, oxidation produces a SiO2-rich glass layer, a subsurface oxide layer, and a MoB, ZrO2, and SiO2 mixture layer [26].
In this work, the possibility of producing graded composites with a fibre gradient ranging from 0 to 50 vol% via hot pressing sintering was investigated. Three UHTC matrices were mixed with carbon fibres:
- ZrB2 -10 vol% SiC - 5 vol% Y2O3 where SiC is the oxidation resistant phase and Y2O3 is the sintering aid [4,27].
- ZrB2 -15 vol% MoSi2 where MoSi2 is both sintering and oxidation resistant aid [28,29].

A B S T R A C T
In this work, ZrB2 composites were produced containing high volumes, 40 vol%, of short carbon fiber plus 10 vol% of oxidation retardant phases. The latter consisted of 5 vol% of SiC combined with 5 vol% of one of three different MeSi2 compounds, viz. MoSi2, HfSi2 or WSi2. The selection of these silicides was based on their compatibility with high-temperature environments, i.e. they offer melting points above 1600 °C, high stiffness and, owing to their beneficial effects on densification, high temperature strength and potential oxidation resistance improvement for borides [23,25-28]. Additionally, a comparative material without MeSi2 but containing the same total amount of SH additives, i.e. 10 vol% SiC, was also prepared as a reference baseline. The question posed by this work was whether the replacement of 5 vol% of SiC with 5 vol% of MeSi2 would significantly improve the ablation resistance. Thus the UHTCMCs were exposed to the flame of an oxyacetylene torch (OAT) at temperatures above 2000 °C for 60 s. The analysis of the as-sintered and oxidized materials is presented and discussed, with the focus being on the effect of the secondary phases in promoting the formation of an ablation resistant microstructure.
The novelty of this work lies in the production and successful testing of a new class of UHTCMCs that can survive extreme conditions of oxidation at up to 2000 °C.

(B) are stacked in alternate sequence (ZrBZrBZrB). The strong covalent bonding between B-B and Zr-B atoms results in high melting temperature, hardness and strength. The high electrical and thermal conductivity are due to the close packed Zr layers that show a metallic-like bonding.
Densification of UHTCs is extremely difficult due to its high melting point, strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion rates (Sonber & Suri, 2011). In the case of borides, oxide layers of B 2 O3 and Zr (Hf-) O 2 covering the surface of boride  particles  are  subjected  to  evaporation-condensation  mechanisms  at  low temperature resulting in mass transfer without densification. At higher temperature grain coarsening becomes predominant over densification and finally causes poor mechanical properties. Temperature above 1800°C and high external pressure are needed to achieve densification in monolithic borides thanks to the activation of grain boundary and volume diffusion mechanisms. Alternatively, densification of borides can be performed at lower temperatures and pressure through either addition of sintering agents or decrease of powder mean grain size and/or increase of the defect concentration through mechanical activation (high energy milling).
Sintering additives may be ceramics or metals. They can either form a liquid phase at the sintering temperature (liquid phase sintering) or may react with and remove oxidized surface film from boride powder particles inducing a solid state sintering densification.

1. Introduction
All the so-called ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs; i.e., borides, carbides, and nitrides of the early-transition metals [1]) have poor intrinsic sinterability due to the serious kinetic restrictions imposed by their strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coef /uniFB01 cients. This is further aggravated extrinsically because the starting powders normally comprise large particles of various microns in size and with surface oxide impurities. Not surprisingly, dedicated sintering studies constitute a large part of the research effort carried out on UHTCs, and particularly on ZrB2-based UHTCs [2,3]. They have enabled the identi /uniFB01 cation of appropriate sintering additives and comminution practices. Some of these suitable additives are carbon [4,5], B4C [6], WC [7,8], and B4C þ C [9,10], which favor the conventional solid-state sintering of

This has been proven both for pressureless sintering (PS) and hot-pressing (HP), both of which apply prolonged sintering cycles (i.e., various hours). Apart from facilitating the conventional sintering, cleaning oxides off of the grain boundaries also results in improved high-temperature strength [5,8]. Furthermore, solid-state sintering additives are normally used synergistically with the wet-attrition milling of the starting powders because solid-state sinterability scales inversely with a power of the particle size (i.e., Herring's scaling law [11]).

The former composite yielded a fracture strength value of 909 MPa while as the later one showed 386 MPa [156]. Thus, it can be concluded that the limitation of lower sinterability in di-borides can be overcome by judicial selection of sintering route, and size and type of reinforcement.
In many applications of UHTCs, especially in the context of their use in a space vehicle, it is mandatory to observe the role of high temperature and surrounding atmosphere during re-entry. Thus, the oxidation resistance becomes a key property to be evaluated by simulating the reentry conditions, which is discussed in section 4.2.3.

5. Densification, microstructure and mechanical properties
As discussed in the previous section, various sintering techniques (both conventional and advanced) have been used to densify transition metal di-boride based materials, with the monolithic di-borides usually necessitating very high sintering temperatures. The reduction of starting powders particle size and the use of sinter-additives are possibly the major approaches that are used to address this issue (see Figs. 7 and 9). Both metallic and non-metallic sinter-additives have been used to obtain fully dense di-boride ceramics at low sintering temperatures. While selecting the sinter-additive, the type and amount must be judiciously chosen as they influence densification, microstructure, phase assemblage and properties of the di-borides. Metallic additives (generally < 2 wt% added to borides) such as Ni, Cu, Co, Cr and Fe etc. are advantageous towards obtaining near-theoretical sinter-density, reducing the sintering temperature considerably due to liquid phase sintering and very effective in improving room temperature mechanical properties of borides [5,9,10,50,204]. However, discussion on the use of metallic sinter-additives is not included here since the addition of metallic additives degrades the high temperature properties of the di-boride ceramics, eventually rendering them unsuitable for the ultra-high temperature applications. For example, the strength of 4 wt% Ni added ZrB2 increased from 370 to 630 MPa with increasing temperature (up to 800 °C) and further increasing the temperature resulted drastic reduction in strength (240 MPa at 1000 °C and 20 MPa at 1200 °C) [10]. Hence, the effects of non-metallic sinter-additives on the densification and microstructure of the important di-boride ceramics have been discussed in the following sub-sections.

In this work, the influence of three MAX phases (Ti 3 SiC 2, Ti 2 AlC, and Cr 2 AlC) on the densification and final properties of dense composites from Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics (UHTC) family was studied. Addition of the MAX phases resulted in the formation of secondary boride phases due to chemical reactions between boron carbide and MAX phase. Mentioned phases were utilized for reduction of sintering temperature of the final composite material. It decreased the sintering temperature up to 800 °C when compared to pure B 4 C. Additionally, the phase composition and derivative mechanical properties were investigated to evaluate differences between final composite materials. All obtained materials remarkably increased their fracture resistance (K IC ) from 33 to 100%. The mechanical properties of B 4 C were either retained (Ti 3 SiC 2 ) or decreased in terms of Vickers hardness and Young's Modulus (Ti 2 AlC and Cr 2 AlC). Systems with Ti 3 SiC 2 appeared to possess significant potential for application, also when compared to UHTC systems of similar purpose.

12.4  SUMMARY
Tantalum carbides comprise a class of UHTC with a range of melting temperatures and phases dependent on the C/Ta ratio. The phase structures and precipitation sequence of these  materials  are  paramount  in  regulating  the  microstructure  morphologies. Microstructures can range from equiaxed grains to equiaxed grains with secondary laths in a crisscross pattern to acicular grains with secondary laths all parallel to the major axis of the grain. These microstructures have been shown to regulate the mechanical responses, particularly increasing the fracture toughness to approximately 13 MPa√m with a high volume fraction of Ta 4 C 3 . Most of the mechanical property work has been done on the monocarbide TaC phase. The mechanical behavior of this carbide shows a range of responses, dependent upon carbon content, porosity of the test specimen, and the testing means or environment. Because of these issues, reported mechanical data show variations. For example, agreement can be found in certain areas, such as decreasing hardness with decreasing carbon content below C/Ta = 0.8, whereas other tests show wide variations, including the reported activation energies in creep experiments. This demonstrates the challenges in studying these classes of materials. It is imperative that any conclusions based on literature reports carefully consider the intrinsic and extrinsic characteristics  of  the  tantalum  carbide  sample  tested.  Regardless  of  these  issues,  the range of mechanical properties with corresponding varied microstructures offers several exciting opportunities to tailor phases together that couple ultra-high melting temperature capability with metallic-like and ceramic-like mechanical responses.

Production of UHTC Complex Shapes and Architectures
The samples' shrinkage after PLS was about 13% and the total porosity was about 63-64 vol % (absolute density 2.21-2.26 g cm -1 ). Concerning the porosity features, hierarchical architectures with anisotropic porosity have been realized, which showed porosity formed by mainly unidirectional channels separated by ceramic plates. Figures 7a and b show the surface of the sample top (that is perpendicular to the main direction of cold advancement front and ice growth and thus of the channel-like pores). The surface is divided in domains, within a single domain the ceramic lamellae are each parallel to others, while adjacent domains show different orientation of the ceramic plates. The ceramic lamellae can form ceramic bridges connecting adjacent ceramic plates. The pore size is thus multidimensional, from micrometers to millimeters. The pores affecting the ceramic lamellae and bridges and the smallest dimension of the channel-like pore, corresponding to the distance of two adjacent ceramic lamella, belong to micrometric scale. While the two main dimensions (width and length, respectively in the plane of or perpendicular to the top surface) of channel-like pores usually fall in the millimetric range.
Preliminary evaluation of the compressive strength shows improved mechanical performance in the load-bearing in the direction of the ceramic lamellae compared to sponge-templated foams: values up to 37 MPa have been detected for ZrB 2 freeze casted samples while 4.8 MPa was reported for samples with 70 vol% of porosity obtained by impregnation of cellulosic sponge (Medri et al, 2011b). The result is even more important considering the absence of any sintering aid in the freeze casted samples while ZrB 2 sponge-templated foams have been produced adding SiC.

In  conventional  precipitationstrengthened  systems,  Orowan  looping  plays  a  critical  role  in  strain hardening,  particularly  when  dislocations  bypass  small,  uniformly distributed secondary phases [82]. A B S T R A C T
In the recent years, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) based on transition metal carbides (TaC, ZrC, TiC) and diborides (ZrB2, TiB2) have emerged as promising materials for re-entry applications. A boride -carbide dualphase composite was designed in order to achieve good mechanical properties with a fine microstructure and strong interfacial bonding. Three compositions: (i) (Zr,Ti)B2, (ii) (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, and (iii) dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 were synthesized using spark plasma sintering at 1850 ◦ C, 40 MPa for 30 min, achieving ~99 % densification. Phase analysis confirmed single-phase solid solutions in (Zr,Ti)B2  and (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, while the dual-phase (Zr,Ti, Ta)C,B2  composite comprised ~42 % boride and ~58 % carbide. Nanoindentation analysis (8 mN) showed a uniform hardness (~36 GPa) for all the compositions, whereas microindentation revealed intermediate hardness of ~27 GPa for the dual phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2, as compared to that of ~29 GPa for (Zr,Ti,Ta)C and ~24 GPa for (Zr,Ti)B2

Processing Methods for Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Despite the  low-intrinsic  sinterability,  which  is  attributed  to  highly  covalent bonds and low volume and grain boundary diffusion rates, significant advances in pressureless sintering of UHTCs have been made. Various additives have been incorporated to improve the densification of ZrB 2 and HfB 2 . Metals, carbon, carbides and silicides have been used to enhance the densification. Addition of carbon and carbides (B 4 C, SiC, TiC, WC) assists in densification of borides to a density of more than 95% ρ th at temperature around 2000-2100 o C. These additives react with surface
Figure 1. Induction furnace
oxide layer and thus enhance the surface energy thereby improving densification. Metals (Fe, Ni) and silicides (MoSi 2 , ZrSi 2 ) improve the densification by forming liquid  phases  during  sintering.  The  additives  should  not  have  very  high  vapour pressure otherwise it leads to porosity in the final product.
Pressureless sintering may be useful for fabrication of shapes where high density is not required. Some thermal protection systems may not require very highmechanical strengths and density. The material porosity may even provide some benefit by lowering the stiffness and the thermal conductivity of the part.

question: What elastic (Young's) modulus values have been reported across these UHTC studies, for materials ranging from a Cf/C-UHTC composite to the ZrB2 phase and carbon-fiber reinforcement used in UHTC composites?
answer:
Elastic (Young's) modulus values reported across the UHTC studies include:

- For ZrB2, the elastic modulus has been reported to range from approximately 350 to 530 GPa depending on porosity and additives [11]. A specific value of 500 GPa was mentioned for ZrB2 ceramic [16].
- For the ZrB2-SiC composite, the Young's modulus was calculated to be around 220 GPa for the UD-0/0°-AR composite [17].
- For the Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites, the Young's modulus ranged from approximately 153.1 GPa for the UD-0/0° composite to as low as 100% less for the 8H-SW composite [4].
- For the Cf/SiC composites, the Young's modulus of the matrix was reported as 110 GPa [15].
- For the UHTC phase, the Young's modulus was reported as 540 GPa [15].
- For the HEB (High Entropy Boride) phase, the Young's modulus was measured as 527 GPa for a high relative density specimen [13].
- For the HEC (High Entropy Carbide) phase, the Young's modulus was reported as 462.4 ± 4.0 GPa [13].
- For the ZrB2-20%ZrC composite, the Young's modulus was measured as 505 GPa at 25°C and decreased with increasing temperature [19].

These values reflect the wide range of elastic moduli observed in different UHTC materials and composites.

Investigations of continuous carbon reinforcements for UHTC-based materials first gained traction in the early 2000s. A study by A. Sayir [184] attempted to use chemical vapor technology to deposit HfC and TaC onto a 2D carbon fabric weave. Although the carbide was incorporated more like a coating than an infiltrated matrix, the investigation represented  an  early  success  at  demonstrating  the  compatibility  between a UHTC material and continuous carbon reinforcement. The authors  of  the  study  alluded  to  the  expected  structural  benefits  of employing a 3D architecture over the 2D fabric that was used at the time. Subsequent studies [185 -192] looked at introducing UHTC phases into composite architectures, although only in small amounts. About 4 vol% of ZrB2 particles were infiltrated into a carbon fiber preform, followed by CVI processes that introduced PyC and SiC as the matrix chemistries.

4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
Beyond the traditional UHTC -SiC systems, a number of unconventional  materials  systems  have  been  looked  at  to  enhance  the  matrix phase in continuous fiber UHTCMCs. Yaghobizadeh et al. [202] incorporated the MAX phase Ti3SiC2 into a Cf/ZrC -SiC, as MAX materials can exhibit some of the same properties as metals, including ductility and thermal shock resistance. The fracture toughness of the composite was tested to be 16.9 ± 0.6 MPa m ½ , which is among the highest metrics reported in literature for UHTCMC materials. Additionally, Ti3SiC2 also has a reported melting temperature higher than 3000 ◦ C, making it a candidate for ultra-high temperature applications with further testing and validation of good oxidation behavior. Another novel matrix phase inclusion are the rare earth oxides, which were studied in two papers [203,204]  by  Vinci  et  al.  A  series  of  Cf/ZrB2 -SiC  composites  were manufactured with 5 vol% of different rare earth additives (Y2O3, Sc2O3, La2O3,  Ce2O3).  Aside  from  the  composite  containing  Ce2O3 -which degraded  in  ambient  atmosphere -all  the  composites  showed  improvements  in  high  temperature  flexural  strength  ( > 600  MPa)  and fracture toughness ( > 10 MPa m ½ ) compared to the baseline Cf/ZrB2 -SiC without rare earth additives.  This  was attributed  to  ternary  lamellar compounds forming for La2O3 and Y2O3 composites, and solid solution M. Ammendola et al.

4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
One study by Hu et al. [187] commented on the mechanical properties as a function of ZrB2 content in the carbon-reinforced SiC matrix, indicating that the composite ' s flexural strength and fracture toughness decreased with increasing amounts of ZrB2. According to the report, the respective values of strength and toughness dropped from 367 MPa to 23.6 MPa m ½ without any boride to 163 MPa and 8.1 MPa m ½ when the matrix contained 60 vol% ZrB2. Their reasoning was that the boride phase  tended  to  react  at  the  fiber-matrix  interface,  causing  the  mechanical properties of the composite to degrade. Similar trends were observed in later studies involving carbide UHTCs. Chen et al. [191] investigated the effects of adding TaC to a Cf/SiC composite, while Yan et  al.  did  the  same  for  ZrC  [193]  and  HfC  [194].  In  all  three  cases, flexural strength decreased with increasing UHTC volume fraction, and the fracture toughness of the Cf/SiC -UHTC composites was lower than the Cf/SiC baseline. These results indicated a need for process refinement, given the preference for higher volume fractions of the UHTC phase due to superior oxidation resistance.

3.2.1 Elastic modulus and flexural strength
The elastic modulus and flexural strength of the Cf -ZrB 2 -SiC composites prepared with different fiber architectures are reported in Table 2. Among the prepared composites, the UD-0/0 ◦ composite has exhibited the highest elastic modulus value ( ∼ 153.1 GPa), when measured along the fiber orientation. In contrast, the elastic moduli of the UD-0/90 ◦ and 8H-SW composites are found to be less compared to the UD-0/0 ◦ by ∼ 41.6% and ∼ 100%, respectively. In other words, the 8H-SW composite has exhibited the lowest elastic modulus among the fiber architectures investigated in this study. These variations in the experimentally determined values of Young's modulus may be explained by considering that the fibers oriented only along the loading direction of the test (longitudinal direction) contribute to the measured elastic constants. The anisotropy of C f is due to the elastic modulus being very high along the fiber axis, but low in the transverse direction. 1 Such anisotropy of elastic modulus is found in the C-fibers due to the strong covalent C-C bonds in the graphite structure being aligned along the fiber axis, and the presence of relatively weak interatomic (van der Waals) forces between the basal planes along the transverse direction. 55 In both UD-0/90 ◦ and 8H-SW composites, a significant fraction of the fibers have transverse orientation with respect to the test direction, and therefore has not contributed to the estimated value of Young's modulus. It should be noted that 50% of the total number of C f in the UD-0/90 ◦ fabric has a transverse orientation (i.e., five out of 10 laminae possess 90 ◦ orientation) with respect to the loading axis, while each layer of the C f fabric in the 8H-SW preform consists of both 0 ◦ and 90 ◦ -oriented fibers forming a mini-composite lamina.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) are a class of materials that are still in relatively early stages of research and development. These composites are made of an ultra-refractory ceramic matrix reinforced with fibers [1 -3]. Those based on ZrB2 and containing carbon fibers (Cf) have been rather intensively studied in the last years, with research efforts spanning basic science works up to qualification in relevant environments. These materials show indeed good promise as near-zero ablation components, demonstrating increased failure tolerance and refractory qualities [4 -7].
The  introduction  of  up  to  60  vol%  fiber  reinforcement  not  only provides increased failure tolerance and thermal shock resistance, but it also results in a notable weight reduction, and lower machining costs and time. However, the sensitivity of the fibers ' to the sintering environment, in terms of temperature and local chemistry [8,9], necessitates a maximum consolidation temperature of 1900 ◦ C under applied mechanical  pressure  in  order  to  obtain  UHTCMCs  with  the  desired

3.3 | Nanomechanical behavior of TaCHfC joint
To obtain further insight into the mechanical behavior of the joined UHTCs, the distribution of elastic modulus at the interface was investigated. Figure 5C shows the distribution of storage modulus in TaC-  HfC joint ceramic, obtained from the scanned area of 5 × 5 μm 2 .  For acquiring an elastic response such as modulus, the imposed load was maintained at a low value of 1.0 μN. For such studies, the surface needs to be extremely flat, which was not possible in the present case of UHTCs (see Figures 5B and 3). The modulus map distribution elicits microstructural heterogeneity, that is, sensitivity  to  the  topography,  shape,  pores,  and  composition. However, similar to the microstructure (Figure 3), modulus distribution  clearly  distinguishes  the  interface  (see  Figure 5C;  i.e.,  higher  modulus  on  the  TaC  side  indicated  by  red color and lower modulus values towards HfC side, marked by purple color). This distribution of the modulus around the interface  separating  it  from  two  UHTC parts also supports that the solid-  state diffusion has taken place to form a joint. It is essential to mention here that the absolute modulus measured at  the  rough  locations  is  extremely  low  compared  to that  of  the  actual  values  for  UHTCs.  This  skews  the  color gradient in the map, making it challenging to visualize the distribution. Hence, the color map's quantitative gradient is normalized to a quantitative one according to the following protocol. At each interface, the highest and lowest measured elastic moduli are designated 1 and 0, respectively. The distribution of moduli between these extremes is divided into four equal quarters, as presented in Figure 5C.

5.3. Mechanical properties
The primary mechanical properties required for analyses of isotropic UHTC components are the tensile modulus and the Poisson's ratio. Both appear as first-order terms in the governing static equations. For isotropic materials, the shear modulus, G , can be calculated from the tensile modulus and Poisson's ratio as G = E /[2(1 + ν ) and does not have to be independently specified.
Thetensile modulus (also called the Young's modulus or elastic modulus) has a large effect on the mechanical response of the component. Ideally, tensile modulus values are derived from the slope (or first derivative) of a material's stress-strain curve under uniaxial tension in the elastic loading regime. However UHTC materials have very limited ductility, and their tensile moduli are almost always either measured by acoustic methods or derived from experimental stress-strain curves obtained during flexure testing. The flexure method was found to produce somewhat lower (by ∼ 15%) values for some UHTC materials than the tensile and acoustic methods. 3,56
Tensile modulus values can be used directly in mechanical analyses, but it is often more accurate to incorporate the actual stress-strain relationship. Most analytical software packages can handle inputs of either type. Incorporating the experimental stress-strain curve is particularly advantageous for materials that exhibit non-linear elastic response or for when the experimental stress-strain curve contains plastic strain contributions deemed important for a particular application. Non-linear, elastic deformation behaviors are observed for some UHTC materials at elevated temperatures. 3,40-42,57 The tensile modulus magnitude tends to decrease with increasing temperature. Accurate temperature-dependent modulus data are desirable, but difficult to measure. In practice, if the expected thermal and load environments combine to push a UHTC component into a highly non-linear or highly temperature-dependent mechanical property regime, a redesign of the component or system is likely.

Abstract
Ultrahigh temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have melting points above 3000 ◦ C and outstanding strength at high temperatures, thus making them apposite structural materials for high-temperature applications. Di-borides, nitride, and carbide compounds-processed via various techniques-have been extensively studied and used in the manufacture of UHTCs. Current analytical models, based on our current but incomplete understanding of the theory, are unable to produce a priori predictions of mechanical properties of UHTCs based on their mixture designs and processing parameters. As a result, researchers have to rely on experiments-which are often costly and time-consuming-to understand composition-structure-performance links in UHTCs. This study employs machine learning (ML) models (i.e., random forest and artificial neural network models) to predict Young's modulus, flexural strength, and fracture toughness of UHTCs in relation to a wide range of mixture designs, processing parameters, and testing conditions. Outcomes demonstrate that adequately trained ML models can yield reliable predictions, a priori, of the three aforesaid mechanical properties. The prediction performance on Young's modulus is superior to flexural strength and fracture toughness. Next, the ML model with the best prediction performance is utilized to evaluate and rank the impacts of input variables on Young's modulus. Finally, on the basis of such classification of consequential and inconsequential input variables, this study develops an easy-to-use, closedform analytical model to predict Young's modulus of UHTCs. Overall, this study highlights the ability of data-driven numerical models to complement, or even replace, time-consuming experiments, thereby accelerating the development of UHTCs.

8.2.1  ZrB 2
Elastic modulus values reported for selected ZrB 2 ceramics with and without sintering additives are summarized in Table 8.1. Elastic modulus ranges from approximately 350 to approximately 530 GPa depending on porosity and additives (Fig. 8.1). Fitting the data to the Einstein (linear) [42], Spriggs [43], and Nielson [40, 41] models (Table 8.2) gives a value for fully dense ZrB 2 of  511  GPa compared to measured values of 490500 GPa in previous studies [38, 44]. However, the materials in the historic studies were not fully dense, and fitting the historic data to similar models predicts an elastic modulus of 519 GPa for dense ZrB 2 . Zhang et al . [45] calculated the elastic modulus of ZrB 2 from first principles to be 520  GPa, in good agreement with the fitted data. Recent work by Okamoto et al .  calculated the polycrystalline elastic modulus of ZrB 2 from single crystal measurements to be 525 GPa [46]. The modulus of 99.8%-dense ZrB 2 was measured to be 489 GPa [26]. However, additions of AlN, Si 3 N 4 ,  B 4 C, and C, as well as impurities picked up during powder milling (i.e., WC, ZrO 2 ), affect the elastic modulus of ZrB 2 ceramics. Small additions of B 4 C and/or C increase the elastic modulus of ZrB 2 [20, 22, 29]. Additions of AlN and Si 3 N 4 lower the elastic modulus [11, 32]. Additions affect the elastic modulus primarily through interactions with impurities on the surface of  the  starting  powders. Additives  such  as  C  and  B 4 C remove low modulus surface ceramics

8.3.1  Elastic Modulus of ZrB 2 -Based Ceramics
The elevated-temperature elastic modulus of hot-pressed ZrB 2 with and without additives is shown in Figure 8.10. Only limited data on elastic behavior of ZrB 2 have been published  for  elevated  temperatures.  Okamoto et  al .  [49]  reported  elastic  modulus  from single  crystal  values  as  being  525  GPa  at  room  temperature,  decreasing  linearly  to 490 GPa at 1100°C. Experimental results of bulk ZrB 2 have resulted in lower values of elastic modulus than those reported by Okamoto. The historical work of Rhodes et al . [25] provided the only reported values for the elastic modulus of bulk ZrB 2 tested in inert atmosphere. The modern studies by Zhu [20] and Neuman et al . [29] were conducted in air,  and  thus  are  affected  by  the  formation  of  an  oxide  scale  on  the  test  articles. Unfortunately, the effect of the oxide scale on the measured elastic modulus was not investigated. Regardless, the elastic modulus of bulk ZrB 2 decreased more rapidly than expected  based  on  single  crystal  measurements  presumably  due  to  a  grain  boundary effect. The modulus decreased linearly from room temperature to approximately 1200°C (450 GPa). Above  this  temperature,  the  elastic  modulus  decreased  more  rapidly  with temperature. The change in slope was thought to be a result of softening of grain boundary phases  combined with the  activation  of  grain  boundary  sliding  and  diffusional  creep mechanisms [20, 25, 29, 69, 90]. 4.6. Moduli
A comparison of selected moduli of the HEB and HEC phases with the reported values show our measurements are consistent with literature. Gu et al. [63] used a mechanical resonance frequency technique to determine Young's moduli, which is similar to our method. They have measured the Young's modulus of their high relative density (97.9 %) HEB specimen (with the same nominal composition as our HEB) to be 527 GPa, which is within the uncertainty of our measurement (524.6 ± 6.9 GPa). At the same time, they also ascribed the low Young's modulus (500 GPa) to more porosity in their low relative density (94.4 %) HEB specimen. For HEC, on the other hand, the Young's modulus measured in this study (462.4 ± 4.0 GPa) is close to those reported by Yan et al. (479 GPa) [23] and Harrington et al. (443 ± 40 GPa) [22], although both previous studies applied nanoindentation in the measurement. Ye et al. [37] reported the Young's modulus of HEC to be 514-522 ± 10 GPa, where the disparity might be attributed to the small indent loading force (8 mN) in their nanoindentation measurement [22]. Shear moduli for single-phase HEB and HEC UHTCs are rather limited in previous studies. Sarker et al. [24] reported the shear modulus of HEC as 188 GPa, which is consistent with our measurement of 193.0 ± 3.6 GPa in this study. In summary, our measured moduli are consistent with those reported in literature for single-phase high-entropy UHTCs, whenever there are reported data, albeit with expected differences due to porosity and loading.
For the four DPHE-UHTCs (8B2C, 6B4C, 4B6C, and 2B8C), both their Young's and shear moduli follow a largely linear interpolation with their single-phase endmembers (HEB and HEC); this trend is also consistent with that expected from a composite rule.

(139 ± 3 MPa), respectively with the difference being as much as ~34 %; and (ii) in spite of the flexural strength (175 ± 32 MPa) being higher for the UD-0/0 ◦ -HT composite in comparison to that of UD-0/0 ◦ -US, the standard deviation is more than 10 times greater in case of the former composite. As a result, the lowest value within the range of flexural strengths observed for the UD-0/0 ◦ -HT composite is comparable to that of the UD-0/0 ◦ -US, whereas the highest value is comparable to that obtained in case of the composites prepared with as-received Cf-fabrics. A typically higher scatter in the data observed for the UD-0/0 ◦ -HT composite could probably be due to the greater heterogeneity of ZrB2 -SiC matrix distribution within the intra-fibre bundle of the Cf fabric compared to that in the UD-0/0 ◦ -US composite.
Higher values of flexural strength observed for the as-received Cf composite is attributed to its minimal degradation, as a substantial portion of it has remained un-infiltrated by the ZrB2 -SiC matrix, which in turn may have helped in minimizing the formation of ZrC through the Reaction (R1). The absence of ZrB2 -SiC matrix in the intra-bundle region may be considered responsible for restricting the Cf degradation in the UD-0/0 ◦ -AR composite to a minimum level. The combined effects of fibre disorientation (as shown in Fig. 4) and Cf damage caused by the ultrasonication along with the formation of ZrC through the Reaction (R1) may be considered as the most acceptable explanation for the lower flexural strength in the UD-0/0 ◦ -US composite. On the other hand, higher flexural strength recorded for the UD-0/0 ◦ -HT composite is suggestive of less fibre disorientation caused through heat treatment than by the ultrasoincation of Cf-fabric.

question: What activation energy values were determined for the densification and sintering kinetics of UHTC ceramics?
answer:
The activation energy values determined for the densification and sintering kinetics of UHTC ceramics are as follows:

- For the intermediate stage sintering of HfB₂, an activation energy (QD) of ~888 kJ/mol was calculated [7].
- For the grain growth during the final stage of sintering of HfB₂, an activation energy (Qg) of ~475 kJ/mol was determined [7].
- For the HEB phase grain growth, an activation energy of 317 kJ/mol was estimated [11].
- For the densification of ZrB₂, an activation energy of 2070 kJ/mol was found [9].
- For the sintering of ZrB₂, an activation energy of 241 ± 62 kJ/mol was reported for lattice diffusion [11].
- For the sintering of (TiZrHfVNbTa)C, an activation energy of 839 ± 53 kJ/mol was associated with lattice diffusion [11].
- For the sintering of TiC, an activation energy of 390 kJ/mol was linked to grain boundary diffusion [11].
- For the oxidation of HfC-TiC, an activation energy of 385.39 kJ/mol was reported [4].
- For the densification of Ta x Hf 1-x C (0 < x < 1) ceramics, the diffusion activation energy of HfC is higher at 60.42 kJ/mol [12].

These values reflect the different mechanisms involved in the densification and sintering processes of UHTC ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
This study investigates the sintering kinetics during the intermediate and final stages of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of HfB2  over a temperature range of 1750 -1950 ◦ C under uniaxial pressure of 50 MPa. During the intermediate stage (T < 1850 ◦ C), a creep deformation-based model was utilised for studying the densification kinetics.  The  evaluated stress  exponent (n) ~1  and an activation energy ( QD )  of  ~888 kJ/mol suggest lattice diffusion  to  be  the  dominant  densification  mechanism  during  this  stage.  At  1850 ◦ C,  a  transition  to  grain boundary sliding was observed, with n ~1.5, as confirmed by microstructural analysis. In the final stage of sintering (T > 1850 ◦ C), a grain growth-based model with grain growth exponent ( m ) ~ 3 and an activation energy ( Qg ) of ~475 kJ/mol supported the dominance of lattice diffusion during grain growth. At 1850 ◦ C, a relative  density  of  ~97  %  was  achieved,  marking  a  shift  from  interface-controlled  deformation  via  grain boundary sliding to lattice diffusion-driven grain growth. Thus, this study highlights the sintering mechanisms of HfB2  ceramics at various SPS temperatures, plausibly aiding in microstructure optimization for improved performance of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) in extreme environments.

The sintering process parameters such as temperature, pressure and holding time significantly affect the densification process and grain growth.
