A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), owing to their exceptional performance under extreme heat and harsh conditions, have become the preferred choice for critical thermal protection materials in high-speed aerospace vehicles. Here, the HfC-TiC ceramics were fabricated through pressureless sintering, and its oxidation behaivor was  investigated  using  non-isothermal  method.  The  oxidation  activation  energy  of  HfC-TiC  sample  reaches 385.39 kJ/mol. Then the ablation behavior of HfC-TiC ceramics was tested under plasma ablation conditions (3000 K, 5.0 MW/m 2 ). The mass ablation rate and linear ablation rate of HfC-TiC can reach GLYPH<0> 1.11 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3  g ⋅ s GLYPH<0> 1 and GLYPH<0> 1.40 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3  mm ⋅ s GLYPH<0> 1 after  being  ablated  for  300 seconds,  respectively.  The  oxide  layers  in  the  center consisted of a porous oxide upper layer, a dense Ti-enriched intermediate layer, and an oxide inner layer. This complex multi-layered oxidative configuration synergistically protected the underlying carbide matrix, significantly enhancing the ablation resistance of HfC-TiC ceramics.
cracks  in  the  oxide  layer  [13].Therefore,  it  is  essential  to  introduce additional components, such as SiC. The oxidation product of SiC, SiO2, forms a liquid phase at high temperatures, effectively sealing pores and cracks,  and  creating  a  dense  Hf-Si-O  oxidation  layer  [14,15].  This significantly improves the oxidation and ablation resistance of the material. However, under low oxygen partial pressure, a large amount of SiC is depleted by active oxidation, ultimately resulting in the formation of a porous layer [16,17]. Additionally, the SiO2  phase rapidly volatilizes  in  environments  exceeding  2000 ℃ ,  thereby  expediting  the decomposition of the oxide layer [18].

A B S T R A C T
Despite  advancements  in  sintering  techniques,  understanding  of sintering kinetics and grain growth mechanisms in spark plasma sintered HfB2 remains limited, particularly under varying sintering conditions. Kalish et al. [21], provided valuable insights into the final stage densification during high-pressure hot-pressing of fine HfB2 particles at 1400 -1900 ◦ C  through  analysis  based  on  the  Nabarro-Herring  creep model. The densification (~98.6 %) is driven by stress-directed vacancy diffusion, with an activation energy of ~95.81 kJ/mol. The rate-controlling process  involves either  interstitial  lattice  diffusion  of boron or grain boundary diffusion of hafnium, while initial densification occurs through particle fragmentation and rearrangement. This creep-based estimation of the densification mechanism has been successfully applied to various ceramics and intermetallics [23 -29].
Studies on related systems provide critical insights into densification mechanisms and sintering kinetics of transition metal diboride (TMB2)- S. Dubey et al.

A B S T R A C T
Entropy-stabilized Ultra High-Temperature Ceramics (UHTC) offer a groundbreaking solution to the challenges of  extreme  environments,  showcasing  enhanced  mechanical  properties,  thermal  stability,  and  resistance  to oxidation  at  high  temperatures.  The  consolidation  of  UHTC  by  ultra-fast  high-temperature  sintering  (UHS) significantly reduces processing times and temperature and can produce dense high-performance ceramics with superior mechanical properties. This study reports the pressureless synthesis and consolidation of the entropystabilized  (Hf0.25Zr0.25Ti0.25V0.25)B2-B4C  composite  through  UHS  within  1  minute,  starting  from  transition metal diboride powders. B4C acts as an effective sintering aid, promoting the densification of the system and the formation of a nearly single-phase hexagonal diboride with a diboride-eutectic phase. Furthermore, a secondary minor hexagonal phase rich in V and Zr is formed close to the eutectic regions. Sintering currents of 40 A were necessary to reach densities higher than 90 % under pressureless conditions, achieving nano hardness higher than 27.3 GPa, comparable with high-entropy diborides produced by Spark Plasma Sintering. The study highlights the entropy-stabilized phase formation, diffusion, densification, and grain growth mechanisms involved during UHS. The work contributes to the understanding of entropy-stabilized ceramics produced by UHS as a faster and less energy-consuming process than conventional sintering methods.

3.4.1. Densification mechanisms during intermediate stage sintering

At 1850 ◦ C, a transition from intermediate to final stage of sintering is observed, which involves the closure of remaining pores, requiring  a  higher  activation  energy  for  continued  densification. Therefore, the activation energy for densification is a key parameter reflecting the temperature dependence of sintering kinetics, which helps identify the dominant mass transport mechanism. In this study, a single mechanism is proposed to dominate the densification process from 1750 to 1850 ◦ C with n ~1 -2. Activation energy is calculated from Fig. 4b as [( )( ) ( ) ]

The consistent strain rate of 6 × 10 GLYPH<0> 4 s GLYPH<0> 1 is  maintained  across  the  three  temperatures  ensuring  that  the observed  differences  in n are  purely  temperature-dependent  and  not influenced by varying strain rates or grain growth. The high activation energy of ~888 kJ/mol reflects the energy required for atomic diffusion to occur during densification, i.e., lattice diffusion to be the dominant mechanism over grain boundary diffusion during the intermediate stage of HfB ₂ sintering. This is also consistent with previous studies, such as those reported for reaction hot pressing (~695 kJ/mol) [32] and microwave sintering (~850 kJ/mol) [40] of similar diborides. Thus, the intermediate  stage  activation  energy  for  HfB2  confirms  the  predominance of energy-intensive mechanisms such as lattice diffusion within this temperature range (1750 -1850 ◦ C). The separation of H1900 and H1950 from this analysis highlights that these temperatures lie in the final  stage  of  sintering,  where  different  mechanisms,  such  as  pore closure and grain growth (as also elicited during microstructural analysis), become more significant.

UHTC COMPLEX SHAPES AND ARCHITECTURES BY WET FORMING TECHNIQUES
Emerging potential applications for UHTCs require complex shapes and/or functionalized surfaces. Some of the current densification technologies cannot satisfy per sé these requirements and machining is necessary. The good electrical conductivity (~10 6 S/cm) (Monteverde et al., 2002) of sintered ZrB 2 based ceramics helps in reducing conventional machining costs (time and tools) and allows the realization of complex geometries and surface functionalization by electrical discharge machining (EDM) starting from simple shapes produced by common densification techniques (Sonber and Suri, 2011). where k is universal gas constant, m is a constant that depends on the mass transport mechanism, and Q is the activation energy. If D 2 is less than D 1 then a reduction in the sintering temperature is predicted (i.e., T 2 < T 1). This is indeed the trend observed experimentally. Unfortunately, the activation energy of the grain-boundary diffusion in ZrB2, responsible for its densification, cannot be inferred from the data of T OIS, T MSR, or T OFS because T OIS is reached in the sintering regime controlled by surface diffusion, T MSR is reached at different degrees of densification, and T OFS is reached in many cases during the regime of isothermal heating at 1900 ◦ C. To compute this activation energy, the temperature at which 80% of densification is achieved ( T 80%) was measured, and is also plotted in Fig. 4. The choice of T 80% is because, with the degree of densification being a constant, it falls within the regime of non-isothermal heating controlled by grain-boundary diffusion for all the powders except the as-purchased powder which will not be included in the calculations. Applying the above expression with m =4 for grain-boundary diffusion, the activation energy is found to be 2070 kJ/mol. This is a very high activation energy, which explains why the commercially-available ZrB2 powders with micrometre-sized particles are considered to be intrinsically unsinterable without sintering additives at moderate temperatures.

6.10  CONCLUSIONS
Although UHTCs are considered to be poorly sinterable compounds, multiple processing options are available to achieve fully dense ceramics with controlled microstructures. All the possible MB 2 /sintering additive combinations have not been tested for both ZrB 2 and HfB 2 , but a tentative rationalization has been devised for the most important densification mechanisms. Densification can be accomplished by the following:
- Solid state sintering, in the presence of carbon, B 4 C, and WC, at temperatures between  1850  and  1950°C;  no  amorphous  phases  are  observed  along  grain boundaries.  Sintered  materials  are  very  refractory,  with  600  MPa  of  flexural strength up to 1500°C in air.
- Liquid-phase sintering in the presence of metals or Si 3 N 4 at temperatures generally lower than 1850°C for both ZrB 2 and HfB 2 .  Low-melting metallic phases, residual  Si-O-based  amorphous  phases,  and  low-melting  silicides  are  found along grain boundaries upon cooling. At temperatures greater than 1000°C, significant strength degradation is observed.
- Transient liquid-phase sintering, in the presence of MeSi 2 (Me = Zr, Mo, Ta, W), at temperatures 1700-1900°C. A special case is ZrSi 2 enabling densification at 1550°C. In all cases, formation of solid solutions and cation interdiffusion are
both mechanisms that help densification. By using ZrSi 2 ,  low-melting silicides are found at triple points, while amorphous grain boundaries are often observed by using TaSi 2 . In contrast, residual amorphous phases are rarely found in MB 2 -MeSi 2 composites  (Me = Mo, W), which retained strength values close to 600 MPa  at  1500°C  in  air,  with  appropriate  tuning  of  processing  and  sintering conditions.

The activation energy relationship based on Eq. 1, is shown in Fig. 9. The linear regression results allow us to estimate an activation energy of 317 kJ/mol for HEB phase grain growth. Lonergan et al. [43] reported that lattice diffusion was the primary sintering mechanism for ZrB2, at temperatures up to 2000 ◦ C, with Ea = 241 ± 62 kJ/mol. In comparison, the sintering activation energy for (TiZrHfVNbTa)C was 839 ± 53 kJ/mol [52], also associated with lattice diffusion, while for TiC with  390 kJ/mol  [52] grain  boundary  diffusion  was  the  primary mechanism. In the present HEB phase, the densification process involves a solid-state grain boundary diffusion and material transport through a transient  liquid  phase,  which  takes  place  at  the  grain  boundaries, improving the ceramic densification.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have been proposed to serve as thermal protection system (TPS) materials for various applications at temperatures above 2000 °C, such as sharp leading edges and nose cones of next-generation hypersonic vehicles, rocket propulsion lining and advanced energy systems [1,2]. Among the UHTCs, ternary Ta x Hf 1-x C (0 < x < 1) ceramics have attracted worldwide attention in recent decades due to their ultra-high melting point (> 3700 °C) [3] Owing to the same crystal structure with NaCl ( B1 , space group Fm3m ), continuous Ta x Hf 1-x C (0 < x < 1) solid solution could be obtained with TaC and HfC across the whole compositional ranges [4].
Di /uniFB00 usion-driven mass transport mechanism contributes mostly to solid-state densi /uniFB01 cation of Ta x Hf 1-x C (0 < x < 1) ceramics [5]. Since the di /uniFB00 usion activation energy of HfC is higher (HfC: 60.42 kJ/mol, TaC: 39.49 kJ/mol) [6], TaC-rich solid solution carbide (Ta0.8Hf0.2C) is more cost-e /uniFB00 ective to be consolidated with commercially available TaC

3.5. Influence of reaction kinetics on reaction sintering of UHTC layers
To this point, much of the analysis of SLRS-UHTCs has been centered on the relationship between non-oxide yields and reaction thermodynamics  without  considering  kinetics.  Thorough  precursor  conversion does not singularly dictate the viability of SLRS for UHTC production and compatibility with powder bed fusion AM. The ability to synthesize numerous refractory ceramics on AM-relevant time scales is only significant for refractory component fabrication if interparticle adhesion can  be  achieved.  Traditional  PBF-AM  relies  on  non-reactive  binding mechanisms like full/partial melting or sintering that are not generally applicable to UHTCs due to their high melting temperatures and poor response to laser energy deposition. For UHTCs with very low diffusion coefficients,  reaction  energies  associated  with  synthesis  can  initiate atomic  mobility  for  interparticle  adhesion.  This  reaction  bonding concept may be applied for the simultaneous synthesis and robust layer formation through interparticle adhesion for AM [78].
Reaction  bonding  during  SLRS  is  based  on  gas-solid  (or  liquid) reactivity and a dynamically changing mixture of product reactants and intermediates.  Thermal  activation  is  necessary  for  reactivity,  atomic transport, and sintering-induced densification. A generalized approach

4.3.1. Mode of energy input
Sintering of UHTCs is either through a solid-state or a liquid phase process. Sintering, as well as EBS, works on the principle of application of thermal energy for powder consolidation. Heat is generally applied to the pressed powder through an induction furnace or resistance heating furnace. Graphite resistors are utilized for pressureless sintering, while hydraulic pressure is applied in the case of hot pressing. Spark Plasma Sintering applies graphite dies and plungers that conduct a pulsed direct current [38]. The heating is usually uniform throughout the powder, and the processes typically last a considerable time to enable densification.
On the other hand, the EBS processing for UHTCs uses a concentrated electron beam as a heat source. The accelerated electrons, which are traveling at about 70% of the speed of light, fall onto the powder bed resulting  in  a  conversion  of  kinetic  energy  to  thermal  energy.  The thermal energy is maintained in such a way that the sintering additive (ZrSi2) is melted, forming a liquid phase that binds the UHTC (ZrB2) grains together. There have already been attempts to sinter ZrB2 based UHTCs through the liquid phase sintering process using various doping agents or sintering additives [39]. However, such a process has not been explored so much using electron beam technology. In this research direction, stability of the melt pool and the control of the molten material are of prime importance, and that is achieved by carefully controlling the parameters that define the EBS process (beam power, beam scanning speed, preheat temperature, beam spot size, etc.)

1. Introduction
All the so-called ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs; i.e., borides, carbides, and nitrides of the early-transition metals [1]) have poor intrinsic sinterability due to the serious kinetic restrictions imposed by their strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coef /uniFB01 cients. This is further aggravated extrinsically because the starting powders normally comprise large particles of various microns in size and with surface oxide impurities. Not surprisingly, dedicated sintering studies constitute a large part of the research effort carried out on UHTCs, and particularly on ZrB2-based UHTCs [2,3]. They have enabled the identi /uniFB01 cation of appropriate sintering additives and comminution practices. Some of these suitable additives are carbon [4,5], B4C [6], WC [7,8], and B4C þ C [9,10], which favor the conventional solid-state sintering of

UHTCs by the carbothermal reduction of the surface metal oxides. This has been proven both for pressureless sintering (PS) and hot-pressing (HP), both of which apply prolonged sintering cycles (i.e., various hours). Apart from facilitating the conventional sintering, cleaning oxides off of the grain boundaries also results in improved high-temperature strength [5,8]. Furthermore, solid-state sintering additives are normally used synergistically with the wet-attrition milling of the starting powders because solid-state sinterability scales inversely with a power of the particle size (i.e., Herring's scaling law [11]).

3. Processing techniques for UHTCs
Effective sintering of UHTCs requires the initial powder to have high purity  (although  metallic  impurities  aid  in  the  densification  process, whereas oxide impurities promote grain growth and are detrimental for densification),  small  powder  particle  size,  narrow  particle-size  distribution, and low agglomeration factor [45]. The low self-diffusion coefficient and strong covalent bonds in UHTCs make sintering difficult [46]. Hot pressing (HP) has typically been employed to densify UHTCs [47 -53], while achieving 94 -97% densification in TaC-based composites sintered at 1800 -2300 ◦ C [50 -53], while 98 -100% densification is obtained in  ZrB2-  based  composites  upon  sintering  in  1600 -1870 ◦ C range [47,49]. To achieve full densification of ZrB2 -30ZrC -10SiC, the total sintering time is reduced from ~140 min in HP (with 3.7 vol % Si3N4 as a sintering aid) to ~23 min in SPS (without any sintering aid) [54].  With  HP  and  SPS,  complex  shapes  cannot  be  prepared,  which pushes utilization of pressureless sintering (with inherently poor mechanical properties attributed to poor densification). Pressureless sintering and hot pressing of ZrB2  have shown Vickers ' hardness of 14.5 GPa and 23 GPa, the elastic modulus of 454 GPa and 489 GPa, and flexural  strength  of  444  MPa  and  565  MPa, respectively  [55]. Other techniques to consolidate UHTCs are atmospheric plasma spraying [56], vacuum plasma spraying [8] and chemical vapor infiltration [57], etc. The pros and cons of these processing techniques towards enunciating microstructure  and  resulting  consolidation,  illustrated  in  Table

Production of UHTC Complex Shapes and Architectures
Pressureless sintering  (PLS) was  considered  quite  impossible  for  UHTCs  until  the 1980s. Densification can be improved using liquid phase formers or reactive agents (Guo, 2009). Refractory metals such as Ni, Fe, Co, Mo, Re, Cu and Cr (Cheng & Gadalla, 1996; Einarsrud et al., 1997; Khanra & Godkini, 2005; Mishra et al., 2002; Yan et al., 2006) as well as disilicide of transition metals, such as MoSi 2 and ZrSi 2 (Guo et al., 2008; Sciti et al., 2006b; Sciti et al., 2006c), have been experimented as liquid phase formers. Metal additions resulted more efficient when amounting to ≥ 2
wt% and when sintering was performed in argon atmosphere rather than in vacuum (Chech et al., 1965). These two conditions ensure respectively sufficient formation and continuous presence and action of a liquid phase (added metals can be lost in vacuum due to volatilization) (Guo, 2009). The action of metallic additives that influence sintering is related to a contraction of the ZrB 2 crystal lattice that affects the surface free energy and thus increases the driving force for densification. For example, additions of Mo decreased the energy of activation of the densification, due to its incorporation into a ZrB 2 solid solution (Kislui & Kuzenkova, 1966).

4. Conclusions
Temperature-dependent  sintering  kinetics  of  HfB ₂ during  spark plasma sintering (SPS) is evaluated at intermediate and final sintering stages.  At  T < 1850 ◦ C  (i.e.,  1750 ◦ C  and  1800 ◦ C),  densification mechanisms dominate, which is characteristic of the intermediate stage of sintering. However, at 1850 ◦ C, grain boundary sliding also contributes to the densification process marked by an increase in the stress exponent n (~1.5). The calculated activation energy QD ~ 888 kJ/mol is a critical parameter, confirming the lattice diffusion-controlled nature of densification in the intermediate stage. At T > 1900 ◦ C densification threshold (~97 %) is reached, thereby entering the final stage of sintering, with grain growth exponent m ~3 , confirming lattice diffusion assisted  grain  growth  as  the  dominant  mechanism  ( R 2 ~0.904, and activation  energy QG  ~ 475  kJ/mol ) .  The  microstructural  evolution, evidenced  by  a  reduction  in  grain  boundary  porosity  (~98.26  % decrease in H1950) and an increase in grain size from H1750 (~5.5 ± 1.9 μ m) to H1950 (~34.5 ± 14.1 μ m), highlights the transition from early-stage sintering (T < 1750 ◦ C), dominated by surface diffusion and limited grain boundary movement, to intermediate (1750 -1850 ◦ C) and final  stages  (1850 -1950 ◦ C).  It  further  confirms  lattice  diffusion  and grain  boundary  sliding  to  be  the  primary  densification  mechanisms, with grain boundary sliding becoming particularly dominant at 1850 ◦ C, consistent with obtained high activation energy. This transition in the sintering  mechanism  from  intermediate  to  final  stage  highlights

question: What holding or dwell time was used during sintering of the ceramic materials?
answer:
The holding or dwell times used during sintering of the ceramic materials varied depending on the specific process and material:

- For the sintering of ZrB2-SiC powder containing B4C and C as sintering additives, the sintering schedule included external isothermal holds at 600 8 C and 1600 8 C for 30 min in vacuum, and then the material was pressureless sintered to 2100 8 C with a holding time of 2 h [1].
- In the production of ZrB2-based composites using ball-milling and rapid Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS), the holding times ranged from 1 to 30 min, depending on the sintering temperature [2].
- For the densification of TiB2-based materials, the dwell time was lower than that reported in literature, with specific values not provided in the text [3].
- In the preparation of TaB2-SiC granules, the sintering was carried out at 1600 ◦ C without holding, but an intermediate holding for an hour at 300 ◦ C was made to remove the organic binder [4].
- In the sintering of ZrB2-SiC-ZrO2f ceramics, the holding time was 60 min at 1850 ◦ C under 30 MPa pressure [8].
- For the sintering of ZrB2 ceramics using spark plasma sintering, the holding times ranged from 4 to 6 min depending on the sintering temperature [13].
- In the case of HfB2 ceramic powder sintering, a dwell time of 10 min at 1900 ◦ C was used [18].

The whole sintering schedule included external isothermal holds at 600 8 C and 1600 8 C for 30 min in vacuum (15 Pa), respectively, and then argon gas (1 atm) was then introduced into the furnace. The gelcast ceramic was then continually pressureless sintered to 2100 8 C with a holding time of 2 h.

A B S T R A C T
Short /uniFB01 bre reinforced ceramic composites, based on ZrB2 with a carbon /uniFB01 bre content of 45 vol%, were produced by ball-milling and rapid Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) at 1800 -2300 °C by varying the holding times. Careful control of the sintering parameters, such as the heating rate, dwell temperature and holding time are required to produce a fully dense matrix and avoid /uniFB01 bre degradation. The sintered materials were characterized in terms of relative density, morphology of the /uniFB01 bres and microstructural features of the matrix. The /uniFB01 nal residual porosity ranged from 16 to 3 vol%. The carbon /uniFB01 bres maintained their original morphology and the matrix consisted of ZrB2, SiC and ZrC phases. In spite of the good adhesion between the /uniFB01 bre surface and the matrix promoted by SPS processing, extensive /uniFB01 bre pull-out was observed in the fracture surfaces of all of the composites sintered at or below 1900 °C.

3.1. Densification behaviour
High-density samples ( > 97 %, see Table 2) were obtained despite a dwell time lower than that reported in literature [48,49]. TW2 samples showed the lowest onset temperature (TON) of 1010 ◦ C. This temperature is quite low compared to other similar TiB2-based materials [50], and is in agreement with the previous work [39]. However, the sintering phenomena start to slow down before the maximum temperature is reached, as indicated by the peak of the densification rate curve occurring well before the dwell step and the plateauing of the curve at 97 % before the start of dwell time (Fig. 1a). This sintering behaviour can be attributed to the presence of Co, which promotes liquid sintering mechanisms that require lower temperatures to be activated, but were not sufficient to complete densification [51]. ZW6 samples reached full densification quickly, while HW6 samples required a longer isothermal hold due to the refractory nature of HfB2. In particular, ZrB2-based material showed the highest densification rate value of 7.3 % min GLYPH<0> 1 allowing full densification within a limited dwell time. It is worth noting that the observed TON of 1400 ◦ C is lower than those reported for ZrB2 with other ceramic sintering aids (Si3N4, SiC, Y2O3, Al2O3, ZrN) [52 -54]. A lower TON was only obtained when using Ni as the sintering aid [55]. However, Ni prevented grain growth, which is desired to increase the thermal conductivity. For this reason, although the activation of densification mechanisms at low temperature and reaching the plateau took less than 15 min, the isothermal hold was extended to 30 min to ensure a coarse and homogeneous microstructure and to raise the thermal conductivity. Noteworthy, the manufacturing process of this work allowed to reduce the starting densification temperature (TON) compared to similar ZrB2 materials [56] containing higher amount of starting WC (15 vol% instead of 4.1 vol%).

2. Materials and methods
Dispersed granular inclusions were obtained by the  method described  in  Ref.  [26].  TaB2 -SiC  powder  mixtures  were  coated  with oleic acid in a rotating ball mill for 14 h. After that, a 1% solution of polyvinyl alcohol was sprayed into an open pot rotating at an angle to the horizon at a speed of 15 rpm using an argon spray gun. The sprayed polyvinyl alcohol solution coated the particles in the mixture and stuck them together. The rotation of the pot led to an increase in the average size of the formed granules. Ceramic granules were sifted through laboratory sieves between mesh 270 and mesh 100, the size of the selected granules ranged from 50 to 150 μ m. The sintering of the granules was carried  out  to  give  them  sufficient  strength  for  further  work  on  the formation of matrix -granule powder mixtures. The sintering temperature was 1600 ◦ C without holding. During the sintering process, an intermediate  holding  for  an  hour  at  300 ◦ C  was  made  to  remove  the organic binder, achieved at a rate of 50 ◦ C h GLYPH<0> 1 . Heating from 300 to 1600 ◦ C was carried out at a rate of 200 ◦ C h GLYPH<0> 1 .
After sintering, the granules had an irregular shape with an average size of 167.3 ± 11.5 μ m, estimated according to the SEM micrograph intercept procedure described in ASTM E112, Fig. 1. A similar approach was applied to estimate the average grain size of sintered ceramics.
The matrix phase and the granules were mixed in a magnetic mixer in an ethanol medium. The final sintering of the studied materials was carried  out  under  a  pressure  of  about  150  MPa  at  a  temperature  of 1800 ◦ C in a vacuum with holding for 20 min. Fig. 2 illustrates the structure  of  the  formed  composites.  The  sample  compositions  are presented in Table 1.
Fig. 1. A general view of TaB2 -SiC granules.

3.5. Machine learning model interpretation
The peak sintering temperature also exerts a positive influence on final material hardness. Extensive studies [39] confirm that densification  behavior  and  grain  growth  in  ceramic  materials  predominantly depend on sintering temperature, holding time, and heating rate. Optimized sintering temperatures enable the fabrication of dense HfB2 materials with refined grains. While relative density increases with elevated sintering temperatures, excessive temperature gains provide diminishing returns on densification and conversely promote grain coarsening, ultimately  reducing  material  hardness  [40].  The  optimal  hardness sample in this study was achieved without employing maximum sintering temperature or holding duration, validating this conclusion and providing guidance for energy-efficient parameter optimization in highhardness UHTC fabrication.
Current  reported  hardness  values  for  HfB2-SiC-X  system  UHTCs predominantly range between 15 -30 GPa, though discrepancies exist due to variations in preparation and testing methodologies. This study successfully identified higher hardness formulations through machine learning-assisted optimization with merely two iterative cycles (8 total samples),  achieving  a  peak  hardness  of  25.13  GPa.  Compared  with conventional trial-and-error approaches, this machine learningintegrated  methodology  demonstrates  superior  efficiency  in  resource conservation while accelerating research progress, offering promising technical support for rapid performance optimization and discovery of novel UHTCs.

3.2. Microwave sintering
Microwave  sintering  utilizes  the  dielectric  properties  of  ceramic materials within a microwave electromagnetic field to achieve sintering and densification by directly heating the material to the required temperature.  Unlike  traditional  sintering,  which  relies  on  heat  radiation from the surface inward, microwave sintering heats the entire material uniformly by converting absorbed microwave energy into the kinetic and potential energy of the material ' s internal molecules. This method enables rapid heating and sintering, increasing the kinetic energy and diffusion  coefficient  of  molecules  or  ions,  thus  allowing  for  lowtemperature, fast sintering without significant grain growth [78]. The sintering temperature in microwave sintering is typically 100 -300 ◦ C lower than in conventional methods, with a significantly shorter holding time (0 -20 min) and a reduced sintering cycle by 50 % GLYPH<0> 90 %. The process of microwave sintering has several advantages: it eliminates the need for heat conduction, reduces thermal inertia, and allows for instant heating and cooling, making it highly efficient and energy-saving. The resulting material has finer grains and improved mechanical properties, making  microwave  sintering  a  promising  technique  for  producing high-strength, high-toughness, and high-hardness ceramic tools [78].

In another study, Mani ` ere et al. used a finite element model for determining the tooling temperatures of flash spark plasma sintering and investigated the  initiation  of  graphite  creep  for  high  temperatures  and  pressures (Fig. 5(b)) [61].

A B S T R A C T
In this work, pure polycrystalline ZrC bulk ceramics were obtained using commercial sub-micron zirconium carbide powder under 5.0 GPa and 800 -1500 ◦ C with a holding time of 10 min. The effect of sintering temperature  on  the  mechanical  properties,  microstructural,  sintering behavior and thermal stability of pure ZrC bulk ceramics is discussed.

4.4. Spark Plasma Sintering
In the case of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of ZrB2 ceramics, the effect of processing parameters (sintering time and sintering temperature) and powder particle size (nano- or micron- sized) on the densification has been shown in Fig. 7b [120]. It can be noted that the densification rate is extremely high for the smaller particle size (≤25 nm) and near-theoretical densification of ZrB2 is attained even at very low sintering temperatures (below 1700 °C) and shorter sintering times of 16 min or lesser. The spark plasma sintered di-borides are usually observed to possess fine microstructure and good combination of properties. In order to address some issues related to non-uniform densification (and properties) primarily due to fast heating rate and lesser holding times, recently we had explored the application of multi-stage spark plasma sintering (MSS-SPS) schedule, which involves holding at one or more intermediate temperatures for few minutes before reaching the final sintering temperatures [98,173-179]. Keeping the total holding time constant (i.e. lesser holding time at the final sintering temperature), more uniform densification and finer microstructures were achieved for ZrB2-SiC-TiSi2 [175] and TiB2-TiSi2 composites [98], resulting in still improved mechanical properties (as compared to similar compositions sintered via the usual single-step SPS route).

A B S T R A C T
SiC, as well as the presence of surface oxides such as ZrO2, SiO2, and B2O3. Therefore, these composites require very high temperature (>1800°C) and long hold time (> 45 min) for sintering using conventional processes such as hot pressing, pressure-less sintering and hot isostatic pressing [11 -19]. However, holding for longer durations at very high temperatures are not considered as desirable due to the possibilities of grain growth. Lately, sintering methods such as reactive hot pressing (RHP), reactive spark plasma sintering (RSPS) and spark plasma sintering (SPS) have become more popular than the conventional sintering techniques [20 -27]. The SPS, one of the pressure-assisted sintering techniques, is widely used to sinter ceramic based composites [21 -24]. In this process, the cumulative e /uniFB00 ect of uniaxial pressure along with the Joule heating and formation of plasma enables the densi /uniFB01 cation process to occur in a very short duration [28 -31]. In order to identify the densi /uniFB01 cation mechanism, Bernard-Granger and Guizard [32] have proposed a relatively simpler analytical model, which assumes the macroscopic applied pressure as the main driving force for pressure-assisted sintering. This model is based on the steady state creep model, which describes the creep of dense materials, established by Mukherjee et al. [33]. 2.1. Material manufacturing
All powders were shaped in a disk of 45 mm of diameter under linear pressure of 60 bar. Then the disks were hot-pressed in the vacuum range of 0.1 -1 mbar using an induction-heated graphite die, which allowed a heating rate above 1200 ◦ C/h (the time required for the heating stage from 950 ◦ C was about 25 min), and applying a constant uniaxial pressure of 35 MPa. The sintering temperatures and dwell times are detailed in Table 2. Dwell times were adjusted from 5 to 50 min to achieve a stable shrinkage plateau and a good densification. The change in thickness of the green pellets under hot-pressing was recorded during heating up and final dwell and then converted into relative density data, under the hypothesis of a full mass conservation. The temperature was monitored by means of an optical pyrometer focused into a blind hole dug on the external surface of the graphite die. At the end of the dwell, free cooling followed. The detail of heating rate and the calculated continuous densification of the pellets are showed in Fig. 1.
From the sintered pellets 12.5 mm × 12.5 mm x 1 mm tiles were machined. A 12.5 mm × 12.5 mm surface of the tiles was manually polished using diamond paste from 30 μ m down to 0.25 μ m grain size.

3.1. SPS sintering behavior
Fig. 1a shows the variation of the displacement, vacuum, temperature and pressure with the sintering time during densifying the HfB2 ceramic powder. The applied pressure of 4 kN(30 MPa) (red line) from the room temperature to 1000 ◦ C and the heating rate of 100 ◦ C/min are firstly  used  at  the  initial  stage,  under  which  the  heat  expand  of  the sample gives the increase in both the displacement (blue line) and the vacuum (green line) due to the gas emission. Further increasing the applied  pressure  up  to  7  kN  (50  MPa),  the  displacement  starts  to decrease at T = 1383 ◦ C, which indicates the densifying occurs due to the powder rearrangement under the load. The slope of the displacement curve first increases and then decreases because of the reduction of the heating rate from 100 to 50 ◦ C/min at T = 1400 ◦ C. Due to gas continually emitting, the highest vacuum is obtained at T = 1673 ◦ C. After that, the fast densification of the HfB2  sample takes place because the sintering neck formation and growth enhance the atomic diffusion. The vacuum also exhibits a sharp decrease. During the dwell time for 10 min at  T = 1900 ◦ C,  the  slope  of  the  displacement  curve continually decreases, giving a high dense sample. When there is no pore between HfB2 particles, the neck growth stops, and the densification ends.
The shrinkage rate of HfB2 specimens at different sintering temperatures  as  the  function  of  sintering  time  is  shown  in  Fig.  1b.  All  the maximum shrinkage rate at the different sintering temperatures occurs at sharp shrinkage stage, and when sintering temperature is increased from 1800 ◦ C to 1900 ◦ C, the maximum shrinkage rate increase from 0.34 mm/min to 0.54 mm/min, which shows the sintering temperature plays  an  important  role  in  achieving  the  fast  densification  of  HfB2 ceramic.

Carbon Vacancy Ordered Non-Stoichiometric ZrC0.6
rent enable the compacted powder to be sintered densely in a short dwell time and at relatively low temperature. Interestingly, the ordered carbon vacancies can be achieved simultaneously during the SPS-sintering process. No long-time post annealing is required for realization of the ordered carbon vacancies after the sintering.

2.1. Materials and sintering
In the SPS process, nano-sized 80 vol.% ZrB2  powders (0.5 μ m, purity > 99 %, Forsman Scientific (Beijing) Co. Ltd., China) and 20 vol.% SiC powders (0.5 μ m, purity > 99 %, Forsman Scientific (Beijing) Co. Ltd.,  China)  were  used  as  the  starting  materials.  In  the  preparation process, the powders were mixed firstly, and then were ball-milled in absolute ethanol for 12 h using ZrO2  balls, at a vibration frequency of 30 Hz. Next, the milled powders were dried in a resistance furnace at 120 ℃ for 10 h. Subsequently, the resulting powders were put in a cylindrical  graphite  die  and  pre-pressed  at  room  temperature  with  a pressure of 10 MPa. After that, the pre-shaped cylinder was sintered in a SPS  apparatus  (LABOX-325S,  Sinter  Land  Inc.,  Japan).  The  sintering process includes the following three steps [39]. First, the sample was heated to an intermediate temperature of 1000 o C with a heating rate of 200 o C/min and then was kept for 1 min. Second, the sample was heated to a peak temperature of 1800 o C in the next 8 min and kept for 15 min. Third, the sample was cooled down to room temperature with a cooling rate of 75 o C/min, as shown in Fig. 1(a). It should be noted that a constant pressure of 40 MPa was applied on the pressure head during the whole sintering stage. The dimensions of the fabricated samples were approximately Φ 20 mm × 5 mm.

3. Results and discussion
Regarding the mechanical properties, the Vickers hardness and toughness measured at room temperature were 15.4 ± 0.9 GPa and 2.0 ± 0.5 MPa m 0.5 for ZrB2 + 20 vol% MoSi2, 10.3 ± 0.6 GPa and 2.9 ± 0.5 MPa m 0.5 for ZrB2 + 20 vol%TaSi2, and 11 ± 1 GPa and 2.1 ± 0.3 MPa m 0.5 for ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2, respectively. The hardness of ZrB2 + 20 vol%MoSi2 is well within the typical range reported for ZrB2-MoSi2 ceramics [25,26], whereas the lower hardnesses of ZrB2 + 20 vol%TaSi2 and ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2 are attributable to the greater ductility of their second phases. This would also explain the toughness trends, which do not correlate directly with the grain size. Fig. 3 shows the Hertz indentation stress-strain curves of the three ZrB2 + 20 vol%MeSi2 (Me = Mo, Ta, or Zr) UHTCs processed in this study. The maximum testing temperature was 800 ◦ C, above which creep already occurred at the high contact pressures used in the Hertzian indention. In the case of ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2, however, the tests were interrupted at 600 ◦ C because, as shown in Fig. 4, at 800 ◦ C in air this UHTC already underwent severe peptization (confirmed by X-ray energy dispersive spectrometry in the SEM; not shown) that impeded its mechanical characterization. Therefore, the ZrB2 ceramic sintered here with ZrSi2 additive does not seem to fulfil the criterion of tolerance to the high temperatures in oxidizing environments that is demanded of UHTCs, an aspect that requires further investigation in future studies. 1. Introduction
Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is an ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) that generates great interest in the aerospace sector, in particular for the protection of vehicle aerosurfaces exposed to high-flow environments of hot oxidizing gases, as is the case in hypersonic flights and atmospheric re-entry [1,2]. However, ZrB2 alone is very brittle and does not have sufficient toughness, strength, and thermal shock resistance to be used successfully for such aerospace applications [1,2]. Reducing this inherent brittleness has thus become a critical issue to ensure the mechanical integrity of ZrB2 UHTCs when they are in service. The strategy adopted to achieve this goal consists of engineering the microstructure of the ZrB2 UHTCs, normally by the addition of second phases [1-26] and sometimes also by the fabrication of laminated-type architectures with compressive residual stresses [27-29] or of complex cell-type architectures [30]. SiC is the typical second phase particle in ZrB2 [1-3], and its introduction improves the toughness because of the crack defection and crack-wake bridging in the weak ZrB2-SiC interfaces. The addition of SiC also enhances the strength of ZrB2 because SiC inhibits grain growth thus reducing the dominant initial flaw size. Other ceramic second phases such as MoSi2, ZrC, HfN, and TiN [1,2,4], or combinations of various second phases [1,2,4-7], that improve densification may also inhibit grain growth improving the strength and toughness of the material.

3.4.1. Hardness
At  the  nanoscale,  all  compositions  exhibit  comparable  hardness values. Consequently, bulk hardness was assessed using Vickers indentation, revealing lower values (24 -29 GPa) compared to that of nanoindentation. The microhardness of (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 at 4.9 N is 27.25 ± 1.8 GPa, falling in between that of (Zr,Ti,Ta)C (29.25 ± 1.5 GPa) and (Zr,Ti) B2  (24.34 ± 1.9 GPa). Variations in hardness at different scales can be attributed to the indenter size effect [41]. Nanoindentation, with a ~150 nm indenter  tip  and  low  loads  (e.g.  8  mN),  confines  indents  within grains (2 -3 μ m), thereby measuring the intrinsic hardness of a specific phase by minimizing the influence of grain boundaries, microporosity, and  defects  [36].  In  contrast,  microindentation  employs  a  larger indenter and higher load (e.g., 4.9 N), encompassing both grain interiors and boundaries, which introduces contributions from these heterogeneities,  resulting  in  lower  hardness  values  [42].  Furthermore,  the dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 composite, having finer grains, lower porosity, and a 58 % carbide phase fraction, exhibits higher hardness than the boride (Zr,Ti)B2. Moreover, the Vickers hardness of all three specimens is more than that of their monolithic counterparts [43,44], because of the solid solution hardening. Even for dual phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, B2  composites, hardness values (~27 GPa) are higher than the previously reported many dual phase composites i.e., ~24 GPa for (Hf, Nb, Ta, Ti, Zr) boride-carbide dual phase [11], ~23 and ~24 GPa for (Zr,Ta) C and (Hf,Zr,Ta)C [44], respectively, as shown in Fig. 7b.

3. Results and discussion
The hardness and toughness measured by Vickers testing were 17.6( ± 0.2) GPa and 3.0( ± 0.1) MPa m 0.5 for the ZrB2-ZrO2 composite, and 16.2( ± 0.2) GPa and 1.9( ± 0.1) MPa m 0.5 for the reference ZrB2 monolith. The increased toughness of the ZrB2-ZrO2 composite compared to the reference ZrB2 monolith, which has a grain size more than twice as large, highlights the fundamental role of ZrO2 in enhancing the ZrB2 toughness via crack bridging and its transformation toughening in accordance with previous observations [22-24]. Furthermore, despite ZrO2 being softer than ZrB2, the ZrB2-ZrO2 composite is harder than the reference ZrB2 monolith, in part because the former is fully dense whereas the latter is only ∼ 96% dense (see Figs. 2B and 3).

A B S T R A C T
In the recent years, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) based on transition metal carbides (TaC, ZrC, TiC) and diborides (ZrB2, TiB2) have emerged as promising materials for re-entry applications. A boride -carbide dualphase composite was designed in order to achieve good mechanical properties with a fine microstructure and strong interfacial bonding. Three compositions: (i) (Zr,Ti)B2, (ii) (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, and (iii) dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 were synthesized using spark plasma sintering at 1850 ◦ C, 40 MPa for 30 min, achieving ~99 % densification. Phase analysis confirmed single-phase solid solutions in (Zr,Ti)B2  and (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, while the dual-phase (Zr,Ti, Ta)C,B2  composite comprised ~42 % boride and ~58 % carbide. Nanoindentation analysis (8 mN) showed a uniform hardness (~36 GPa) for all the compositions, whereas microindentation revealed intermediate hardness of ~27 GPa for the dual phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2, as compared to that of ~29 GPa for (Zr,Ti,Ta)C and ~24 GPa for (Zr,Ti)B2

1. Introduction
To thrive under extreme high-temperature conditions, the design of UHTC coatings prioritizes exceptional ablation resistance, high mechanical performances and interfacial adhesion, homogeneous material distribution, and a matched coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) with the substrate. For a coating to be deemed qualified, it must endeavor to fulfill these stringent design criteria to the highest possible extent. In order to enhance the performance and service life of UHTC coatings in long-term high temperature and high velocity airflow, researchers began to study UHTC coatings in terms of both structural design and material optimization. Hu et  al .  [13]  designed a SiC/UHTC/SiC  three-layer  coating  on  C/SiC  composites via CVD  and  brushingsintering. The inner SiC layer (CVD at 1050-1150 ℃) reduced thermal mismatch. The ZrB2-based UHTC middle layer (ZrB2/SiC/SiO2/B2O3/Al2O3) was sintered at 900 ℃ in Ar/air, forming borosilicate glass to seal cracks. The outer SiC layer (CVD at 12001250  ℃)  inhibited  B2O3  evaporation  and  oxidized  into  SiO2  at  high  temperatures. During oxidation at 1100-1500 ℃, ZrB2 reacted with oxygen to form ZrO2 and B2O3, while the B2O3-SiO2 glass phase exhibited fluidity to seal cracks. This synergy achieved low  mass  losses  (0.36-3.45  mg/cm²  after  68-114  h),  enabling  long-term  protection through oxygen barrier formation, self-healing, and thermal stress mitigation. Feng et al .  [14]  developed  a  spent  MoSi2-modified  ZrB2-SiC-MoSi2  composite  coating  on carbon substrates via spark plasma sintering (SPS). The coating was sintered at 1500 ℃ under 30 MPa pressure, forming a pore-free structure (~800 μm thick) with continuous and tight metallurgical bonding at  the  interface. 4. Conclusion
In  this  work,  we  use  the  natural  adsorption  and  in-siu  chemical conversion of ZrB2-polycarbosilane mixed slurry to construct UHTC-SiC antioxidation  coating  on  the  surface  of  3D  Cf/UHTC-SiC  composite, which effectively fills the original pores and forms a dense layer of ZrB2SiC coating.
The  PCS  after  infiltration  and  curing  parceled  around  the  ZrB2 granules and fill the gaps among the ZrB2 particles. After in-situ chemical conversion to nano SiC under 1300 ◦ C, the physical contacted ZrB2 granules are densely bonded in-situ. It is this kind of filling and sintering mechanism that endows ZrB2-SiC antioxidation coating prepared under such low temperature with high uniformity and density, which is very hard to obtain by conventional methods.
Most importantly, the three oxide layers of dense glassy SiO2, sintered SiO2-ZrO2, and particle stacked ZrO2 provide stable anti-ablation protection to the matrix. It resists constant entry and impact from the ultra-high temperature and strong oxidizing airflow. The 3D PyC-Cf/ ZrC-SiC with ZrB2-SiC coating shows nearly zero ablation under 1800 ◦ C holding for 300 s, which realizes the synergy between antioxidation and toughening.  The  awkward  situation  of  oxide  layer  peeling  is  also addressed, thus improving the anti-ablation performance effectively.
This brand-new strategy of preparing surface coating is faster and more  low-cost  compared  with  traditional  methods  such  as  chemical vapor deposition, plasma spraying, liquid precursors, and embedding. Moreover, it is not restricted by UHTC components or matrix composite with promising universality and designability, which could be extended to the surface antioxidation modification of C/C, C/SiC, graphite, and high-temperature metal materials.

A B S T R A C T
In the present work, five kinds of UHTCs coatings (ZrC, ZrC-SiC, ZrB2, ZrB2-SiC, ZrB2-ZrC-SiC) were fabricated. The difference of ablation behavior between ZrB2- and ZrC-based coatings was evaluated and focused on. The microstructure  evolution  of  resulted  oxide  layer  were  characterized  in  detail.  The  results  showed  that  the ablation resistance of ZrB2-based coating was better than that of ZrC-based coating under the same environment, exhibiting lower surface temperature, thinner thickness of both the total oxide layer and the SiC-depleted layer. The possible reasons for the phenomena were supposed based on microstructure characters and thermodynamic analysis.
phase to enhance UHTCs oxidation resistant performance at high/ultra-high temperature. Related reports of the ablation behaviors of ZrC-SiC and ZrB2-SiC were summarized in Table 1. It is found that appropriate incorporation of SiC was beneficial to improve the ablation resistance of ZrC and ZrB2, due to the formation of silica or borosilicate glasses with high viscosity and oxygen diffusion resistance. Even though the ablation mechanism of ZrC-SiC and ZrB2-SiC has been studied by several  groups  [6 -20],  the  difference  of  ablation  behaviors  between ZrC-SiC and ZrB2-SiC was rarely investigated and reported.
At  the  present  stage,  the  reported  fabrication  methods  of  UHTCs coatings include pack cementation (PC) [15,18], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [8], plasma spraying (PS) [21 -23], reactive melt infiltration  (RMI)  [20]  and  a  combination  of  multiple  methods  [16,20]. Vacuum plasma spray (VPS), on account of the merits such as efficient deposition rates, controllable spray thickness and inert gas protection, is an effective method for fabricating UHTC coatings [16,21 -23].

4.2.1. Processing, microstructure, and the role of impurities
Carbon- and carbon-based additives have essentially been found to assist sintering of ZrB2 by removing surface oxides, which are prevalent on ZrB2  particles ' surfaces  [122].  Apart  from  densification,  sintering additives, such as SiC, TaB2, B4C, and MoSi2, have improved the mechanical, thermal, and oxidative properties of UHTCs. SiC among these is  considered one of the best additives [7,86,123 -131]. SiC has been used in different contents (~up to 40 vol %), in different powder particle sizes  (micron, sub-micron or nano-sized) and in different forms (like whiskers, fibres and particulates) [122]. The particulate SiC reinforcement  is  discussed  in  this  section,  whereas  aspects  of  SiC  fiber  reinforcement  are  discussed  in  section  5  and  7.  Different  content  of  SiC (5 -40 vol %) has been reinforced in ZrB2  [132] sintered using SPS at 1900 ◦ C. Complete densification ( > 99%) occurred with 20 vol % SiC reinforcement. Though hardness increased with increasing SiC content (22.7 GPa with 40 vol % SiC), highest flexural strength (553 MPa) and highest fracture toughness (2.66 MPam 1/2 ) was obtained with 10 vol % SiC and 20 vol % SiC addition, respectively. On the other hand, 10 vol % SiC  reinforced  ZrB2 exhibited  the  lowest  ablation  and  oxidation resistance as compared to that of 15 vol % and 30 vol % reinforcement when tested under different heat fluxes in plasma wind tunnel [133]. The presence of SiC can be observed at the triple point junction of ZrB2 grains (Fig. 13a), however, HRTEM image from one of such interfaces shows a thin (3 -6 nm) amorphous oxide layer, Fig. 13b [130]. Further, carbon-based  additives  such  as  CNT  and  GNP  have  also  shown

A B S T R A C T
To mitigate this issue, researchers have explored the addition of SiC, MoSi2 [14,15], and LaB6 [16], to ZrB2 ceramics. These additives, achieved through the introduction of cations, alter the viscosity of the glass phase that develops during oxidation, thereby enhancing the ablation resistance of the ZrB2 coating. Among high-temperature ceramic materials, MoSi2 stands out, offering the capacity to form continuous SiO2 scale at high temperatures. Extensive research has highlighted the role of MoSi2 as an antioxidant additive [17 -20]. Coatings composed of SiC/SiC-MoSi2-ZrB2 [21], ZrSi2-MoSi2-ZrB2 [22], and MoSi2-(ZrB2-SiC) [23] have been shown to consistently produce a composite oxide glass, comprising SiO2, ZrO2, and ZrSiO4 with superior thermal stability and self-healing characteristics at high temperatures. This effectively prevents oxygen from permeating the inner and reaching the substrate, significantly enhancing the oxidation resistance when compared to pure MoSi2 or ZrB2 coating.
Based on the above, we novelly exploited the advantages of plasma spraying technology which can easily deposit thick coatings, and the pack cementation technology which can convert refractory metals into corresponding silicides. Firstly, the plasma spraying technology was used to prepare a ZrB2-Mo UHTC/refractory metal composite coating with a gradient structure on the Ta-10 W substrate. Then, the pack cementation technology was used to convert Mo into MoSi2 and part of ZrB2 into ZrSi2. Finally, a dense, thick and multilayered ZrB2-ZrSi2MoSi2 UHTC composite coating was successfully prepared. The microstructure and mechanical properties were evaluated. More importantly, the ablation behavior and mechanism of the dense and multilayered coating was investigated through plasma ablation tests under varying heat flux densities.

In this study, we systematically incorporated metals from Groups V and VI ( M = V, Nb, Ta, Cr, Mo, W), while maintaining Group IV metals (Hf,  Ti,  Zr)  constant  in  four  element  carbide-boride  ceramics.  This resulted in six multicomponent ( M ,Hf,Ti,Zr)B2-( M ,Hf,Ti,Zr)C compositions that are designated HEDP-V, HEDP-Nb, HEDP-Ta, HEDP-Cr, HEDPMo and HEDP-W. The findings of this work contribute to a deeper understanding  of  the  influence  of  different  metals  and  their  thermodynamics aspects, carbon vacancies, size factor, phase stability, microstructure  evolution  and  mechanical  performance  -  specifically hardness - of dual-phase UHTCs.

4.1. Carbon continuous fiber reinforcement
One study by Hu et al. [187] commented on the mechanical properties as a function of ZrB2 content in the carbon-reinforced SiC matrix, indicating that the composite ' s flexural strength and fracture toughness decreased with increasing amounts of ZrB2. According to the report, the respective values of strength and toughness dropped from 367 MPa to 23.6 MPa m ½ without any boride to 163 MPa and 8.1 MPa m ½ when the matrix contained 60 vol% ZrB2. Their reasoning was that the boride phase  tended  to  react  at  the  fiber-matrix  interface,  causing  the  mechanical properties of the composite to degrade. Similar trends were observed in later studies involving carbide UHTCs. Chen et al. [191] investigated the effects of adding TaC to a Cf/SiC composite, while Yan et  al.  did  the  same  for  ZrC  [193]  and  HfC  [194].  In  all  three  cases, flexural strength decreased with increasing UHTC volume fraction, and the fracture toughness of the Cf/SiC -UHTC composites was lower than the Cf/SiC baseline. These results indicated a need for process refinement, given the preference for higher volume fractions of the UHTC phase due to superior oxidation resistance.

The application of ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) coatings is  an  effective  way  to  improve  the  in-service  performance  of  CMC structures  at  higher  operating  temperatures  and  for  longer  duration without  dramatically  hampering  the  overall  weight  of  the  structure. Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) is the preferred compound, because of its excellent combination of high melting temperature, high thermal conductivity (40 -100 W/m K) [9 -14], and superior ablation [15 -18] and erosion resistance [19,20] . Particularly, ceramics in the ZrB2 - WC - SiC system have shown to possesses outstanding strength retention up to 2100 ◦ C [21] and high thermal erosion resistance at 2600 -2800 ◦ C [22, 23]. As compared to pure ZrB2, these properties seem to be related first to  a  better  oxidation  resistance  of  the  (Zr,W)B2  solid  solution grown around the original ZrB2  cores upon sintering compared to pure ZrB2 [24,25], coupled with the formation of an outermost more protective oxide scale, where possible stabilization of B2O3 glass by W occurs [26], and with the formation of a more dense subscale, where ZrO2  grains encapsulate W solid products, as a consequence of the (Zr,W)B2  solid solution oxidation. 4.5. Hardness
For HEC, the measured nanohardness values (obtained from nanoindentation) are significantly greater than the measured microhardness values (30-40 vs. 15-30 GPa). Our measured Vickers hardness (25.3 ± 1.4 GPa at 9.8 N and 26.8 ± 1.6 GPa at 1.96 N) is similar to that presented by Ye et al. [37] (18.8 ± 0.4 GPa at 9.8 N and 22.5 ± 0.3 GPa at 0.98 N). In addition, a lower value of 15 GPa at 9.8 N indent loading force was reported in Ref. [23]. Besides the different indent loading forces applied in different studies, the relatively low Vickers hardness can also be attributed to their low relative densities (95.3 % and 93 %, respectively, in comparison with the high relative density of ∼99 % in this study).
It has already been shown by several independent studies that the single-phase HEB [21,37,62] or HEC [22,24] is harder than the rule-ofmixture (RoM) average of those of the five individual components (binary borides or carbides). A new observation in this study is that the Vickers hardness of a dual-phase UHTC is further enhanced above the linear interpolation of two single-phase endmembers (HEB and HEC), which is indicated by the blue dash line in Fig. 9; on the other hand, hardness data taken at indent loading force of 1.96 N in Fig. S5 also confirms this phenomenon. A commentary here is that the grains in DPHE-UHTCs (4.2-11.9 μm in size) are substantially smaller than those of single-phase HEB and HEC counterparts (with size of 15.0 μm and 12.1 μm, respectively); with higher hardness being reported on finer grain ceramics [75,76], the actual mechanism of this enhancement on Vickers hardness for DPHE-UHTCs would require further study.

question: How do the processing routes differ between the slurry brushing-sintering technique used for the ZrB2-based UHTC layer by Hu et al. (2019) and the spark plasma sintering (SPS) process used for the HEDP dual-phase ceramics by Feltrin et al. (2025)?
answer:
The provided sources do not contain information regarding the HEDP dual-phase ceramics by Feltrin et al. (2025). However, the slurry brushing-sintering technique used by Hu et al. (2019) involves applying a ZrB2-based UHTC layer through a slurry brushing method followed by sintering [8]. This technique includes steps such as mixing powders, brushing the slurry onto a substrate, drying, and heat treatment in a controlled atmosphere to achieve a dense UHTC layer [8]. 

In contrast, spark plasma sintering (SPS) is a well-established technique for consolidating ceramics rapidly, involving the application of a pulsed electric current through a graphite die to achieve high heating rates and dense microstructures [1][3]. While the specific details of the SPS process for HEDP dual-phase ceramics by Feltrin et al. (2025) are not available in the provided sources, SPS is generally characterized by its ability to achieve high relative densities with minimal processing time and lower sintering temperatures compared to conventional methods [1][3]. 

Thus, the processing routes differ in the application of slurry brushing-sintering versus SPS, with the former involving a slurry application and heat treatment process, and the latter utilizing a pulsed electric current for rapid sintering [8][1].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) is the predominant sintering technique for  achieving  dense  entropy-stabilized  UHTC.  The  first  high-entropy diboride  (HEB)  was  successfully  produced  using  SPS  at  2000 ◦ C  for 5 min, using transition metal diboride powders subjected to high-energy ball milling as starting materials [23]. Gild et al. [24] introduced a new approach named reactive flash Spark Plasma Sintering (reaFSPS), which combines rapid SPS and the flash sintering technique to quickly densify high-entropy UHTC. The utilization of reaFSPS enabled the achievement of  dense  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)B2 and  (Ti0.2Zr0.2Hf0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2)C ceramics,  which  contained  3  wt%  graphite  sintered  in  120  s.  While advanced sintering techniques can successfully yield dense high-entropy UHTC,  conventional  methods  often  involve  sintering  temperatures exceeding 1800 ◦ C,  leading  to  increased  energy  consumption and the development of a coarse microstructure [9].
Ultra-fast high-temperature sintering (UHS) was developed in 2020 by Wang et al. [25], where the sintering of a green body was achieved within seconds by placing a pellet between two Joule-heated carbon felts,  with  an  ultra-high  heating  rate  of  10 3 -10 4 ◦ C/min  achieved without the aid of external pressure. UHS has clear advantages over SPS, including a reduced processing time, ' light heating elements ' with low thermal capacity, simple experimental equipment, and the possibility of obtaining components with complex geometries [25 -27]. Due to the use of  thermally  insulating  graphite  heating  elements,  the  temperature within the felt can exceed 2500 ◦ C, thus enabling the densification of different refractory compounds  including SiC [28], Si3N4 [29], ZrB2-based  materials  [30,31],  WC  [32],  ZrC  [33] and  high-entropy carbides [34].

G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T
In the present work, the possibility to achieve nearly fully dense UHTCMCs by the rapid SPS technique, while preserving the /uniFB01 bres integrity, was investigated. Spark plasma sintering is a well-established consolidation technique that can pomote near full densi /uniFB01 cation of ceramics in short processing times of the order of minutes. This is especially true for conductive ceramics, such as borides and carbides, where the electrical currents have been demonstrated to signi /uniFB01 cantly enhance densi /uniFB01 cation by eliminating oxide phases like MxOy and B2O3 [27]. The inherent advantages of SPS processing are the rapid heating rate, which is up to 1000 °C/min in the case of rapid SPS [28]; [29]; [30] and up to 10,000 °C/min for Flash SPS technique [31]. Such high heating rates, besides contributing to a signi /uniFB01 cant energy saving during the consolidation process, also enable the possibility to produce metastable materials, which would not be possible to achieve using other sintering routes.
The production and microstructure evolution of ZrB2 + 8 vol% Si3N4 + 3 vol% SiC, materials reinforced with 45 vol% of high modulus pitch based chopped /uniFB01 bres is presented and correlated to the sintering temperature and time.

Spark Plasma Sintering
Spark plasma sintering (SPS) is a processing technique in which ceramic powders can be sintered very rapidly to full density. It is similar to HP and is carried out in  a  graphite  die/punch  assembly.  High-energy  plasma  can  be  generated  by  an instantaneous pulsed direct current applied through electrodes at the top and bottom of the die punch. Such plasma is expected to bring about phenomena that can enhance densification by particle surface activation, local melting and evaporation on the surface of the powder particle, so that heat transfer and mass transfer can be completed rapidly. In nearly all the investigations on SPS/field assisted sintering (FAS) indicated the higher rate of heating, lower sintering temperatures and shorter dwelling times compared to those attained in conventional sintering techniques (pressureless  sintering,  hot  pressing  etc).  Rapid  heating  rates  (hundreds  of  °C/ min) minimize coarsening and facilitate rapid densification, which leads to better thermo-mechanical properties. SPS has recently been applied to densify UHTCs based on ZrB 2 - and HfB 2 - with SiC/MoSi 2 /ZrC as secondary phases. Additions of 3wt% lanthanum yielded 99% RD (Kim et al., 2003). Mechanical alloyed Zr-B-C powder mixture resulted in 98% RD ZrB 2 -ZrC composite after exposure at 1800°C for 5 min (Tsuchida et al., 2004). Fast densification of HfB 2 and ZrB 2 based powder mixtures resulted in ~100% RD composites at 2100°C for 2 min (Bellosi et al . 2006) with a reduced grain size (compared to pressureless sintering) of ~1.4-2μm. Results of SPS studies on ZrB 2 - and HfB 2 - based ceramics are summarized in Table 4.

A B S T R A C T
The processing routes for fabricating HE-UHTCs, reported so far in literature are hot pressing (HP) [39,43,44,49,56-58], arc melting [26], spark plasma sintering (SPS) [7,13,18,22-25,28,33-38,45,59-61], free pressure-less  spark  plasma  sintering  (FP-SPS)  [51],  reactive  spark plasma  sintering  (R-SPS)  [30,50],  and  reactive  flash  spark  plasma sintering (RF-SPS) [17]. The first-ever report on HE-borides (Hf, Zr, Ta, M, Ti;  where  M  =  Nb,  Cr,  and  Mo)B2 as  a  new  class  of  UHTC  was published  in  2016  [13]  while  on  HE-carbides  (Hf,  Ta,  Zr,  M;  where M = Ti and Nb)C in 2018 [33] via the combinational approach of ballmilling and SPS of procured powders. An emergence of this new field can be seen from the jump in the increased number of publications in the last four years, as shown in Fig. 1.

Production of UHTC Complex Shapes and Architectures
As a matter of fact the most straightforward method to produce high strength dense UHTCs is through the use of pressure assisted techniques ,  most often in combination with sintering additives to reduce the processing temperature as much as possible (1900°C or lower).
Hot pressing (HP) is historically the most common pressure assisted sintering technique for advanced ceramics. ZrB 2 ceramics and ZrB 2 based composites with final density in the range 70-100% have been realized by HP using different starting particle size, additives and sintering temperature in the range 1600-2000 °C, sintering times from 5 to 120 min and pressures from 20 to 60 MPa (Guo, 2009).
Recently, spark plasma sintering (SPS) has  become a  routine  technique  to  densify advanced ceramics. A pulsed electric direct current is applied contemporary to a unidirectional mechanical pressure in a graphite die. Heating up is mainly due to the Joule effect when electrically conductive materials are processed. The effectiveness of SPS in the densification of refractory borides is highlighted by the processing time, which is 10 times lower than for HP (Medri et al., 2005), and allows to obtain final relative density higher than 99% without the use of sintering aids (Bellosi et al., 2011). HfB 2 based materials processed by SPS (Bellosi et al., 2006; Sciti et al., 2008) retained their flexural strength up to 1500°C in air, with values over 800 MPa, thanks to absence of a thermally unstable grain boundary phase deriving from sintering aids. ZrB 2 and/or HfB 2 compacts with final density of 80-100% have

3.2.  Brushing method
attracted  considerable  research  attention  due  to  the  advantages  including operational simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and minimal equipment investment [60]. Ji et al. [61] improved the density of ZrB2-SiC coatings prepared using the slurry method by incorporating expandable anatase materials during the sintering process. The coating exhibited excellent  interfacial  bonding  strength  (19.2 MPa)  and  demonstrated weight changes of 1.7 %, -0.6 %, and -1.7 % after 40 h of oxidation at 800 °C,  1000 °C,  and  1200 °C,  respectively.  Li  et  al.  [62] developed TaSi2-MoSi2-ZrB2-borosilicate  glass  coating  on  C/SiCO  nanoporous ceramic  composites  through  combined  slurry  brushing  and  pack  cementation. With an increasing number of brushing cycles, the ablation rate  of  the  coatings  gradually  decreased  after  200 s  of  ablation  at 1600 °C. After 24 brush cycles, the mass ablation rate and linear ablation rate of the coating were reduced to 2.5 × 10 -4 g/s and 1.3 × 10 -4 mm/s,  respectively.  Duan  et  al.  [63] employed  the  slurry  brushing method to prepare ZrB2-SiC coatings on a graphite matrix surface (as shown in Fig. 11), examining the effects of ZrB2  content and binder concentration on the static oxidation behavior of ZrB2-SiC coating at 1200 °C. An increase in ZrB2  content from 30 wt% to 45 wt% led to a decrease in weight loss rate of the coating samples from -0.92 % to -1.67 %. after 120 min of oxidation at 1200 °C. Ren et al. [64] applied the slurry brushing method to prepare La2O3-modified ZrB2-SiC coating on  a  graphite  matrix  coated  with  a  SiC  layer.  The  ZrB2-SiC-La2O3 coating  exhibited  improved  oxidation  resistance  compared  to

2.2. Slurry preparation, in /uniFB01 ltration, and thermal treatment
The ZrB2 powder was mixed with 5 vol% of B4C (H.C. Starck, Grade HS, Germany), and dispersed in aqueous solution with a polyacrylate (PEI, Sigma-Aldrich50% (m/v) in H2O) by ball-milling in a polyethylene bottle with SiC balls for 3 h. The UHTCMCs were fabricated by impregnating the coated unidirectional fabrics with the slurry, and subsequently stacking 8 layers in a unidirectional con /uniFB01 guration. Vacuum-bagging in a furnace at 90 °C for 1 h, was used to consolidate the green composite. Sintering was conducted in a hot uniaxial pressing at 40 MPa in low vacuum using an induction-heated graphite die of 30 × 30 mm. Cycles were carried out at 1900 °C for 10 min.

3. Experimental procedure
Continuous carbon /uniFB01 ber reinforced SiC matrix (C/SiC) composites were fabricated by electromagnetic-coupling CVI (E-CVI) [34,35]. The composites were machined to a size of 20 mm × 20 mm × 7 mm and polished by 120 and 400 grit diamond grinding tools in return for using as substrates. Low-temperature CVD (LTCVD), slurry brushing with a novel sintering technique and high-temperature CVD (HTCVD) were successively applied for the preparation of the SiC/UHTC/SiC coating. Firstly, a SiC bonding layer was applied on the C/SiC substrates by LTCVD at a relatively low temperature of 1050 -1150 °C for 48 h through the pyrolysis of methyltrichlorosilane (CH3SiCl3, MTS). Subsequently, a ZrB2-based UHTC middle layer was applied on the specimens by slurry brushing-sintering technique. Commercially available ZrB2 ( ∼ 5 μ m), SiC ( ∼ 2 μ m), SiO2 ( ∼ 5 μ m), B2O3 (500 mesh) and Al2O3 (300 mesh) powders were weighed according to a desired mass ratio of 16: 2: 2: 2: 1 and then mixed by ball milling for 12 h. The powders were mixed with a caking agent and then directly brushed on the SiC-coated C/SiC. After drying at 120 °C, the specimens were heat treated at 900 °C for 2 h in an electric furnace. A gaseous mixture of 70 vol.% Ar and
30 vol.% air was injected to achieve a slight oxidizing atmosphere in order to prepare a dense UHTC layer. Finally, a SiC outer layer was deposited by HTCVD at a relatively high temperature of 1200 -1250 °C for two 24 h.

A B S T R A C T
A process technology for the processing of the continuous carbon fibre reinforced ZrB2  (Cf -ZrB2) ultra high temperature ceramic matrix composite (UHTCMC) has been developed. The processing steps are slurry infiltration followed by curing and pyrolysis. The composite was then subjected to high temperature pressureless sintering.  The  microstrcutural  and  mechanical  properties  of  Cf -ZrB2  UHTCMC  were  characterized  at  the completion of each processing step. The de-lamination between the carbon fibre and the ZrB2 matrix layer in 2D Cf -ZrB2 composite after pressureless sintering was found. However, the problem of de-lamination after sintering was not detected in the 2.5D Cf -ZrB2 composite by stitching the infiltrated layers in the Z-direction. Significant improvements in the sintered density, Young ' s modulus and flexural strength were achieved through 2.5D Cf -ZrB2  composite.

4. Conclusions
2D and 2.5D Cf  - ZrB2  composites are successfully fabricated using pressureless sintering based process technology. The process consists of slurry  infiltration,  vacuum  bagging,  curing  and  pyrolysis  before  the pressureless  sintering.  After  pyrolysis,  a  porous  Cf -  ZrB2 ultra  high temperature  ceramic  composite  formed  which  was  densified  during sintering. De-lamination between carbon fibre and ZrB2  matrix layers was significantly reduced in the 2.5D Cf - ZrB2 composite. As a result, the 2.5D Cf  - ZrB2  composite (Flexural strengrth = 63 ± 8 MPa & Young ' s modulus = 49 GPa) showed superior mechanical performance to the 2D composite (Flexural strengrth = 20 ± 5 MPa & Young ' s modulus = 29 GPa).

4.1. A new and novel processing route
In the current processing, each metal was only present in either boride or carbide form (but not both) in the initial mixture of five powders of binary borides or carbides (see Table 1 for the specific start mixture of powders for each case). Subsequently, the cations will be partitioned to both the HEB and HEC phases after SPS in the final specimens, presumably in a chemical equilibrium with one another. Thus, this process may be considered as a 'reactive SPS' since it involves a chemical reaction, albeit it is not a reactive sintering directly from metal and B/C elements. We should note that sintering methods involving alternative chemical reactions ( e.g. boro-carbothermal reduction of metal oxides) may also be regarded as 'reactive sintering' [60,61].
In addition to adding minor graphite, a strategy to minimize the native oxides is to adopt carbides of the metals that are more prone to oxidations, e.g. , ZrC and HfC (instead of ZrB2 and HfB2 that typically have more native oxides in the initial commercial powders), as the starting powders, whenever possible.
A key achievement of the current work is the new and novel processing route to attain great than ∼98.8 % relative densities with virtually no native oxides and minimum impurity phase. This has been achieved via utilizing 1 wt. % graphite as the reducing agent and sintering aid, along with further optimization of processing ( e.g. , incorporating a two-step prolonged pre-heating stages at 1400 °C and 1600 °C) to allow reduction and out-gassing. On top of that, holding the specimen at 2200 °C for 20 min at 80 MPa also facilitates the densification process.

4.1. A new and novel processing route
The total amount of porosity plus impurity phases (mostly remaining graphite and possibly B4C) in the final sintered specimens is less than 1.5 vol. % for all specimens, as confirmed independently by digital image processing. This novel processing itself is an advancement from the state of the art of sintering high-entropy UHTCs, particularly in comparison with prior studies of fabricating HEBs from commercial powders [21]. In the past, high-density HEBs had to be made by synthesized high-entropy powders [62,63] or via special method of reaFSPS [34].
It has been widely accepted that existence of native oxides would hinder densification in both boride and carbide systems [48,64,65]. In our study, 1 wt. % graphite was added as an in situ reducing agent, and powder processing was conducted in an argon atmosphere. Extra carbon in the specimens can help to keep a local reducing environment during both HEBM and SPS. Moreover, pellets were held at 1400 °C and 1600 °C for longer periods of time before the final densification. Annealing at 1400 °C in vacuum promotes removal of intrinsic metal oxides and B2O3 with the assistance of extra graphite [48]. Holding at 1600 °C, together with a low ramping rate of 30 °C /min during densification, can facilitate pore elimination before rapid grain growth [66]. The combination of these strategies in this new procedure helped us to achieve the high relative densities (with the lowest measured relative density of ∼98.8 %, and >99 % in all but one case) with virtually no native oxides observed in the final specimens. This has not been achieved previously for HEBs made from commercial powders via normal HEBM and SPS.

5. Conclusions
- A new reactive SPS route is developed to make single-phase and dual-phase high-entropy UHTCs directly from commercial powders to achieve ∼99 % or higher relative densities with virtual no native oxides via utilizing 1 wt. % graphite as a reducing agent and sintering aid, which represents a significant advancement in the processing of high-entropy UHTCs (particularly high-entropy borides that have been proven difficult to densify). Noticeably, 3-5 % W contamination from HEBM is observed in all sintered specimens.
- We discovered a thermodynamic relation that dictates the compositions of the HEB and HEC phases in equilibrium and drives them away from equimolar composition (despite the nearly equimolar composition overall for the DPHE-UHTCs), which is important and essential to design dual-phase high-entropy UHTCs. In general, the metal cation fractions in the two high-entropy phases in any dualphase high-entropy ceramics (DPHEC) should be different; the two high-entropy solid-solution phases are likely in an equilibrium with each other, where a thermodynamic relation exists and governs the (generally non-equal) partition of each of the metal elements.
- The grain sizes and properties of dual-phase high-entropy UHTCs can be tailored by changing phase fraction and microstructure.
- The hardness of the dual-phase high-entropy UHTCs are improved from the weighted linear average of the two single-phase high-entropy UHTCs, which are already harder than the RoM averages of individual binary carbides and borides.
- The Young's moduli of high-entropy UHTCs, which are measured accurately from acoustic waves, are higher than the theoretical RoM predictions.
