2.1. Materials and process
Commercially attainable ZrB2, SiC and ZrC powders as well as short carbon /uniFB01 bers were purchased as starting materials. The speci /uniFB01 cations of these materials, including purity, particle size and suppliers, are reported in Table 2 and their morphologies are displayed in Fig. 1.
Five composite samples, with di /uniFB00 erent compositions, were prepared using mixtures of the above-mentioned starting materials. Based on the literature on ZrB2 -SiC composites, in order to get an acceptable combination of mechanical properties, the volume ratio of ZrB2:SiC powders were chosen 4:1 in all samples. The composite mixtures were arranged by the addition of 0 -10 vol% ZrC and 0 -5 vol% Cf as reinforcements to the ZrB2 -SiC baseline with a constant volume ratio of 2:1 for ZrC:Cf. The details of composition of samples are presented in Fig. 2 and Table 3.
The dry-mixing process was carried out using a low-energy ballmilling (200 rpm) with a ball to powder ratio of 5:1 in an argon atmosphere for 2 h. It's worth noting that the soft carbon /uniFB01 bers were not added to the cup during the milling process. They were initially dispersed in acetone-ethanol solution by ultrasonication and then the dry mixture was added to the slurry. Beaker of mixed starting materials slurry was located on heating magnetic stirrer under a laboratory fume hood for drying the slurry. Each of the prepared mixtures was loaded into a graphite die with an inner diameter of 30 mm, while being and covered by /uniFB02 exible graphite foils. The composite samples were fabricated by vacuum spark plasma sintering at 1800 °C temperature for 5 min dwelling time under 30 MPa pressure using a DC current rate of 400 A/min up to a /uniFB01 nal current of 2700 A. The thickness of such sintered disk-shaped samples was ~6 mm.

2.1. Sintering procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ = 10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ = 3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions. ZrB2, HfB2 and 𝛂 -SiC (6H) have a hexagonal structure with lattice parameters of a = 3.17 ˚ A, c = 3.53 ˚ A, a = 3.139 ˚ A, c = 3.473 ˚ A and a = 3.08 ˚ A, c = 15.12 ˚ A, respectively. Powders with the following compositions were prepared, ZrB2, ZrB2+ 20 vol.% SiC (hereafter termed ZS20), ZrB2+ 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (hereafter termed ZS20La), HfB2, HfB2+ 20 vol.% SiC (hereafter termed HS20), HfB2+ 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (hereafter termed HS20La). They were wet ball milled in plastic containers using ZrO2 balls in ethanol for nearly 24 h and dried using a rotary evaporator under vacuum. Then, they were sintered in a spark plasma sintering (SPS) furnace (Type HP D 25, FCT systeme GmbH, Rauenstein, Germany). A 40 mm graphite die was used and covered with graphite felt to reduce heat loss, the temperature was monitored and controlled by an optical pyrometer.

Self-Propagating High-Temperature Synthesis (SHS) and Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS)
Figure 1. Schematic representation of the processing route based on the combination of the SHS and SPS techniques for the fabrication of bulk UHTCs
Finally, the possible utilization of such processing route for the consolidation of fibers or whiskers reinforced UHTCs will be discussed.

A B S T R A C T
Fig. 1. (a) Comparison of the XRD pattern of the raw powders, ball-milled powder with SPS sintered sample showing the formation of solid-solution in SPS sintered (TaNbHfTi)C sample referred to as MC-UHTC; and (b) SEM and EDS area analysis of the SPS sintered MC-UHTC. The composition of MC-UHTC is (TaNbHfTi)C.

Self-Propagating High-Temperature Synthesis (SHS) and Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS)
UHTCs indicate that SHS powders possess improved sintering ability in comparison with analogous materials produced using alternative preparation routes.
This statement is well supported at least by three relevant experimental findings. Indeed, ZrB 2 powders prepared by combustion synthesis exhibited higher sinterability as compared to the same compound synthesized by the carbothermic reduction process (Mishra et al., 2004). The same conclusion can be made when consolidating by SPS powders obtained by SHS or relatively finer commercial powders with ZZS composition, under the same conditions (Licheri et al., 2008). Indeed, relatively denser samples are correspondingly obtained in the first case, i.e. about 99% dense instead of 91%. Moreover, relatively milder sintering temperatures (1800 °C) were required to obtain fully dense ZrB 2 -SiC composites when using SHS powders as compared to the case of reactive SPS (1900 °C), where synthesis and densification were carried out in a single processing step (Licheri et al., 2007).
The beneficial effects obtained when taking advantage of the SPS technology can be clearly associated to the direct passage of the electric pulsed current through the sintering powders and the die containing them. This feature produces very high heating  rates,  so  that  significantly  shorter  times  and,  generally,  lower  sintering temperatures are required with respect to traditional HP.
The possible future exploitation of the SHS and SPS processing routes is related to the fabrication of new whiskers- or fibers-reinforced UHTCs systems.

4. Effects on fibre reinforcement and mechanical performance
Galizia  et  al.  [134]  compared  the  mechanical  performance  of RMI-prepared UHTC-CMCs to other processing methods, as shown in Fig.  7c,  and  slurry-infiltration  (SI)  used  as  a  pre-RMI  densification method. Although polymer infiltration and pyrolysis, chemical vapour infiltration and reactive gaseous infiltration are considered competing techniques to RMI, they can be utilised as pre-RMI densification processes as well. Thus, the advantages of RMI are better leveraged when combined  with  PIP  [135],  CVI  [136],  SI  [134]  and  sol-gel  [137]. Although PIP, CVI and room temperature SI processes do not damage fibres; PIP and SI produce relatively low-density composites than RMI

(b) Bending strength and flexural modulus of various UHTC-CMCs prepared by RMI [30,39,65,74,100,106,126,131,134,136,139 -147](c)  Comparison  of  flexural strength and specific flexural strength graphs of UHTC composites prepared by other methods (HP = hot pressing, SI = slurry impregnation, SPS = spark plasma sintering, CVI = chemical vapour infiltration, RF = radio frequency, and RMI = reactive melt infiltration) [134].

gaps: processing routes differ between the slurry brushing-sintering technique used for the ZrB2-based UHTC layer by Hu et al. (2019) and the spark plasma sintering (SPS) process used for the HEDP dual-phase ceramics by Feltrin et al. (2025)

question: How do the peak flexural strength values of Demirskyi et al. (2017) TiB2–boron ceramics compare with the microwave dielectric performance targets of Gao et al. (2025) CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramics, in terms of what each study identifies as its record-setting metric?
answer:
The peak flexural strength values of Demirskyi et al. (2017) TiB2–boron ceramics reached 910 MPa at room temperature and 1105 MPa at 1600 °C, which is the highest strength reported for non-oxide ceramics at 1600 °C [8]. However, the specific peak flexural strength values of these ceramics are not explicitly detailed in the provided sources.

Regarding the microwave dielectric performance targets of Gao et al. (2025) CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramics, the study reports a Q × f value of 27,375 GHz, which is the highest Q × f value among the glass-ceramics evaluated in their work [10]. This represents the record-setting metric identified by Gao et al. (2025).

The comparison between the two studies' record-setting metrics is not directly provided in the sources, as the flexural strength data for Demirskyi et al. (2017) and the microwave dielectric performance targets for Gao et al. (2025) pertain to different material properties and application domains.

3. Results and discussion
TiB2-boron ceramics show good flexural strength at room temperature ( 𝛔 25 ◦ C) ranging from 740 to 910 MPa, with a mean value among six tested specimens of 820 MPa, (Fig. 1) [3-6,12-14,21,22]. Fig. 1 shows data for three-point (open figures) and four-point (closed figures) configurations obtained for TiB2, B4C and TiB2based composites at room and elevated temperatures. It is known that the difference in configuration for threeand four-point methods does not allow comparing them directly. In this respect data for four-point method presented in Fig. 1 show general trends observed in original studies. Furthermore, in order to verify high values obtained by the three-point method, we also conducted some reference tests using the four-point method (using a 20/10 mm span of the supports and specimens with size 2 × 2.5 × 26 mm) using four specimens. The mean value obtained was 829 ± 17 MPa. These data points are not presented in Fig. 1. When comparing two testing methods, we should note that, since only six samples were tested using the three-point set-up, it is clear that the difference in strength can be treated as minor, since it is well within the measurement error. Further large-scale testing is suggested to confirm these findings.
Fig. 1. Temperature dependence of strength for TiB2-boron ceramic composites consolidated by SPS and data on high-temperature flexural behavior of TiB2 [3-6], B4C [21,22] and TiB2-B4C [12-14] ceramics. Closed figures indicate that four point flexural strength test was performed, while open figures are for three-point strength tests.
Nevertheless, among TiB2-based ceramics the results of the present study show one of the best values reported so far. Only Huang et al. [13] for TiB2-B4C (60/40 vol %) showed higher mean value of 867 MPa at room temperature using three-point configuration.

frequencies, the ε r and tan δ of glass-ceramics exhibit a dramatic instability, which may be related to the polarization disorder caused by unstable  modifier  ions  in  the  glass  phase.  The ε r of  all  glass-ceramics decreases  with  increasing  test  frequency,  due  to  partial  polarization behavior failure (such as space charge polarization and relaxation polarization) at higher frequencies. At 10 GHz, the ε r ranges from 6.061 to 6.425, and the tan δ stabilizes within the range of 6.9 × 10 GLYPH<0> 4 -1.0 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 . As the CuO content increases, the variation trend in the ε r and tan δ at 10 GHz  is  consistent  with  that  in  the  Hakki-Coleman  method.  Demonstrating the stability of the dielectric properties of CZB glass-ceramics at high frequencies.
In the currently reported glass-ceramics with potential for ULTCC applications [9,13,50 -59], the novel CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass-ceramic prepared in this work exhibits the highest Q × f value and extremely low sintering temperature, as shown in Table 3. Trace CuO doping has been confirmed to cause changes in boron ion coordination and superstructural units in borate glasses. The variation of short-range and medium-range in glass network structure effectively affects the  crystallization behavior of glass-cramics [60]. The chemical bond properties, lattice structure, phase composition, and microstructure of glass-cramics  have  been  significantly  optimized.  Trace  CuO  doping resulted in a nearly 40 % increase in the Q × f value, indicating a huge potential for  improvement  in  microwave  dielectric  properties  of glass-cramics.  Glass-cramics  materials  have  great  development  prospects in the field of microwave dielectrics.

The ε r of CZB0, CZB1, CZB2, CZB3, and CZB4 glass-ceramics is 6.05, 5.96, 6.10, 6.25, and 6.31, respectively, showing a limited variation. This is because the trace CuO doping did not cause significant differences in the densification degree of glass-ceramics (with a maximum relative density difference of 4 %) and the difference in ε r between the main crystal phases of these glass-ceramics is also very small (Zn3B2O6  of 6.7, and Zn4B6O13 of 6.9) [37,38]. In detail, the lowest ε r of CZB1 glass-ceramic is related  to  its  microstructure  without  granular  crystals.  The  granular crystals in clusters will produce more grain boundaries, and the polarization behavior at grain boundaries will be more diverse, which will increase the ε r of the material [39]. With further increasing the CuO content, the number of granular crystals in the glass-ceramics increases and the particle size decreases, creating more grain boundary positions and thus increasing the ε r .  Moreover, the ionic polarizability of Zn 2 + cations and Cu 2 + cations is 2.04 Å 3 and 2.11 Å 3 , respectively [40]. The increase in Cu 2 + ion content with higher polarizability also contributes to the increase of ε r .
Compared to ε r , the Q × f value of glass-ceramics varied significantly with the trace CuO doping. The Q × f value of CZB0 ~ CZB4 is 19,775, 18,293, 16,873, 25,445, and 27,375 GHz, respectively. Multiple factors, including intrinsic and extrinsic factors, affect the Q × f value of glassceramics. 1. Introduction
Titanium diboride (TiB2) is an important ceramic material which is extensively used as a cutting tool material, wear components and lightweight armour for ballistic protection. High elastic modulus and good strength at room temperature is often reported for monolithic TiB2 and TiB2-based ceramic composites [1-6]. In view of its refractory nature, high temperature applications are also within reach. In the latter case the significant decrease of strength after 1400 ◦ C is expected due to activation of the high-temperature plastic deformation mechanisms such as grain-boundary sliding and creep [6].
An obvious solution to this problem is the consolidation of composites [6-14]. TiB2-TaC composites have demonstrated a flexural strength of 480 MPa at 1600 ◦ C, which is twice of that for monolithic TiB2 ceramics [6]. It was summarized that even in TiB2-TaC case a decrease in strength is expected at higher temperatures owing to electro-covalent nature of bonding in titanium diboride. Therefore in order to reach higher strength values TiB2-based ceramics armored with covalent compounds is proposed.

8.3. High temperature strength
The effects of temperature on the flexural strength of various di-borides with or without sinter-additives addition are shown in Fig. 18. A general observation is that ZrB2 and HfB2 ceramics exhibit better strength properties. More importantly, it is to be noted here that monolithic TiB2 and also TiB2 with small amount of sinter-additive show increasing strength with temperature (up to 1000 °C) (See Fig. 18a) [45,100,328,329]. Though the exact reasoning is still under debate, it is possible that the residual stress gets relieved as a result of temperature increase during the elevated temperature tests. This, in turn increased the grain boundary strength and hence the flexural strength of the di-boride-based ceramic/composites. However, upon incorporation of larger amount of sinteradditive (i.e. 10 wt% MoSi2) to TiB2, the strength decreased considerably at 1000 °C. As shown in Fig. 19, it is evident that with the increase in sinter-additive content and test temperature, the mode of fracture for TiB2 changed from predominantly transgranular to intergranular. Additionally, an increased fraction of grain pull-outs is noticeable for TiB2-10 wt% MoSi2 when compared to the other TiB2 ceramics. Demirskyi et al. [171] reported high flexural strength of 910 MPa at room temperature and, interestingly, further increase to 1105 MPa with temperature up to 1600 °C. Nevertheless, the strength decreased rapidly to 620 MPa with further increase in temperature to 1800 °C for TiB2-10 wt% B (developed via reactive SPS). It was proposed that the presence of amorphous boron improved the strength to fracture, in addition to plastic deformation and stress relief of material at high temperature. In a recent work, Demirskyi et al. [211] revealed that the strength of SPS processed TiB2 could be improved with the addition of NbB2 (up to 50 wt%) even up to 1800 °C in Ar environment.

a b s t r a c t
Spark plasma sintering of TiB2-boron ceramics using commercially available raw powders is reported. The B4C phase developed during reaction-driven consolidation at 1900 ◦ C. The newly formed grains were located at the grain junctions and the triple point of TiB2 grains, forming a covalent and stiff skeleton of B4C. The flexural strength of the TiB2-10 wt.% boron ceramic composites reached 910 MPa at room temperature and 1105 MPa at 1600 ◦ C. Which is the highest strength reported for non-oxide ceramics at 1600 ◦ C. This was followed by a rapid decrease at 1800 ◦ C to 480-620 MPa, which was confirmed by increased number of cavitated titanium diboride grains observed after flexural strength tests.
© 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
This class of TiB2-based composites is naturally restricted to TiB2-AlN [8,9], TiB2-SiC [10,11] and B4C-TiB2 [12-17] ceramic composites, which show high room-temperature strength and toughness. High-temperature performance of these TiB2-based composite is scarcely reported and is limited to the B4C-TiB2 eutectic composites [14-17].
The B4C-TiB2 eutectic composites are known to have a complex preparation route, and have a certain size limitation. Therefore, it was an objective of this study to propose a simple route for manufacturing TiB2-B4C composites, that is by the reaction spark plasma sintering [18-20] using commercially available powders of TiB2 and amorphous boron. A reactive consolidation approach was used in order to ensure homogeneous distribution of secondary phase, which is formed in situ during spark plasma sintering. In the framework of this study, characterization of high-temperature mechanical properties was performed using the three-point flexural strength tests at temperatures between 25 ◦ C and 1800 ◦ C.

3. Results and discussion
There are two possible reasons for such high values: (i) a complex composite structure with uniform distribution of newly formed secondary boron carbide phase (Fig. 2); and (ii) interfacial microcracking [23] as result of large difference in the linear thermal expansion between TiB2 matrix and B4C inclusion. Finally, (iii) small value of the grain size, which corresponds to the initial particle size of raw TiB2 powder (i.e. 2-5 𝛍 m). Recent analysis in [6] showed that strength of monolithic titanium diboride ceramic have a peak in strength for a grain size between 3 and 5 𝛍 m. This shows a good correlation with data obtained in the present study.
In case of composites, there is no obvious interdependence between the TiB2 grain size in TiB2-B4C ceramic composites, since in the majority of these ceramic composites titanium diboride was added as the reinforcement phase, while in the present study it serves as a matrix. One noticeable observation is that microcracking [23] was observed after room temperature and in part at 600 ◦ C flexural strength test.
This situation may be attributed to the residual stresses formed during the cooling process. In case of TiB2-B4C system consolidated at temperature of 1900 ◦ C, the mismatch in the coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE) between TiB2 and B4C will result in formation of the residual stresses in the bulk ceramic composite [23,24]. Using the Taya's [24] model and data of [12] and temperature difference of 1600 ◦ K one may evaluate that residual stresses on the diboride matrix 𝛔 m and boron carbide 𝛔 i are 348 MPa and -1165 MPa, respectively. Here negative value of the residual stress indicates compression, while positive value suggests tension.

a b s t r a c t
Ultra-high temperature transition-metal ceramics are potential candidates for fusion reactor structural/ plasma-facing components. We reveal the irradiation damage microstructural phenomena in Boron-11 enriched titanium diboride (TiB2) using mixed-spectrum neutron irradiations, combined with state-ofart characterization using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM). Irradiations were performed using High Flux Isotope Reactor at ~220 and 620 /C14 C up to 2.4 /C2 10 25 n.m /C0 2 (E > 0.1 MeV). Total dose including contribution from residual Boron-10 ( 10 B) transmutation recoils, was ~4.2 displacements per atom. TiB2 is susceptible to irradiation damage in terms of dislocation loop formation, cavities and anisotropic lattice parameter swelling induced micro-cracking. At both 220 and 620 /C14 C, TEM revealed dislocation loops on basal and prism planes, with nearly two orders of magnitude higher number density of prism-plane loops. HRTEM, electron diffraction and relrod imaging revealed additional defects on f 1010 g prism planes, identi /uniFB01 ed as faulted dislocation loops. High defect cluster density on prism planes explains anisotropic a-lattice parameter swelling of TiB2 reported in literature which caused grain boundary micro-cracking, the extent of which decreased with increasing irradiation temperature. Dominance of irradiation-induced defect clusters on prism planes in TiB2 is different than typical hexagonal ceramics where dislocation loops predominantly form on basal planes causing c-lattice parameter swelling, thereby revealing a potential role of c/a ratio on defect formation/aggregation. Helium generation and temperature rise from residual 10 B transmutation caused matrix and grain boundary cavities for the irradiation at 620 /C14 C. The study additionally signi /uniFB01 es isotopic enrichment as a viable approach to produce transition-metal diborides for potential nuclear structural applications.

A B S T R A C T
Oxide doping with similar chemical characteristics is an effective way to enhance the Q × f value in the research of microwave dielectric ceramics. R. Peng et al. doped 5 mol% content of CoO in Mg3B2O6 ceramics. The trace CoO doping modifies the phase composition, the band structure properties and improves the Q × f value to 81,200 GHz [10]. J. Li et al. optimum the Q × f value of Li2MgSiO4 ceramic, which is attributed to the variation of grain size and distribution resulting from the ZnO doping [11]. R. Peng and H. 4. Conclusions
An optimization method for the quality factor of glass-ceramic was developed in this work. Based on the regulation of the glass network structure  in  the  short-range  and  medium-range,  the  crystallization behavior of glass-ceramic is optimized by doping with trace amounts of CuO. 1.6mol% CuO doping resulted in the main crystalline phase of glass-ceramic complete  transformation into  Zn3B2O6 crystal.  The chemical bond characteristics and microstructure of glass-ceramic have also been optimized. This increases the Q × f value of glass-ceramic by 40 %. At an ultra-low sintering temperature of 650 ◦ C, the novel CuO-ZnOB2O3 glass-ceramic exhibits satisfactory microwave dielectric properties of ε r ~6.31, Q × f ~27,375 GHz, τ f ~ GLYPH<0> 8 ppm/ ◦ C, Vickers hardness ~599 kgf/mm 2 and good chemical compatibility with Ag or Al electrodes. This dielectric behavior optimization method provides new possibilities for the application of glass-ceramic materials in ULTCC substrates.

3. Results and discussion
A special notice should be addressed strength behavior of pure B4C [21,22,29] or B4C-TiB2 eutectic composites [14-17]. For the high-density polycrystalline bulk samples of B4C at temperatures of 900-1500 ◦ C, the flexural strength was found to be constant or decrease slightly with increasing temperature [21,28]. This increase was attributed to the increase in contribution of plastic deformation to the fracture stress above 1500 K, which could be one of the reasons of the observed increase in strength for 1400 and 1600 ◦ C, which is similar to the recent study on bulk boron carbide [22].
Furthermore, the flexural strength obtained in the present study is the highest among non-oxide ceramics at 1600 ◦ C, which suggests that further increase in strength of other metal diborides by forming a similar type of composites is anticipated. The grain size in the consolidated ceramic specimens should be identical to that for initial powder size of metal diboride. Hence the described method should result in bulk ceramic originated from metal diboride with even relatively 'coarser' particle size (up to 10 𝛍 m). Moreover, due to the even character of distribution of secondary B4C phase should be beneficial to the elastic properties as well as thermal conductivity.

question: In terms of high-temperature protective performance, how does the reduction of Ta or Nb content in Li et al. 2025's (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C ablative coatings compare with the general thermal conductivity behavior of high entropy carbides (HECbs) described by Ujah and Olubambi 2025?
answer:
The reduction of Ta or Nb content in Li et al. 2025's (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C ablative coatings was found to effectively improve ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure [5]. This implies that lower Ta or Nb content can lead to better high-temperature protective performance by reducing the formation of low-melting-point oxides and enhancing structural stability.

In contrast, Ujah and Olubambi 2025 described that the thermal conductivity of high entropy carbides (HECbs) tends to rise with temperature, and this behavior is influenced by factors such as carbon stoichiometry and configurational disorder. For example, the thermal conductivity of (Hf, Ta, Zr, Nb)C HECb increases from 10.7 W/mK at ambient to 39.9 W/mK at 2000 ◦ C [1]. Additionally, altering carbon stoichiometry in (TiZrHfVNbTa)Cx (x = 0.6 -1) showed a decline in the thermal conductivity from 10.6 W/mK at ambient to 6.4 W/mK due to increased scattering of electron and phonon by carbon vacancies [1].

However, the specific comparison between the reduction of Ta or Nb content in (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C ablative coatings and the general thermal conductivity behavior of HECbs is not covered in the provided sources.

chunks.text:
A) Thermal properties of high entropy carbides
High entropy carbides (HECbs) form a class of ultra-high temperature  ceramics  with  distinct  thermal  behavior,  making  them  more appropriate than traditional ceramic materials in high temperatures and extreme environment applications like those of hypersonic cars and high temperature structures. It was reported that the thermal conductivity of HECbs  tends  to  rise  with  temperature.  Just  as  a  five-cation  HECb exhibited increased thermal conductivity up to 1200 ◦ C, which is in line with single-metal carbides [43]. More so, there was a report that the thermal conductivity of (Hf, Ta, Zr, Nb)C HECb rose from 10.7 W/mK at ambient to 39.9 W/mK at 2000 ◦ C as a result of electron influences [81]. Further research shows that thermal conductivity of HECbs is affected by the amount of carbon in the composite. As such, carbon deposition exceeding the stoichiometric quantity showed decline in thermal conductivity  with  retention  of  some  temperature-dependent  curve  [43]. Meanwhile, altering carbon stoichiometry in (TiZrHfVNbTa)Cx (x = 0.6 -1) showed a decline in the thermal conductivity from 10.6 W/mK at ambient to 6.4 W/mK because of increased scattering of electron and phonon by carbon vacancies [82]. It was revealed also that HECbs like (HfTaZrTi)C and (HfTaZrNb)C have high thermodynamic stability with negative enthalpy of formation, which shows that they have good phase integrity  at  elevated  temperatures  [83].  Table  4 shows  the  thermal properties of some high entropy carbides.

3 High-entropy UHTC for performance optimization
Apart from metal diborides, the performance of high-entropy metal carbides has also been investigated.[93, 94] Wang et al . [88] prepared a high-entropy (TiZrNbTaMo)C ceramic by using a two-step hot pressing method with a hardness value of 25.3 GPa at 9.8 N, which was higher than that of ordinary ultra-high temperature ceramics. The lattice  distortion  caused  by  the  difference  of  atomic  size  leads  to  the  increase  of hardness, which is consistent with the principle of high entropy borides. Apart from the mechanical properties, the oxidation resistance behavior of high-entropy metal carbides is an important aspect as thermal protective coatings for space applications, especially considering the relatively poor oxidation resistance behaviors of metal carbides UHTCs comparing to metal diborides. Recently, Li et al . [95] systematically investigated the ablation  resistance  of  high-entropy  carbides,  including  (Hf-Ta)C,  (Hf-Ta-Nb)C,  and (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C, prepared via pressurelessly sintering at 2100 ℃ for 2h. After 30 s of ablation at 2.4 MW/m² oxyacetylene flame, the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C exhibited the thinnest oxide layer, only 51.5% of (Hf-Ta)C. This performance enhancement is attributed to the enrichment of Ta in HfO2/ZrO2 lattices, which forms low-melting-point phases that compromise the stability of the oxide layer, while the mutual dissolution of Zr and HfO2 forms a (Hf, Zr)O2 solid solution, effectively delaying the structural destabilization of the oxide layer. 3.1. Ablation behavior of protective (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C system at high temperatures
As  characterized  in  Fig.  2b,  the  sprayed  Ta25Nb25  coating  is composed of carbides, (Hf, Zr)O2 and (Ta, Nb)Ox. The detailed PDF cards for  as-sprayed  coatings  with  different  composition  are  presented  in Fig. S2c. Within the 2 θ range of 30 -40 ◦ , two typical diffraction peaks of carbides were detected, corresponding to the (111) and (200) planes of their  FCC  structures,  while  some  (Ta,  Nb)Ox  oxides  (around  37.5 ◦ ) related  with  Ta  or  Nb  elements  were  also  observed.  Limited  by  the spraying process carried out in an atmospheric environment, the melted and semi-melted carbide powders were inevitably oxidized. In our previous  work  [35],  the  ball  milling  and  agglomeration  (spray  drying technique) process were found to have little effect on the oxidation of original powders. Generally, the maximum temperature during agglomeration process is about 300 ◦ C, lower than the initial oxidation temperatures of carbides. The XRD pattern of the Ta25Nb25 agglomerated powders was supplemented in Fig. S2b. It can be detected that there is no oxide observed in the agglomerated powders, suggesting that the  remaining oxides  in  the  sprayed  coatings  were  generated  by  the thermal spray process.
Fig. 2. Analytical results of Ta25Nb25 agglomerated powder and sprayed coating:(a) surface BSE images of agglomerated powders and sprayed coating; (b) XRD patterns of sprayed coating; (c1-c2) cross-sectional BSE images and corresponding TEM image.

3.1. Ablation behavior of protective (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C system at high temperatures
Fig. 4e compares the roughness and cross-sectional BSE images of different (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C ablated samples with changing Ta/Nb molar ratios. It can be seen that with the addition of Nb element, the coatings ' roughness gradually decreased and typical pore defects developed towards the interior of the coatings. Numerous pores and defects appeared in  the  oxide  layer  of  all  the  coatings  (Ta50,  Ta25Nb25  and  Nb50), indicating that none of these coatings could provide effective long-term protection ( < 120 s) in an oxyacetylene ablation environment under the heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 . The reason is, when the content ratio of high- melting-point (Hf/Zr-based oxides) and low-melting-point phases (Ta/ Nb-based oxides) is close to 1:1, the excessive Ta or Nb element will result  in  the  massive  loss  of  (Ta,  Nb)2O5 and  residual  (Hf,  Zr)6(Ta, Nb)2O17 oxides [48].

4. Conclusions
Relationships  between  element  types,  composition  and  ablation property in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems have been discussed step by  step  via  SAPS  coating  preparation  and  performance  tests  under oxyacetylene ablation (2.4 MW/m 2 ). The synergistic effects of Hf, Zr, Ta and  Nb  elements  were  studied  through  different  procedures:  data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment  verification,  where  an  optimized  coating  system  for  long-term ablation protection above 2000 ◦ C was obtained. Main conclusions are as follows:
- (1)  Non-equimolar design based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems was found to be related to intrinsic porosity and self-derived low-
Fig. 12. Surface BSE images (a) and cross-sectional BSE images (b-c) of Zr70Nb10, Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation.
melting-point phases. When the content ratio of high-meltingpoint  (Hf/Zr-based  oxides)  and  low-melting-point  phases  (Ta/ Nb-based oxides) is close to 1:1 in the as-prepared coatings systems (~15 % porosity), they are difficult to provide long-term effective protection ( < 120 s) above 2000 ◦ C.
- (2)  The decline of Ta or Nb relative content in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating  systems  was  found  effective  in  improving  ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure.
- (3)  The optimized (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C coating system exhibited the best  long-term  ablation  property  (-0.01 μ m/s,  300 s),  corresponding to the Zr/Hf molar ratio around 1:1 in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system via first-principles calculation screening and experimental verification.

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
Based on the above analysis, considering the similarity and structural integrity effect of Nb and Ta addition, the next composition optimization on the sprayed (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating system can be based on (Hf-ZrNb)C system. Fig. 6 exhibits the optical photos and surface morphology of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10 coatings after 120 s ablation under the heat flux  of  2.4 MW/m 2 .  Significant  ablation  damage  occurred  in  Nb50 coating,  and  the  oxide  layer  showed  a  splashing  characteristic  with maximum ablation crater depth of 216.5 μ m and surface roughness of 47.5 μ m. Compared to Nb50 coating, both Nb20 and Nb10 coatings exhibited a complete oxide layer structure without any peeling bulging and splashing. Especially, Nb20 coating presented a few micro craters resulting from structural instability with surface roughness of 31.0 μ m, while the Nb10 coating still exhibited a smooth surface with no apparent cracks or pores with surface roughness of only 16.2 μ m. The XRD patterns of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10 coatings were given in Fig. S4, where more (Hf, Zr)O2  phase was observed with the content decrease of Nb element. Hence, the decline of Ta or Nb relative content in the (Hf-Zr-TaNb)C coating systems was found effective to improve ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure.

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
Fig.  7d-e  show  the  phase  diagram  of  HfO2-ZrO2  and  ZrO2-Nb2O5 systems. In the HfO2-ZrO2  phase diagram, the lowest liquidus temperature is about 2680 ◦ C. When turning to ZrO2-Nb2O5 binary phase diagram, liquid phase regions (shaded area) are significantly enlarged at similar temperatures, accompanied with the replacement process of lowmelting-point Zr6Nb2O17  for ZrO2, indicating a significant increase in liquid content and instability for both Nb50 and Nb20 coating. In this work, the composition change and corresponding ablation resistance of Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb-O films were highly related to Ta/Nb addition amount and (Hf, Zr)O2-based solid solution skeletons with high-temperature stability. Taking Zr-Nb-O binary system as an example, as discussed in Fig. 7e, the composition range around 2000 ◦ C was in t-ZrO2  or t-ZrO2 + L regions  despite  the  Nb  addition  amount  up  to  40 mol%.  As  samples ' temperature drops to room temperature from the ablation temperature (~2000 ◦ C), the phase precipitation process will occur in different (Hf- Zr-Ta-Nb)C coatings, corresponding to the detected A6B2O17 (A = Hf, Zr; B = Ta, Nb) phases in the XRD patterns (Fig. S4). As for other Hf-Ta-O, Hf-Nb-O, Zr-Ta-O binary systems, similar analytical results were also expected. However, despite that the A6B2O17 phases precipitated in the oxide films when samples cooled down to room temperature, the t-ZrO2 or  t-HfO2 solid  solutions  still  mainly  contributed  to  maintaining  the structure stability of the oxide film under ablation conditions, according to the reported phase diagrams in Fig. 7e.

3.2. Mechanism analysis on the addition of low-melting-point components
From another perspective of doping effect of Ta and Nb elements, it was reported [15 -17,34] the critical Ta content for structure instability of  Zr-Ta-O,  Hf-Ta-O  and  Hf-Zr-Ta-O  films  is  no  more  than  20 mol%, while the critical Nb content for structure instability of Zr-Nb-O and Hf-Nb-O films exceeds 30 mol%, suggesting smaller deconstructive effects of Nb addition than that of Ta addition. Table S3 compares the reported  linear  ablation  rates  under  the  same  oxyacetylene  ablation condition (2.4 MW/m 2 , 120 s). The HfC-NbC coating did not exhibit any thickness reduction even with the content of NbC reaching 40 mol%. In contrast, a thickness reduction in HfC-TaC coatings was observed at the TaC  content  of  30 mol%.  As  for  the  ZrC-NbC  coating,  no  thickness reduction was noted with the content of NbC reaching 30 mol%. However, for the ZrC-TaC coating, a thickness reduction occurred at the TaC content of 20 mol%. According to the recorded phenomena in Table S3, the thickness reduction of oxide layer occurred with the addition of TaC, quite different from NbC addition.

high melting temperature ( > 30 0 0 ° C), excellent thermochemical stability, high strength and ablation resistance [ 7 , 8 ]. However, the intrinsic thermal conductivity of these ultra-high temperature carbides is considerably high, for instance, 33.5 W/(m /22c5 K) for ZrC and 29.3 W/(m /22c5 K) for HfC [9] . Therefore, it is challenged to make effective modifications to reduce thermal conductivity, so that they can satisfy the requirements for thermal insulation materials. Generally, enhancing the porosity and high entropy effect are two methods to decrease thermal conductivity. It is well known that realizing ultra-high porosity ( > 90%) could obtain extremely low thermal conductivity [10] . In addition, the significant lattice inhomogeneity arising from high entropy adoption may inhibit heat transportation through the lattice, and subsequently reduces thermal conductivity of a high entropy ceramic [11] . A good example is demonstrated by the very low thermal conductivity of high entropy carbide (Zr 0.2 Hf 0.2 Ti 0.2 Nb 0.2 Ta 0.2 )C that has a value of 6.45 W/(m /22c5 K) [9] . The magnitude is much lower than the averaged thermal conductivity (24.96 W/(m /22c5 K)) of the five binary carbides. A proper combination of the above two methods can lead to a lower thermal conductivity of ultra-high temperature carbide. To date, high en- tropy carbide (Zr 0.2 Hf 0.2 Ti 0.2 Nb 0.2 Ta 0.2 )C with 80.99% porosity was successfully fabricated and displayed moderately high compressive strength of 3.45 MPa and low room-temperature thermal conductivity of 0.39 W/(m /22c5 K) [12] , which supports the feasibility of aforementioned perspective. Besides, it is still a challenge for both ultrahigh porosity ( ∼ 95%) and high strength.

3.4. Compressive strength and thermal conductivity
This is because high entropy effect can efficiently reduce thermal conductivity of solid skeleton due to significant phonon scattered at the disordered cation sublattice.
Besides, Fig. 6 (b) plots the trends of thermal conductivity of porous samples with different porosities as a function of testing temperature. As can be seen, thermal conductivity of porous high entropy carbide slowly increases with elevated temperatures. Thermal conductivity of porous (Zr 1/5 Hf 1/5 Nb 1/5 Ta 1/5 Ti 1/5 )C with porosity of 86.4% is 0.239 W/(m /22c5 K) and 0.436 W/(m /22c5 K) at room temperature and 300 ° C, respectively. Moreover, the sample with higher porosity has lower thermal conductivity than those samples with lower porosity at both room and high temperatures. The values range from 0.436 W/(m /22c5 K) to 0.326 W/(m /22c5 K) when porosities increase from 86.4% to 95.9% at 300 ° C. Specially, thermal conductivity for porous ZrC [34] with porosity of 85% is shown in Fig. 6 (b) for comparison. From room temperature to 300 ° C, thermal conductivity of ZrC has a larger variation from 0.94 W/(m /22c5 K) to 1.35 W/(m /22c5 K), which is much higher than those of porous high entropy carbide with porosity of 86.4% (from 0.239 W/(m /22c5 K) to 0.436 W/(m /22c5 K)). High entropy effect effectively reduces thermal conductivity of UHTC sample. Based on the above comparison, it is clear that our sample has enhanced mechanical and thermal properties than those carbides in previous works, as well as superior properties compared with typical oxides. Hence, it can be concluded that the specimens in this work have better comprehensive performance.

A) Thermal properties of high entropy carbides
Generally, thermal conductivity in HECbs is a function of its family and operating temperature, and it maintains relatively high k at elevated temperatures due to significant electronic heat transport, enabling their use in cutting and machining tools. It is imperative to state that the influence  of  configurational  disorder  on  transport  characteristics  is  a function of the dominant heat-carrying mechanism. In HECbs, where electron-mediated  transport  stimulates  significantly  to  total  thermal conductivity, moderate disorder can enhance the electronic density of states and promote carrier mobility at elevated temperature, resulting in higher conductivity. In contrast, thermal transport in HEBs and high entropy monoborides is primarily phonon-driven; here the same disorder raises phonon and mass-fluctuation scattering, suppressing lattice thermal  conductivity.  Thus,  whether  disorder  enhances  or  reduces transport  depends  on  the  balance  between  electronic  and  phononic contributions and the severity of structural distortion.

2.7. Comparative properties of HEBs, HENs and HECbs
High  entropy  carbides  typically  have  the  most  superior  hardness among  the  three  HECs,  around  30 GPa  at  optimal  valence  electron concentration  (VEC)  of  8.4,  being  far  much  higher  than  the  rule-ofmixtures  values  by  as  much  as  30 -50 %.  Exceedingly  large  also  is their  elastic  moduli  which  is  attributed  to  the  strong  Metal -carbide bonding  and  severe lattice distortion. HECbs,  moreover,  exhibit adjustable ductility and toughness by manipulating the carbon-to-metal ratios [117].
On the other hand, high entropy nitrides exhibit an average hardness of 20 -25 GPa, with excellent elastic moduli that is 17 % above the rule- C.O. Ujah and P.A. Olubambi of-mixtures, and hardness that is higher by 22 %. HENs are noted for their high stiffness and improved ductility making them useful in applications requiring resilience under strain like hypersonic leading edges and propulsion components in aerospace [77].
Moreover,  high  entropy  borides  have  a  balance  of  hardness  and elevated thermal conductivity of > 20 GPa and 75 -105 W/mK, respectively.  Their  resistance  to  oxidation  is  10  times  greater  than  binary diborides. They exhibit low fracture toughness but high thermal stability which are attributed to  their  hexagonal  configuration.  By  and  large, HECbs  are  good  at  highest  hardness  and  strength,  HENs  have  high stiffness and toughness, while HEBs have excellent oxidation resistance and elevated temperature stability [118]. Table 7 shows the comparative properties of HEBs, HENs and HECbs [104].

3.1. Ablation behavior of protective (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C system at high temperatures
Fig. 4 depicts the characteristics of Ta25Nb25 coating after 120 s ablation under the heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 .  As shown in Fig. 4a, the surface temperature gradually increased at initial 20 s and then stabilized. The peak temperature in the 1st cycle was about 1954 ◦ C, while the peak temperature in the 2nd cycle showed an increase, reaching about  2047 ◦ C.  According  to  Fig.  4b,  the  surface  oxide  layer  of Ta25Nb25 coating was composed of small lath oxides and flake oxides accompanied by some pores. The XRD pattern of ablated Ta25Nb25 coating was given in Fig. S4, the phase composition corresponds to B2O5 and A6B2O17 (A = Hf, Zr; B = Ta, Nb). After further characterization on surface morphology (measured diameter: φ 18 mm), the central region of  Ta25Nb25  coating  after  ablation  exhibited  significant  damage,  as displayed in Fig. 4c, with a crater depth of 292.5 μ m. When considering formation reasons for the service-derived oxide scale, the formation of small lath oxide can be attributed to the preferential orientation characteristic  of  Ta2O5 phase  ( a = 1.2785 nm, b = 0.4854 nm, c = 0.5528 nm  [47])  and  (Hf,  Zr)6(Ta,  Nb)2O17 phase  (Zr6Ta2O17: a = 4.092 nm, b = 0.493 nm, c = 0.527 nm [47]) along a certain axial direction,  as  shown  in  Fig.  4d.  The  presence  of  flake  oxides  can  be attributed  to  the  formation  of  Nb2O5 phase  ( a = 2.108 nm, b = 0.3823 nm, c = 1.933 nm [47]).

1. Introduction
Thermal-insulation  materials  capable  of  enduring  ultra-high  temperatures are urgently required by hypersonic vehicle thermal protection systems and nuclear reactor cladding [1 -4]. There are two critical factors  in  developing  ultra-high  temperature  materials  for  thermal insulation:  the  selection  of  an  appropriate  ultra-high  temperature resistance material and the reduction of its thermal conductivity [5 -7]. As a kind of transition metal carbides, Ta4HfC5  is considered as promising  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  because  of  extremely high melting point (4215 K), excellent high-temperature phase stability, and outstanding high-temperature oxidation resistance [8 -10]. The key for the ultra-high temperature thermal insulation application of Ta4HfC5 ceramics is the reduction of the thermal conductivity [11]. Increasing the porosity of the material and regulating its pore structure is one of the main strategies to reduce the thermal conductivity of ceramic materials. The numerous pores with appropriate size and structure in porous ceramics can efficiently hinder solid-phase heat conduction and reduce the thermal conductivity. Additionally, nanoscale ceramic particles can be used to construct the porous ceramic skeleton with nanopores to form hierarchical  pore  structure  and  further  reduce  the  solid-phase  heat conduction and convective heat transfer of Ta4HfC5  ceramic [12 -16].

1. Introduction
Efficient and appropriate composition design of multi-elemental UHTCs for extremely thermal protection is in great demand and promising for carbon-based materials. Based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C protective coating system via plasma spraying, the synergistic effects were clarified through several steps: data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment verification. Results showed that, (Hf0.25Zr0.25Ta0.25Nb0.25)C coating exhibited insufficient protective ability (2000 ◦ C, < 120 s), due to the similar content ratio of high-melting-point and low-melting-point phases. The decline of Ta or Nb relative content effectively improved ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2  skeleton phases. After further screening for the proper composition of Hf, Zr, Ta and Nb elements, the (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C protective system was optimized and verified step by step, which delightfully showed long-term ablation property (2000 ◦ C, > 300 s) with nearly zero linear ablation rate.

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
To visually compare the effect of varying Nb element content on the surface temperature distribution of coatings, Fig. 7a presents the surface temperature curve and surface temperature field distribution of Nb50, Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings  during  120 s  ablation,  where  the  surface temperatures increased with more Nb content. Fig. 7b shows the average linear ablation rate and average mass ablation rate of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10  coatings  after  120 s  ablation.  The  Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings exhibited thickness increase and weight gain and Nb10 showed smaller change in mass and thickness ( GLYPH<0> 0.65 ± 0.04 μ m/s, GLYPH<0> 0.47 mg/s) change, while Nb50 coating showed significant thickness decline. As reported by Li et al. [19], the service-derived (Hf, Zr)O2 layer of (Hf0.5Zr0.5)C coating under 2.4 MW/m 2 oxyacetylene flame was porous and loose after 120 s ablation, with an average linear ablation rate of GLYPH<0> 0.88 μ m/s, which is also higher than that of Nb10 coating in this work.

(2) Modi /uniFB01 ed with carbide by PIP
As the PIP process has a lower cost and a shorter preparation period than other techniques, SiC [152 -155], ZrC [156 -158], HfC [159 -161] were introduced into carbon matrix to improve anti-oxidation properties and ablation resistance. Liu et al. [153] evaluated the e /uniFB00 ect of ablation products on the ablation resistance of C/C-SiC composites. The ablation products play several key roles during ablation: decreasing surface temperature, acting as a barrier to oxidizing species attack, and conglutinating ablated carbon /uniFB01 bers. E /uniFB00 ect of SiC locations on ablation properties of C/C-SiC composites was studied [154]. Li et al. [156]
found that the ZrC-rich layer could seal surface pores and cracks to restrain preferential ablation in defect areas and form a continuously melting ZrO2 layer to prevent carbon from oxidizing during ablation. The strengthened ZrC-rich layer could also reduce the mechanical denudation of composites. In addition, Li et al. [159] discovered that C/C composites with 6.5 wt.% HfC exhibit the best thermal conductivity and ablation resistance. During ablation, tree-coral-like HfO2 particles formed and functioned as thermal barriers, and the HfCxOy phase acted as an oxygen di /uniFB00 usion barrier, protecting composites from further ablation [161]. Ablation results showed that improvement of the thermal conductivity could slow down oxidation of carbon and improve the ablation resistance of composites, because it reduces surface temperature of composites during ablation.

4.5.1. Modified with carbide
Single-phase modified carbon matrix:
The  PIP  process,  being  cost-effective  and  quicker  than  other methods, introduced SiC [45,172 -174], HfC [46,175], and ZrC [176 -178] into carbon matrices to enhance anti-oxidation and ablation resistance. Liu et al. [173] found that ablation products reduce surface temperature, act as barriers against oxidation, and conglutinate ablated carbon  fibers  in  C/C-SiC  composites.  The  effect  of  SiC  locations  on ablation properties was studied [174]. Li et al. [176] observed that a ZrC-rich layer seals surface pores and cracks, forming a ZrO2  layer to prevent oxidation and reduce mechanical denudation. Li et al. [175] also found that C/C composites with 6.5 wt. % HfC exhibits optimal thermal conductivity and ablation resistance, with HfO2  particles and HfCxOy phase acting as thermal and oxygen diffusion barriers. Improved thermal  conductivity  was  shown  to  reduce  oxidation  and  enhance ablation  resistance  by  lowering  surface  temperature  during  ablation. C/C-SiC -HfC  composites  were  made  using  PIP  and  Gaseous  Silicon Infiltration (GSI) at 1900 ◦ C and 2100 ◦ C, reducing preparation time. After 120 s, composites at 2100 ◦ C showed lower linear (5.06 μ m/s) and mass  ablation  rates  (1.03  mg/s)  [179].  Therefore,  modifying  carbon matrices through the PIP process has significantly enhanced composites ' anti-oxidation and ablation resistance by incorporating SiC, HfC, and ZrC, effectively reducing surface temperatures and improving overall thermal conductivity.
Multiple phase modified carbon matrix:

a b s t r a c t
The results showed that various structurally stable oxides were formed during the ablation process of C/C-SiC -(Zr, Hf, Ti)C, and the synergistic effect of these oxides enhanced the resistance of the material to thermochemical ablation. Additionally, boride UHTCs exhibited higher thermal conductivity than carbides (ZrB 2 : 60 W m -1 K -1 , HfB2 : 104 W m -1 K -1 , ZrC and HfC: 20 W m -1 K -1 ). This enhanced thermal conductivity helped lower the temperature of the ablation surface and improved ablation performance [ 18 ]. Wang et al. [ 19 ] investigated the effect of added ZrB2 on the ablation performance of C/C-ZrC-SiC composites. The incorporation of ZrB2 reduced the surface temperature of the composite material from 2314 ° C to 2068 ° C, thereby enhancing its ablation resistance. Feltrin et al. [ 20 ] demonstrated that medium-entropy borides exhibit superior properties, including hardness and oxidation resistance, compared to individual monolithic borides such as ZrB2 and HfB2 . These findings suggest the feasibility of incorporating medium-entropy carbides and borides into C/C composites (C/C-MECs) to form a structurally stable multiphase oxygen barrier layer during ablation. This approach further reduces the surface temperature of the material and enhances its thermochemical ablation resistance.

question: What were the energy and force prediction errors of the deep potential model trained by Dai et al. 2022 compared to DFT calculations?
answer: The energy and force prediction errors of the deep potential model trained by Dai et al. 2022 compared to DFT calculations are 15 meV/atom and 400 meV/Å, respectively [1].

2.1. Construction of GBs
A dataset generated by DFT-based calculations was used to train and test the DP model. Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) [36] was adopted to perform DFT-based calculations. Ultrasoft pseudopotential [37] and Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation (PBE-GGA) [38] were applied in the calculations. The cutoff energy of the plane-wave basis set was set to be 500 eV. The k -points mesh according to the Monkhorst-Pack method [39] with a separation of 0.03 ˚ A -1 was adopted in the Brillouin zone. Supercells for non-defective crystals, crystals with vacancies, melts, surfaces, and GBs were constructed. For each supercell, 10 steps of MD simulation under NVT ensemble with time step being 5 fs and temperature randomly chosen from 0 K to 5,0 0 0 K were implemented to generate the DFT dataset. One-tenth of the data were left as a test set, while the others were used to train the DP model. Fig. 2 compares the energies and forces between the DP predictions and the DFT calculations for the test set. Statistics on the prediction errors reveal that the prediction errors in energy and force are 15 meV/atom and 400 meV/ ˚ A, respectively, which guarantees reliable simulations in this work. In addition, the supplemental information (SI) provides more comparisons between the DP model and the DFT-based calculations, which reveal good reliability of the DP model.

II. Calculation Methods
First-principles calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) were performed using the Cambridge Serial Total Energy Package (CASTEP) code 19 in order to elucidate the electronic structure, chemical bonding and mechanical properties of NiB. The Vanderbilt-type ultrasoft pseudopotential 20 and generalized gradient approximation based on the Wu-Cohen (WC) scheme 21 for the exchange-correlation functional were used for DFT calculations. Upon calculation, 'spin-polarized' function was checked so that the calculation was performed using different wave functions for different spins. The plane-wave basis set cutoff was 500 eV. The special points sampling integration over the Brillourin zone was employed by using the Monkhorst Pack method with 9 9 3 9 8 special k-points mesh. 22 To minimize the enthalpy and interatomic forces, lattice parameters were modified. In geometry optimization, the Broyden -Fletcher -Goldfarb - Shanno minimization scheme 23 was used. The tolerances for geometry optimization are difference on total energy within 5 9 10 /C0 6 eV/atom, maximum ionic Hellmann -Feynman force within 0.01 eV/ /C23 A, and maximum ionic displacement within 5 9 10 /C0 4 /C23 A and maximum stress within 0.02 GPa. The calculations of projected density of states (PDOS) were performed using a projection of the plane-wave electronic states onto a localized linear combination of atomic orbitals basis set.

2.2. Computational methods
The first-principles calculations were performed within the framework of density functional theory (DFT) using the Cambridge Sequential Total  Energy  Package  (CASTEP)  code  [23].  The  interaction  between valence electrons and ion nuclei was denoted by ultrasoft pseudo-potential [24]. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof represented exchange-correlation energy (PBE) scheme within generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [25]. To ensure convergence of the total energy in the ground state, the plane-wave cutoff energy was set to 440 eV. The integration  in  the  Brillouin  zone  (BZ)  was  done  on  a  set  of  special k-points  determined  according  to  the  Monkhorst-Pack  scheme,  the k-points grid of NiB, Ni2B, Ni3B and Ni4B3  are 5 × 2 × 5, 3 × 3 × 3, 3 × 2 × 3 and 1 × 5 × 2 respectively. Geometry optimization was carried out using the Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) method, which minimizes the energy and enhances stability by optimizing the system parameters  [26].  During  the  optimization  process,  the  total  energy convergence threshold was set to 5.0 × 10 ⁻⁶ eV/atom, the force on each atom was kept below 0.01 eV/Å, the maximum atomic displacement was within 5.0 × 10 ⁻⁴ Å, and the maximum stress was below 0.02 GPa. After geometry optimization, the elastic constants (Cij) of the Ni-B material were determined based on the stress-strain relationship and fitted linearly using Hooke ' s law [27]. The results are shown in Table S1.

2.3. DFT calculations
First-principles  calculations  were  performed  with  the  Vienna  Abinitio Simulation Package ( VASP ) code based on the density functional theory (DFT). The exchange-correlation effects were processed by the generalized  gradient  approximation  (GGA)  with  the  Perdew-BurkeErnzerhof (PBE) function [22 -24]. Interactions between ionic real and valence electrons were determined by applying the Projector-Augmented Wave (PAW) pseudopotential. The valence configuration are 5 d 2 6 s 2 ,  5 d 3 6 s 2 and 2 s 2 2 p 2 for Hf, Ta and C, respectively. After the convergence test, the plane wave cut-off energy was set to 600 eV, and the matching Monkhorst GLYPH<0> Pack k-points grid sampling was set to be 15 × 15 × 15 for the Brillouin zone [25]. After the full optimization of the cell, the total energies were converged well to 5 . 0 × 10 GLYPH<0> 6  eV/atom, the maximum ionic Hellmann-Feynman forces convergence value was no more than 0.01 eV/Å, and the maximum of the stress tensor was less than 0.02 GPa.

Therefore, the t -Zr0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 grains are isolated by Y-Si-O phase in agglomerated particles ( Figure 4e,f), and the growth of t -Zr 0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 crystals www.advancedsciencenews.com www.advancedscience.com is restricted due to the high di/uniFB00usion energy of Zr in the Y-Si-O system and stronger interaction of Y-Si-O -t -Zr 0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 . Overall, the results of DFT calculations provide the guidance of the structural design for oxides layer, which is of significance for the further studies on reusable UHTCMCs.

2.1. Construction of GBs
The machine learning potential proposed by Zhang et al. [ 32 , 33 ] is adopted to train an interatomic potential for multi-element transition metal carbides from a dataset generated by DFT calculations. The model is called DP, which maps the local atomic configurations to atomic energies by a deep neural network [ 32 , 33 ], and has been proven to be applicable to high entropy materials [ 34 , 35 ]. The DP model is comprised of two sets of neural networks. The first one is a descriptor net, which automatically encodes local atomic configurations to symmetry-preserving descriptors. The second one is a fitting net, which maps the symmetry-preserving descriptors to atomic energies. In this work, the architecture of DP was set as follows: (1) the descriptor was a smooth edition descriptor from reference [32] , which consisted of 3 layers of residual net with each layer having 40, 80, and 160 nodes; (2) the cutoff was set to be 6.5 ˚ A with a smooth function imposed from 6.0 ˚ A; (3) the projection dimension of the descriptor was set to be 16; (4) the 'type-one' technology was adopted to reduce the scale of the descriptor net; (5) the fitting net included 4 layers with each layer having 160, 160, 160, and 160 nodes. For details of the DP parameters, please refer to the original papers and the open-source software [ 32 , 33 ].

3.1. Electronic structure and dielectric functions
The  electronic  structures  of  transition  metal  diborides  have  been extensively  investigated  previously  by  DFT  calculations  and  experiments [16,17,28,29]. Generally accepted features are the finite density of states and a pseudogap at Fermi level, indicating these systems are metallic and strongly-bonded [16]. The bonding states below the Fermi level are dominated by the TM to TM d -d interactions and B to B p -p interactions, while the anti-bonding states are mainly from TM d orbitals [16]. Due to the similarity in electronic structure, only the band structure of ZrB2  is illustrated for simplicity. Since the energy of solar spectrum is not higher than 5 eV, the bands from DFT calculations (solid lines) in energy range from GLYPH<0> 15 eV to 10 eV and QP corrected bands (blue  and  red  dash  lines)  in  energy  range  from GLYPH<0> 10 eV to 5 eV are presented in Fig. 2(a). With QP correction considered, the metallic nature of ZrB2 is preserved, the main difference between original DFT band structure and corrected one is the broadening of energy gap where no crossing of valence and conductive bands is found. Since the interband dielectric  function  is  directly  determined  by  the  band  structure,  QP correction is vital to the reliable prediction of optical properties.

3.2. Modeling
A grid search was conducted on the training set to determine the optimal  set  of  hyperparameters  for  each  model,  and  the  hyperparameters are listed in Table 4. The mean absolute error ( MAE ) and mean squared error ( MSE ) of the model predictions on the test set reveal that among the 8 models (Fig. 7), the Random forest model exhibits the highest prediction accuracy with an mean absolute error ( MAE ) of 2.75 μ m s GLYPH<0> 1 and an mean squared error ( MSE ) of 7.3 ( μ m s GLYPH<0> 1 ) 2 , whereas the performance  of  the  other  models  on  this  dataset  is  suboptimal.  The scatter  plots  of  the  model ' s  training  and  prediction  data  also  clearly demonstrate that the Random forest model ' s data points are closest to the 45-degree diagonal line for both the training and test sets (Fig. 8), with the coefficient of determination ( R 2 ) for training and prediction reaching 0.81 and 0.71, respectively, indicating relatively high predictive precision. Consequently, we select the Random forest model as the surrogate model for subsequent analysis of the ablation resistance of UHTCMCs.
The  Shapley  additive  explanations  (SHAP)  values  based  on  the Random forest model are shown in Fig. 8. Negative Shapley additive explanations  values  indicate  that  a  feature  negatively  influences  the J. Xiao et al.

2.1. Construction of GBs
Simulating GBs of medium/high entropy ceramics is challenging due to the chemical complexity of the random solid solu- tion state. Though density functional theory (DFT) based methods can in principle deal with the chemical complexity well, their computational demanding nature impedes their application to GBs of medium/high entropy materials, where thousands of atoms or more are necessary to model the chemical complexity. Thanks to the advancement in artificial intelligence, a new paradigm, machine learning potential, has come forth in recent years [29-32] . The machine learning potential bridges the gap between DFT-based methods and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, which guarantees atomistic simulations with accuracy comparable to DFT-based methods at a cost comparable to MD simulations.

To further enhance the predictive fidelity of oxide scale growth under service-relevant conditions, future research could focus on integrating advanced Finite Element Modeling (FEM) approaches with multilayer diffusion frameworks. This would improve the model's ability to simulate the complicated interplay between transport kinetics and mechanical constraints, encompassing oxide scale spallation which may arise from stress accumulation and/or grain boundary diffusion.

A B S T R A C T
In this study, we used the ANN to achieve the aim to predict melting points of non-oxide UHTCs. In order to improve the accuracy of the model, we /uniFB01 rst made a data cleaning of the dataset, forming a huge dataset containing most of non-oxide UHTCs. Then, we adjusted the accuracy of model prediction by training and testing the model, and analyzed the in /uniFB02 uence of network structure in accuracy, while several reported data have been used to ensure the accuracy of the model. We set Hf-C-N system as an example to introduce the ability of the prediction model, whose average relative error is as low as 5%. Finally, we gave a theoretical interpretation by using of the materials thermodynamics and the density functional theory (DFT). The novel material developing process is successfully applied in the melting points

predication of non-oxide UHTCs by taking the advantage of the highperformance computing and theoretical calculation.

3.2. Machine learning model prediction and selection
The predictive accuracy of ML models is highly dependent on the quantity and quality of datasets. Given the limited size of the original dataset (83 entries), we aimed to enhance model accuracy through data augmentation,  thereby  enabling  the  trained  surrogate  model  to  efficiently guide active learning for rapid discovery of high-performance compositions. To align with the active learning framework, the reciprocal of oxide layer thickness was adopted as the output target during model  training  and  prediction,  facilitating iterative optimization. Twelve  ML  models  were  implemented  using  Python ' s  scikit-learn  library. The original dataset (83 entries) and the combined dataset (1083 entries,  original  and  augmented)  were  separately  used  for  model training. The oxide layer thickness in the combined dataset was mathematically transformed by taking its reciprocal to serve as the prediction target. Both datasets were normalized and randomly split into training (80 %) and testing (20 %) subsets for independent model training and evaluation. Model performance was assessed using the coefficient of R², MAE and MSE on the test set. To ensure robustness, all models underwent  100  randomized  training  and  prediction  cycles,  with  reported metrics representing averages across these iterations.

2.2. Modeling
To achieve high prediction accuracy for the surrogate model, a series of mainstream models were trained and compared based on 845 clustered data. The models /uniFB01 rst underwent random search hyperparameter optimization (Table S2, Supporting Material), followed by model training and prediction with an 0.8 : 0.2 training-testing split ratio. Fig. 2(a) demonstrates the predictive performance comparison of twelve machine learning models on the same dataset, including KNeighbors Regressor (KNR), RandomForest Regressor (RFR), XGBoost Regressor (XGBR), LightGBM Regressor (LGBMR), MultilayerPerceptron Regressor (MLPR), GradientBoosting Regressor (GBR), Ridge Regressor (RR), Catboost Regressor (CBR), AdaBoost Regressor (ABR), GaussianProcess Regressor (GPR), SupportVector Regressor (SVR), and DecisionTree Regression (DTR). Each subplot illustrates the prediction results for both training (green dots) and test (orange dots) sets, along with their corresponding R 2 values. The results indicate that ensemble learning-based models, such as GBR (test set R 2 ¼ 0.90), XGBR ( R 2 ¼ 0.89), and CBR ( R 2 ¼ 0.90), exhibited superior performance, while KNR ( R 2 ¼ 0.64) and RR ( R 2 ¼ 0.65) demonstrated relatively poor performance. Although most models typically demonstrate higher accuracy near zero-value regions and exhibit lower accuracy in extreme-value regions, models trained with augmented data show signi /uniFB01 cantly improved prediction accuracy compared to those without data augmentation with predicted R 2 from /C0 0.1 to 0.66 (Fig. S2, Supporting Material). This indicates that data augmentation enhances the distributional trend of the sourced data, enabling models to better capture the distributional characteristics of the original data.

A B S T R A C T
Recent advancements in machine learning (ML) offer new opportunities for accelerating the design of oxidation-resistant UHTCs. Bianco et al. [15] developed a random forest regression model to predict oxide layer thickness after oxidation tests based on composition, processing parameters, and microstructural features. Trained on 107 experimental data points extracted from literature, the model achieved a mean absolute error (MAE) of 37.45 μ m and an R² of 0.83. However, data heterogeneity  from  diverse  sources  introduced  uncontrollable  biases, limiting its predictive accuracy and capacity to identify superior compositions. Hao et al. [16] explored multiple ML algorithms to predict oxidation/ablation  resistance  of  UHTC  coatings,  demonstrating  that random forest regression achieved the best performance (R² = 0.87) using a high-fidelity experimental dataset. Nevertheless, conventional ML models demand large datasets and exhibit constrained predictive accuracy under small-sample conditions. To address these challenges, strategies such as data augmentation (e.g., generative adversarial networks  (GANs)[17,18],  variational  autoencoders  (VAEs)[19]),  active learning[20 -22], Bayesian optimization[23], and transfer learning[24] have been proposed to alleviate the problem of underfitting caused by sparse  samples  and  enhance  model  robustness.  Among  them,  active learning has great potential in effectively identifying potential high-performance  materials  and  in  the  exploration  of  formulae.  Kim et al. [25] used active learning combined with experiments to search for the optimal multi-metal alloy catalyst. Feng et al. [26] utilized Bayesian optimization to achieve the best molecular formula, thereby accelerating the discovery of a high-performance methane combustion catalyst composed of multi-component PtPd@CeZrOx core-shell nanospheres.
Kim et al. [27] accelerated the discovery of the solvation structure of stable  aqueous  rechargeable  zinc  batteries  through  physically-guided Bayesian active learning.

question: According to Zapata-Solvas 2018, what phase was detected on the external surface of HS20 samples at temperatures higher than 1800°C during compressive deformation?
answer: According to Zapata-Solvas 2018, the formation of HfB is detected at the external surface of HS20 samples at temperatures higher than 1800°C during compressive deformation [1].
