3.2. HS20 microstructural characterization after deformation
The concept of SiC-reinforced MeB2 comes from the exceptional strength shown by SiC-ZrB2 composites, as high as ∼ 1 GPa [18], and the increased fracture toughness of MeB2 with SiC additions, from values of ∼ 3 for monolithic MeB2 to values as high as ∼ 7 MPa/m 1/2 for composites with SiC [19]. However, this trend reverses at high temperature as SiC acts as a softening agent. A possible reason for this softening is the formation of an eutectic in the Me-B-C ternary system with a melting point of 2390 °C for Zr-B-C, which is nearly 1000 °C lower than the melting temperature of ZrB2 [47]. In addition, the Me-BC phase diagrams reported by Rudy and Windisch [48] suggestthat the combination of MeC + MeB2 might be of interest at temperatures lower than 1500 °C as there could be temperature-dependent B-C exchange reactions associated with volume changes in the carbide particles, resulting in bodies with poor mechanical properties and subsequently limiting their high temperature potential use at temperatures above 1500 °C. If we consider the results observed here and previous available data [26,39,40,43], including the e /uniFB00 ect of 1 wt.% B in SiC, it could be concluded that B also has a negative e /uniFB00 ect on the high temperature mechanical behavior of MeB2 + SiC systems. Fig. 4, shows XRD patterns of HS20 external surfaces before and after deformation revealing that at temperatures higher than 1800 °C, the formation of HfB is detected at the surface. However, XRD from an internal surface in the bulk does not show formation of HfB at any temperature. Plastic deformation is a phenomenon controlled by the bulk, so this observation could be considered as a con /uniFB01 rmation of the existence of a B /uniFB02 ux from the surface towards the bulk.

3.3. HS20La high temperature mechanical properties
La2O3 addition does not act as a high temperature structural reinforcement. In addition, powders were observed immediately around the sample after testing at 1800 °C, a smaller amount (< 10 mg) of powder was observed at 1900 °C but no trace of powder was observed at 2000 °C, which could be related to the development of either porosity next to the external surface or a chemical reaction during deformation. C content of the powders found at 1800 °C was 1.70 ± 0.02 wt.%. However, the amounts of powder collected at 1900 °C were not enough for C content analysis. Considering that C content for HS20La is 2.01 wt.%, the latter /uniFB01 nding suggests that SiC might segregate in Si and free C at 1800 °C. Moreover, the higher the temperature, the lower the porosity detected at the external surface as di /uniFB00 usion phenomena are more active balancing the presence of any damaging mechanisms such as cavitation or reaction (Fig. 9). For example, Fig. 9a shows the cross section after compression testing at 1800 °C, in which the highest degree of porosity is observed and is reduced as temperature increases to 1900 °C (Fig. 9b) and 2000 °C (Fig. 9c). In a previous study, La2O3 particles were observed to be located next to SiC particles [19] which could have an in /uniFB02 uence on the SiC disociation as this phenomena was not observed in HS20. However, after deformation at 2000 °C there was no O in what were originally La2O3 particles as shown in Fig. 10. The remnant La2O3 particle contains La-Si-Hf-B after deformation, which suggests that O might react with C from SiC particles. Also, the stoichiometry of the SiBn particles is di /uniFB00 erent in HS20 than HS20La: the maximum n in HS20 is 2 whereas it is 12 for HS20La.

3.2. HS20 microstructural characterization after deformation
Furthermore, an increase in the SiC cluster size was observed when testing at or above 1800 °C through post mortem SEM only [23]. Therefore, it is important that future studies give improved microstructural characterization to enable a better understanding of the phenomena involved in high temperature deformation.
Melendez-Martinez et al. [44] showed; (i) ZrB2 at 1500 °C is 200 times more creep resistant than HS30 and (ii) the deformation mechanism is grain boundary sliding and that the deformation of individual ZrB2 grains could be neglected as well as any contribution to deformation from ZrB2-ZrB2 grain boundaries. Therefore, grain boundaries of secondary phases with ZrB2 and with the secondary phases are responsible for the macroscopic deformation and link the B di /uniFB00 usion to form new secondary phases in the Hf-B-Si and Hf-B-Si-C systems as the existing accommodation process of the deformation mechanism. However, it is di /uniFB03 cult to assess how the formation of these secondary phases start and how each phase contributes to macroscopic deformation. Future studies at low deformations will try to answer how instabilities are triggered and how deformation is controlled in the initial stage of plastic deformation. In summary, our study suggests that HS20 behaves at high temperature as follows; (i) a power law creep with thermal activation process accommodated by B lattice di /uniFB00 usion (n=3 and Q=242 kJ/mol) in the temperature range 1500 -1800 °C, consistent with the observation of dislocations by TEM, (ii) grain boundary sliding/pure di /uniFB00 usional mechanism accommodated by B grain boundary di /uniFB00 usion (n=1 and Q=334 kJ/mol), consistent with the absence of dislocations and the generation of new phases in the HfB-Si and Hf-B-Si-C systems.

3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
2b shows activation energy versus SiC content calculated by Talmy et al. for ZrB2 in which the activation data for this study are included. Note that Talmy et al. ' s study is from 1200 to 1500 °C. Therefore, the activation energy used to make the comparison is the one obtained in this study in the lower temperature range studied (1500 -1800 °C). Good agreement between our estimated activation energy and the activation energies of Talmy et al. [27] is shown in Fig. 2b, suggesting that SiC content might have a stronger in /uniFB02 uence on the plastic response of MeB2 (Me = Zr, Hf) UHTCs than the kind of MeB2 analyzed. This statement is supported by the plastic response of 1 wt.% boron-doped SiC in Fig. 2b at 1758 °C, which is similar to the plastic response of HS20 at 1800 °C, more creep resistant than ZS30 at 1800 °C, and shows a strong in /uniFB02 uence of B in the high temperature behavior of SiC. Liquid-phase sintered SiC and additive-free nano β -SiC are more than 3 orders of magnitude more creep resistant than 1 wt.% B-doped SiC at the same temperature ( ∼ 1750 °C) [39,40,43], highlighting the enormous enhancement of creep rate or di /uniFB00 usion rate during plastic deformation by an addition of 1 wt.% B. There is a lack of data about plasticity of monolithic MeB2 to con /uniFB01 rm the role of SiC, but Melendez-Martinez et al. studied the plastic response under compressive creep of ZrB2 from 1400 to 1600 °C [44]. In addition, it was reported that at 1500 °C ZrB2 deforms at ∼ 10 -7 s -1 under 400 MPa, which is ∼ 300 times less resistant than HS20 reported herein in spite of ZrB2 having a porosity of 13.5 vol.% [44].

3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
Concerning the high temperature creep properties of MeB2 reinforced with MeC, there are a couple of studies in /uniFB02 exure for ZrB2-20 vol.% ZrC and TiB2-20 vol.% TiC in addition to other compositions with MeC contents in excess of 40 vol.%, which are far from the interest of this study [45,46]. ZrB2 + 20 vol.% ZrC deforms at 2000 °C in the stress range 5 -30 MPa with a strain rate range of 1.3-8.7 × 10 -3 s -1 respectively and a stress exponent of n=1, suggesting grain boundary sliding as the deformation mechanism. However, the ZrB2-20 vol.% ZrC is nearly 100 -150 times less creep resistant than the ZrB2-20 vol.% ZrC studied by Gangireddy et al. [26] and HS20 from this study. In addition, contamination during milling of up to 6 wt.% with WC-Co was reported, which could explain the poor creep response of ZrB2-20vol.% ZrC studied by Kats et al. [45] as Co melts at 1500 °C. This example illustrates how important it is to minimize contamination as undesired additional second phases can have a detrimental e /uniFB00 ect on physical properties at temperatures of 2000 °C or higher. Furthermore, monolithic ZrB2 and TiB2 were also studied at temperatures higher than 2000 °C [45,46]. However, if an extrapolation is made to 2000 °C, they are 100 times less creep resistant than ZS30 in the best case, which highlights the importance of controlling second phase impurities as well as the need to obtain reliable data on plasticity of monolithic MeB2.

Fig. 3. Comparison of the strain rate versus stress from HS20 with previously reported data.

3.3. HS20La high temperature mechanical properties
Assuming the conditions of temperature and stresses described above, 1800 °C and 400 MPa, and the results from this study (Fig. 8), we suggest (i) HS20 is deforming at 3 × 10 -4 s -1 and (ii) HS20La at 10 -3 s -1 . In addition, plastic deformation at 1800 °C starts at around 4% and 3% for HS20 and HS20La (Fig. 1 and 8), so that at stresses below 400 MPa there will be no dimension changes for 130 s and 30 s respectively. Moreover, 10% deformation will be reached in 330 s and 100 s respectively. In a sharp leading edge component the deformation should not go above 10% under any circumstances due to the negative impact on /uniFB02 ight aerodynamics. Therefore, considering that an atmospheric re-entry lasts around 10 min being at least half of the time under the hardest conditions, i.e. 1800C and 400 MPa in this discussion, only HS20 would be able to withstand it with a deformation below 10% (9% approximately) while HS20La would be far above 20% deformation, mechanically failing before the end of the re-entry. Ideally, the material should not exceed elastic deformation to be reusable from a structural point of view although those materials exceeding the elastic deformation developing a certain shape change such as HS20 might be suitable for single use. Considering other UHTCs studied, ZrB2-30 vol.% SiC deforms at 5 × 10 -4 s -1 , which means that it is slightly out of the secure range de /uniFB01 ned in this study as a 10% deformation and the elastic threshold (4%) will be reached in 200 s and 80s, respectively. ZrB2-20 vol.% SiC samples tested under plasma wind tunneling testing conditions showed the tip of a sharp leading edge was slightly curved and deformed afterwards even if testing times were no longer than 50 s [54]. Note that 1800 °C and 400 MPa are mild re-entry conditions and ideally materials capable of withstanding re-entries with temperatures in excess of 2000 °C are needed in the long term.

3.1. HS20 high temperature mechanical properties
Fig. 1 shows the creep response of HS20 from 1500 °C to 2000 °C approximately over the strain rate range 2.8 × 10 -5 s -1 and 1.4 × 10 -4 s -1 for strains as high as 24%. At 1500 °C (Fig. 1a), HS20 shows a limited ductility (< 6%) for strain rates ε ˙ <6×10 -5 s -1 . At higher strain rates, the deformation is increased to nearly 12% and signi /uniFB01 cant softening is also observed. Deformation-induced softening could be related to either cavitation or phase instabilities. In addition, true steady states of deformation are only observed at 1900 °C or higher temperatures as softening is still active at 1800 °C. Stresses for the steady states of deformation were calculated through extrapolation of steady state to the initiation of the plastic regime at 1900 °C and 2000 °C, as plotted in Fig. 2a. Calculated stress exponents for the steady states of plastic deformation are shown in Fig. 2a, ranging from 3 to 1 as the temperature increases from 1500 °C to 2000 °C. In addition, calculated activation energies are 242 and 334 kJ/mol in the temperature ranges 1500 -1800 °C and 1900 -2000 °C respectively, suggesting the activation of di /uniFB00 erent deformation mechanisms as the stress exponent also changes from 3 to 1 in these same temperature ranges. The last deformation state in Fig. 1c and 1d is not a true stationary as some softening is observed. Therefore, the stress exponent for this last deformation state might not be accurate and this state is not considered for the plot of stationary strain rates versus stress, so the stress exponent shown in Fig. 2a is more accurate than the one displayed for the last deformation state in Fig. 1c and 1d respectively. In addition, no true stationaries were observed at 1800C either, but the data points were considered to allow comparison with previously reported data. The satisfactory agreement of the calculated activation energy value with literature data in Fig. 2b suggests that it was a reasonable assumption.

3.3. HS20La high temperature mechanical properties
2 wt.% addition of La to HS20 (HS20La) helps reduce the internal stresses produced during cooling in the SPS without altering mechanical properties at RT, such as strength and toughness [19]. In addition, La addition stabilizes the thermal conductivity in the temperature range from 1500 °C to 1900 °C due to a compensation of CTE mismatch between SiC and HfB2 as La2O3 is located next to SiC particles [6], which could be an advantage for designers when calculating UHTCs performance [3]. Moreover, La2O3 addition stabilizes the long-term response during oxidation at 1500 and 1600 °C due to the formation of Hf-O-C particles which slow down growth of the oxide protective layer [11]. Therefore, unlike HS20 HS20La could be a good candidate for hypersonic applications. However, Fig. 8 reveals that the plastic response is far from being satisfactory for several reasons; (i) There is a sudden softening after the end of elastic deformation suggesting development of structural damage with the plastic deformation, (ii) the lack of a true deformation stationary as softening is observed under any of the conditions studied, and (iii) HS20La is even less creep resistant than HS20 (Fig. 8b). There was no attempt to calculate activation energies as no true stationary state was observed under any condition. In addition, the stress exponents given in Fig. 8 are only indicative to enable a quick comparison with HS20 but they were not calculated with the intention of suggesting a deformation mechanism.

A B S T R A C T
The composition that has received most attention to date and is currently considered as a baseline UHTCs composition for hypersonic TPS development is SiC-reinforced UHTCs, with a SiC content ranging from 10 to 30 vol.% [2,9]. Oxidation of SiC produces an outer BS protective glass that could protect UHTCs components up to ∼ 1650 °C, at which temperature pure SiO2 melts and bulk UHTCs would be exposed to continuous oxidation. Although the BS coating melts at lower temperatures it could withstand temperatures up to 2000 °C for up to 1 h under static oxidation conditions [12 -17]. However, this liquid layer will be blown away during a hypersonic reentry.
On the other hand, hypersonic applications require high strength at high temperature and estimated conditions for hypersonic re-entry are ∼ 1800 °C and ∼ 400 MPa [3]. Furthermore, the latter conditions are at the less harsh end of reentry conditions and in the long term, the

3.3.4. HS20La
HS20La behaves similar to ZS20La, with the difference that it is not more oxidation resistant than HS20 at 1400 ◦ C due to lower content of B2O3in the BS layer in HfB2-based UHTCs. However, the presence of SiC agglomerates did not affect the stability of its behavior at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C, which is protective as shown in Table 1 and Fig. 2. In addition, the oxide layer thickness kinetic exponent at 1600 ◦ C is smaller than ZS20La, which could be related to either the presence of SiC agglomerates, the lower oxidation rates of HfB2-based UHTCs compared to ZrB2-based UHTCs or a lack of evidence of BS melt phase in the oxide layer. Furthermore, the formation of different HfO x C y phases was detected by EDS. Fig. 10a shows the oxide scale layer of HS20La after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C for 3 h, in which an area near the top surface layer and oxide layer interface with unoxidized volume are magnified in Fig. 10b and c, respectively. Oxidation mechanisms are slightly different than in ZS20La because in HS20La SiC gradually oxidized and there are small SiC particles (0.5 -2 𝛍 m) clearly visible at the interface (Fig. 10b). In addition, a new oxide phase, HfO3, was detected. A BS image (Fig. 11), reveals different contrast between HfO2 and HfO3 as well as with La-SiO x C y particles, small particles of HfOxCy (5 - 10 𝛍 m) with high (85 atomic %) C content and small (0.5 - 2 𝛍 m)SiCparticles in the oxide layer. question: What was the mass ablation rate of the Ren 2017 ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating with HfC nanowires after 90 seconds of oxyacetylene torch ablation?
answer: The mass ablation rate of the Ren 2017 ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating with HfC nanowires after 90 seconds of oxyacetylene torch ablation was -0.12 mg/s [1][3].

chunks.text:
a b s t r a c t
To improve the ablation resistance of the coated carbon/carbon composites, HfC nanowires acting as reinforcement materials were introduced into ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating. Synthesis, morphology, mechanical properties and ablation resistance of the coating were researched. A porous HfC nanowire layer was uniformly covered on SiC coating. Both of the toughness and interface bonding strength of ZrB2-SiC coating with HfC nanowires were higher than those of the coating without HfC nanowires. HfC nanowires could effectively inhibit the cracking and peeling of outer coating during ablation. After oxyacetylene torch ablation for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of the coated sample without and with HfC nanowires was 0.20 mg/s and -0.12 mg/s, respectively.
© 2017 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
on C/C composites through three-step process including pressing, pyrolysis and reactive silicon in /uniFB01 ltration, and then analyzed the ablation performance of the coating. Previous research proved that ZrB2-SiC multiphase coating could prevent C/C composites from ablation in severe environment. Among all of the preparation methods, SAPS is a very high-ef /uniFB01 ciency and low-cost method to prepare a dense ZrB2-SiC coating [14,15]. The thickness and composition of the coating could be controlled by adjusting the spraying parameters.

3.3. Ablation behavior of the coatings
Ablation performance of the coatings was measured under oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame. After being ablated for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of pure ZrB2-SiC/SiC coated C/C sample is 0.20 mg/s. While, the mass ablation rate of the coated sample with HfC nanowires is positive ( -0.12 mg/s). During ablation, on the one hand, the coating would gain weight because of its oxidation; on the other hand, mechanical denudation and volatilization of oxide layer would lead to weight loss. By incorporating nanowires, the weight gain of the sample is predominant, so the mass ablation rate is minus. Above results imply that HfC nanowires could inhibit the oxidized layer being denudated, which could reduce the mass loss of the oxide layer.
XRDpattern of the coating with HfC nanowires after ablation for 90 s is shown in Fig. 8. After ablation, ZrO2 phase emerges and the ZrB2 and SiC phases disappear. In ablation environment, ZrB2 would be oxidized to ZrO2 and B2O3, and SiC would be oxidized to SiO2, SiO, CO and CO2. After ablation, only ZrO2 remained on the sample surface owing to its high melting point (2953 K). SiO2, SiO, B2O3 and other gaseous products wouldbeevaporated /uniFB01 ercely in ablation environment, so they could not be found in the XRD pattern.

4. Conclusions
HfC nanowires acting as reinforcement materials for ZrB2-SiC/SiC multilayer coating were synthesized by CVD. Hardness, elastic modulus and fracture toughness of the outer coating were all improved because of the incorporation of HfC nanowires. After incorporating HfC nanowires, the interface bonding strength between inner and outer coating increased from 5.9 to 7.5 N. During ablation test, HfC nanowires could inhibit the cracking and peeling of outer coating, resulting in an improvement of ablation resistance of the coating. After ablation for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of ZrB2-SiC/SiC coated sample with HfC nanowires decreased from 0.20 to -0.12 mg/s.

3.3.  Ablation test results
The results of ablation test including weight change percentage (ΔW %), mass ablation rate (Rm), thickness change (% ∆l) and linear ablation rate (Rl) after the test for single-layer SiC and double-layer C/SiC/ZrB2 coatings are presented in Table 2. In order to investigate the performance of the coatings at different temperatures, they exposed to oxypropane and oxyacetylene flame separately. Results show that in the oxyacetylene flame test, the ablation rate of samples is higher than the oxypropane flame. It could be attributed to the higher temperature of oxyacetylene  flame  (about  3000  °C)  in  comparison  with  oxypropane flame (about 2200 °C). On the other hand, in both oxyacetylene and oxypropane  tests,  ablation  rate  has  raised  by  increasing  the  testing time. Considering the data in Table 2, the ablation rate of SiC coating under oxypropane flame after 60s is 5.28 × 10 -4 and the ablation rate of the SiC/ZrB2  coating after 300 s is only 0.426 × 10 -4 . This shows that, the double layer SiC/ZrB2 coating has a higher ablation resistance compared with single-layer SiC coating.

Fig. 11. SEM image and EDS analysis from SiC coating after ablation test.

3.2. Ablation properties of Cf/ZrC -SiC composites in oxyacetylene torch
An  ablation  test  of  Cf/ZrC -SiC  composites  is  conducted  using  a custom built oxyacetylene torch for 60 s and the mass loss and erosion depth are measured to be 0.52 g (mass ablation rate of 8.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 g/s) and  0.66 mm  (linear  ablation  rate  of  11 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 mm/s),  respectively. After oxyacetylene torch test, the ablated surface of Cf/ZrC -SiC composites can be divided into three zones from center to external based on the appearances, i.e. central pit (zone 1 in Fig. 3c), oxidized zone (zone 2 in Fig. 3c) and unaffected zone (zone 3 in Fig. 3c), corresponding well with  the  distribution  of  oxyacetylene  torch  temperature  (inset  in Fig. 3a). As far as the oxyacetylene torch is concerned, it involves high heat flux (~4 MW/m 2 ), high flow velocity ( > 150 m/s), high temperature (~3000 ◦ C), high heating/cooling rates (500 -1000 ◦ C/s) and severe  oxidizing  environment  (high  mole  fractions  of  molecular  and atomic oxygen) [20]. Nevertheless, both the flow velocity and torch temperature decrease sharply away from the flame core, i.e. in the radial directions. In our case, the distance between torch exit and specimen surface was 10 mm. At this location, the flame core has the high flow velocity and temperatures (~3000 ◦ C) and erodes the materials surface like a drill, leaving not only the ablated pit but also the splashing ZrO2 droplet (Fig. 3c). However, the flame has extreme radial gradients of temperature and velocity (Fig. 3a, b). Therefore, the outer oxidized zone experiences lower temperatures (1800 -2200 ◦ C) and much lower flow velocity, so this zone is severely oxidized but not eroded.

question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2014, Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) was used to process ZS20 and ZS20La, but the resulting microstructure showed that La2O3 location affected SiC particle dispersion. How did the proximity of La2O3 to SiC particles influence the homogeneity of the microstructure in the La2O3-doped composition?
answer: The proximity of La2O3 to SiC particles in the La2O3-doped composition (ZS20La) influenced the homogeneity of the microstructure by promoting a more effective relaxation of elastic strains during SPS cooling, which subsequently reduced the fracture toughness compared to ZS20. This is because the higher thermal expansion coefficient of La2O3 (8-10 × 10^-6 K^-1) leads to more rapid shrinkage during cooling, providing free volume to SiC particles [3]. However, the sources do not explicitly discuss how this proximity influenced the homogeneity of the microstructure in terms of SiC dispersion or agglomeration.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ =6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously. 10 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, as shown in previous work 10 and Fig. 1, are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density); (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ = 10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ = 3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma-Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2, ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2, HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described in a previous work. 22 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, previously described, 22 are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density) except monolithic ZrB2 which has a density ∼ 95% as a consequence of O impurities which prevent its full densification; (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

2. Experimental procedure
HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 μ m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( α -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 μ m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 μ m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously [10]. The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs [11,19] are; (I) high density (> 99% of theoretical relative density); (II) HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 μ m; and (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles and improve the SiC dispersion throughout the HfB2 matrix.

3.1. Microstructure and composition of the as-fabricated composites
The volume fractions of ZrB2, SiC and La2O3 phases is determined as normalized volume fractions excluding open porosity which are illustrated in Fig. 2 a .  Due to the content of La2O3 in the composites is limited, X-Ray diffraction peaks of La2O3 are not detected in Fig. 2 b . Besides, the SiC formed by PCS at 1100 ℃ is  amorphous phase which cannot be detected by XRD in Fig. 2 b . The density and open porosity of the composites were determined and shown in Table 1.
Take sample ZS 4.6 L as an example, the microstructure and element distribution of the composites are characterized by SEM and EDS and illustrated in Fig. 3. The sample ZS 4.6 L shows a similar microstructure feature with ZS which has a multi-layer structure [27,28]. The SI method is efficient to introduce the ZrB2-La2O3  powders into the inter-bundle area of the fiber fabrics. The intra-bundle area is difficult to fill up by ZrB2-La2O3  during SI due to the impeding of the carbon fibers. Therefore,  ZrB2-La2O3 mainly  concentrates  in  the  inter-bundle  area.  By contrast, the SiC matrix can be introduced into the intra-bundle area effectively during the PIP process, due to the PCS precursor is liquid. Therefore, the La2O3 modified Cf/ZrB2-SiC composites present a specific multi-layer structure. Otherwise, the La2O3  covered by SiC phase and distributes in inter-bundle area which prevents the contact and reaction among La2O3, PyC interface and carbon fibers. The PyC interface and fibers remain complete without clear damage.

3. Results
All samples attained high density values, higher than 99% of theoretical density (TD) except ZrB2 which was 95.8%. Theoretical density was calculated using the rule of mixtures. Table 2 shows SPS conditions and density values measured after sintering. Phase analysis by XRD confirmed all starting phases remained in UHTCs after SPS.
Fig. 1 shows secondary electron (SE) images of ground, polished and etched UHTCs surfaces after SPS. ZrB2 are the larger grey grains (2-6 𝛍 m), finer dark grains are SiC (1-2 𝛍 m) and light grey grains are La2O3 ( ∼ 1 𝛍 m) difficult to distinguish from ZrB2 grains, which are, respectively, labelled with 1, 2, and 3 in Fig. 1c as they were confirmed with EDS. Homogeneous dispersion of SiC in ZS20 and SiC/La2O3 in ZS20La is found in the ZrB2 matrix. SiC and La2O3 are usually located together forming small agglomerates as an interconnected network in the ZrB2 and HfB2 matrix. However, less than 5% of SiC and La2O3 particles were detected separately. On the other hand, SiC agglomerates as large as 20-30 𝛍 m were found in HfB2-based composites as seen in Fig. 1b indicated by an arrow.

The header and byline of this document reads: Available online at www.sciencedirect.com Journal of the European Ceramic Society 34 (2014) 3535-3548 Effect of La2O3 addition on long-term oxidation kinetics of ZrB2-SiC and HfB2-SiC ultra-high temperature ceramics E. Zapata-Solvas a , ∗ , D.D. Jayaseelan a , P.M. Brown b , W.E. Lee a a Centre for Advanced Structural Ceramics, Imperial College London, SW7 2AZ, UK b Dstl, Porton Down, Salisbury, Wiltshire SP4 0JQ, UK Received 4 May 2014; received in revised form 28 May 2014; accepted 2 June 2014 Available online 1 July 2014

4. Discussion
XRD analysis indicated all starting materials remained in UHTC composites after SPS and new phases were not formed during sintering. Monolithic UHTCs had the largest grains (Table 3) owing to the higher temperature needed for SPS, 2000 ◦ C for ZrB2 instead of 1950 ◦ C required to sinter ZS20 and ZS20La, 2100 ◦ C for HfB2 instead of 2000 ◦ C for HS20 and HS20La, as well as the longer dwelling time. In addition, ZS20, ZS20La and HS20, HS20La had similar matrix grain sizes (4 𝛍 m and 6 𝛍 m) because of the similar temp/time profile used in processing. With regard to the secondary phase dispersion, hard agglomerates of SiC and La2O3 were not observed in ZrB2based UHTCs. However, in La-doped UHTCs SiC and La2O3 particles tended to remain in close proximity as expected when two particles of similar but small size are mixed with a larger one. The presence of SiC hard agglomerates (20-30 𝛍 m) in HfB2based UHTCs is attributed to the density difference between HfB2 (10.5 g/cm 3 ) and SiC (3.217 g/cm 3 ) which is double than in ZrB2-based UHTCs and makes SiC float in the ethanol solution during thermo-rotary drying. Use of higher solution viscosity or addition of a dispersant to avoid the presence of strong agglomerates after sintering is worthy of investigation.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited what high-temperature strength at 1800°C, and how did this compare to the undoped version?
answer: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited a high-temperature strength of 390 ± 21 MPa at 1800°C. This compared to the undoped version (ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2), which had a high-temperature strength of 34 ± 6 MPa at 1800°C [6][7].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.
be prepared at a lower sintering temperature of ~1600 ◦ C [19,23 -25].

3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
Fig. 7 shows the 1800 ◦ C-flexural strength versus sintering temperature of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics with and without C dopant, compared with several typical ZrB2  and HfB2-based UHTCs. In the previous studies, in order to obtain superior high-temperature properties, some refractory materials have been considered to be added into the matrix. The most widely  used  refractory  phases  were  ZrC  [15,40]  and  SiC  [13,38].  In addition, high-temperature resistant phases such as WC [41 -43], B4C [44],  Mo2C  [45]  and  SiB6 [46]  were  also  considered.  For  superior strength at ultra-high temperatures, it is generally not recommended to employ sintering additives with low melting point. Consequently, the sintering temperature of these reported ZrB2-based UHTCs with superior high-temperature strength typically exceeds 1800 ◦ C, resulting in significant energy expenditure. In contrast, introducing sintering additives with low melting point into ZrB2 ceramics can significantly reduce the densification  temperature,  however  the  residual  lower  melting  point phases would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs (e.g., the strength of ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2 at 1800 ◦ C is only 30 MPa). In the current work, active carbon was added into the ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic exhibited excellent flexural strength (390 MPa) at 1800 ◦ C, surpassing that of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC [15,40], ZrB2-30 vol% SiC [38], HfB2-20 vol% SiB6  [46], and HfB2-5 vol% SiC-5 vol% WC [41]. Meanwhile, the densification temperature of C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2

1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics.  Thus,  there  is  a
* Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Aerospace Vehicles of Liaoning Province, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, China. E-mail address: yfzu@dlut.edu.cn (Y. Zu).
1 Present Address: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, China

4. Conclusion
In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
High-temperature flexural strength of the C-doped ZrB2-based UHTC (ZC1600-2h)  was  tested  at  1800 ◦ C  under  a  vacuum  condition.  For comparison, a ZrB2-based UHTC with unconverted sintering additive (ZrB2-15 vol% ZrSi2, labeled as ZS15) was also tested under the same conditions. 3.3. High-temperature flexural strength
ZS15 at 1800 ◦ C under vacuum. The insert images (Fig. 6(b and c)) present the macrographs of the specimens after flexural tests. From the stress-strain  curves,  the  load  of  ZC1600-2h  increased  almost  linearly until reaching a maximum value, and then sharply decreased after the final  brittle  fracture.  As  shown  in  Fig.  6(b),  it  confirmed  that  the ZC1600-2h  specimen  was  broken  into  two  pieces  without  obvious plastic deformation (Fig. 6(b)). In contrast, the ZS15 specimen yielded at only approximately 30 MPa, and then steadily deformed with a large strain at a low stress of 30 -34 MPa. The low yield strength was attributed to the presence of Zr -Si phases with a low-melting point. It has been reported that the low melting point phases would significantly reduce the  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based  UHTCs  [38,39].  After flexural test, the ZS15 specimen was obviously bent and not completely broken into two pieces, as displayed in Fig. 6(c). The high-temperature flexural strength of ZC1600-2h was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was an order of  magnitude higher than ZS15 (34 ± 6 MPa), as shown in Table 2. Compared  to  its  room-temperature  strength  of  494 ± 2  MPa,  the high-temperature strength retention ratio (the ratio of 1800 ◦ C-strength to RT-strength) of ZC1600-2h was as high as ~79%, which was much higher than that of ZS15 (~6%).

Notably, the use of pitch fibre in the same study has led to a flexural strength of 260 ± 50 MPa, which is somewhat comparable to the values obtained for the flexural tests in the current research. On the other hand, Zhang et al. [47] have reported a significant increase in the flexural strength of nano-slurry infiltrated and hot-pressed 2D-Cf-ZrB2-SiC composite from 196 ± 14 MPa to 234 ± 18 MPa through the heat treatment of Cf at 1800 ◦ C. [23] have obtained a flexural strength value of 199 ± 26 MPa, which is somewhat comparable to the range of σ f values obtained in the present work.

4.2.2. Mechanical behavior
The flexural strength of hot pressed ZrB2 tested between room temperature and 1600 ◦ C showed a value of 360 MPa at room temperature decreasing to a minimum of 170 MPa. The same ceramic when tested in Ar  between  1500 ◦ C  and  2300 ◦ C  raised  its  strength  to  300  MPa  at 2200 ◦ C, which then lowered down to 220 MPa at 2300 ◦ C [151]. A historic study by Rhodes [143] demonstrated the increase in bending strength  of  ZrB2  from  325  MPa  at  room  temperature  to  420  MPa  at 800 ◦ C. Further, increase in temperature reduced the strength to 145 MPa at 1400 ◦ C.  Bend strength again raised to 200 MPa at 1900 ◦ C followed by a drop at 2200 ◦ C to 50 MPa. The initial rise in bend strength was ascribed to the thermal residual stresses relieving at higher temperatures. The second rise in bend strength was attributed to the plastic flow from creep, again relieving the stresses [143]. ZrB2  ceramic composite (with 5 vol % WC + 3 vol % SiC) developed by Silvestroni et al. [152] retained a flexural strength of 836 MPa at 1800 ◦ C. The elevated temperature bend strength of some ZrB2  and HfB2  based ceramics is illustrated  in  Fig.  18.  Particulate  reinforced  di-borides  especially  SiC
reinforced composites display improved fracture toughness up to ~6 MPam 1/2 , hardness ~26 GPa and flexural strength ~1000 MPa [136] (while most others have exhibited flexural strength of ~650 MPa), while retaining flexural strength at high temperatures (~850 MPa at 1500 ◦ C).

4. Discussion
ceramics studied to date (217 MPa at 1800 ◦ C) for matrix grain size of 8 𝛍 m. However, plastic deformation occurred at 1800 ◦ C for matrix grain sizes lower than 5 𝛍 m. Elastic behaviour with relatively high strength was found with increasing ZrB2 grain size introducing a microstructural tailoring factor that controls the high temperature mechanical response. A systematic study of grain size effect and SiC content on high temperature strength of ZrB2 based ceramics is required to understand the mechanical responses and failure mechanisms of these systems. In addition, this is an effect that has been characterised for room temperature strength. The effect of sintering method on mechanical properties has been analysed. Pressureless sintered ZrB2-20 MoSi2 had higher strength at 1500 ◦ C than hot-pressed ZrB2-20 MoSi2 while the room temperature strengths remained similar. In addition, hot-pressed ZrB2-15 MoSi2 and SPSed ZrB2-15 MoSi2 have similar room and high temperature strength. This effect is explained in terms of the secondary phases formed during hot-pressing and SPS of these materials, such as SiO2 and ZrO2 which soften the grain boundaries at high temperatures, especially the residual silica pockets. The addition of carbon to the system avoided the residual silica pocket formation during hot-pressing increasing substantially high temperature strength of ZrB2-15 MoSi2. In the present study, the general trend is that HfB2-based UHTCs had higher flexural strength at high temperature than ZrB2-based UHTCs (Tables 6 and 7). The strength at 1400 ◦ C of ZS20 and ZS20La and HS20 and HS20La is approximately twice that of ZrB2 and HfB2, 37 respectively. The reduction of the strength at 1400 ◦ C in the La2O3-doped UHTCs could be attributed to La2O3 inducing grain boundary softening.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), how did the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolve from 1350°C to 1600°C during hot-press sintering, and what was the final state of the Zr-Si-C phases?
answer: In Liu et al. (2023), the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolved as follows during hot-press sintering: at 1350°C, the polished surface of the ceramic was quite rough with a lot of pores, and the loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C was transformed into a porous Zr-Si-C cluster [1]. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450°C, the pores became less, and the Zr-Si-C cluster precipitated spheres with a diameter of ~85 nm [1]. After hot-sintering at 1600°C for 30 min, the Zr-Si-C cluster transformed into a core-shell phase, with the core being carbide enriched Zr and the shell being carbide enriched Si [1]. The final state of the Zr-Si-C phases after sintering at 1600°C for 2 h was fully dense, with ZrSi2 almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC [4].

3.1. Phase composition and microstructure
Fig. 3 shows the typical morphologies on the polished surface of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at different temperatures. As can be seen in Fig. 3(a), the polished surface of ZC1350 was quite rough with a lot of pores. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450 ◦ C, the pores became less (Fig. 3(c)). In comparison, the polished surfaces of ZC1600 and ZC1600-2h were flat with almost no pores (Fig. 3(e, g)). Fig.  3(b,  d,  f,  h)  shows  the  high  magnification  images  to  reveal  the morphological  evolution.  After  hot-sintering  at  1350 ◦ C,  the  loose mixture of ZrSi2 and C was transformed into a porous Zr -Si -C cluster, as marked by the dotted ellipse in Fig. 3(b). Based on the reaction between the siliceous component and C (Reaction 4 -6), the Zr -Si -C cluster may contain ZrSi2,  ZrSi,  Si,  ZrC,  and  SiC.  It  is  difficult  to  distinguish  the various phases. With the sintering temperature increasing to 1450 ◦ C, the  Zr -Si -C cluster  precipitated  spheres  with  a  diameter  of  ~85  nm (Fig. 3(d)). After hot-sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 30 min, as shown in Fig. 3 (f), the Zr -Si -C cluster tfransformed into a core-shell phase (as marked by the dotted lines). Based on the micro-area analysis (inserted image in Fig. 3(f)), the core was carbide enriched Zr, and the shell was carbide enriched Si. The reactivity of Zr species with carbonaceous materials is higher than that of Si species, as can be confirmed in the erosion of carbon  fiber  by  molten  ZrSi2 [35,36],  tending  to  form  a  similar core-shell  structure  [37]. 4. Conclusion
In  order  to  achieve  low-temperature  preparation  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon was doped into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  to prepare ZrB2-based ceramics via in situ reactive sintering.  Effect  of  active  carbon  on  phase composition, microstructure, densification  behavior  and  high-temperature  strength  of  ZrB2-based UHTCs  was  investigated.  The  main  conclusions  are  summarized  as follows:
- (1)  With the sintering temperature increasing, ZrSi2 was successively transformed by active carbon into Zr -Si -C clusters, Zr -Si -C coreshell phases, and nano-sized SiC/ZrC particles.
- (2)  Given the densification behavior and microstructure evolution, two rapid densification stages occurred during the in-situ reactive sintering process, due to the softening of ZrSi2 and liquidation of Si intermediate product, respectively. After sintering at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, a fully dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained.
- (3)  The high-temperature strength (1800 ◦ C) and strength retention ratio  of  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic  were  390 ± 21 MPa and 79%.  The  excellent  high-temperature  mechanical  properties could be attributed to the transformation of sintering additives into refractory phases.

2.2. Hot-press sintering
The  ball-milled  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders  were  hot  pressed  at 1350 -1600 ◦ C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement,  load  and  temperature  were  recorded  using  TestMark (Fangrui  Technology  Co.,  Ltd.,  Changchun,  China)  to  analyze  the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate  of  10 ◦ C/min and then held for 30 min. When the temperature reached above 800 ◦ C, the uniaxial load began to be applied at a cross speed of 1 mm/min. After reaching 40 MPa, the uniaxial pressure was automatically maintained. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦ C, 1450 ◦ C, 1600 ◦ C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600,  respectively.  The  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics  sintered  at 1600 ◦ C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350 -1600 ◦ C following the same sintering process mentioned above for comparison. The as-sintered ZrSi2 ceramics were labeled as Z1350, Z1450, Z1600.

1. Introduction
Sintering additive of ZrSi2  is beneficial to low-temperature densification of ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), nevertheless, residual ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of UHTCs. In the current work, to achieve low-temperature preparation of ZrB2-based UHTCs with good high-temperature strength, active carbon (C) was added into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramics to convert the sintering additive into refractory phases of SiC/ZrC. C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders were hot-pressed at different temperatures to investigate the phase composition, microstructure and densification behavior. TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦ C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. In addition, the high-temperature (1800 ◦ C) strength of Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was much higher than that of the C-undoped one. The excellent high-temperature mechanical properties could be attributed to the transformation of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.

Notably, the enthalpy change calculated from the peak area during 1200 -1400 ◦ C for mixed powders was 1127 J/g, which was much larger than 403 J/g of the pure ZrSi2 powders. Fig.  2  shows X-ray diffraction patterns of the C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 powders  and  as-sintered  C-doped  ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics.  As  shown  in Fig. 2, for the powders, the diffraction peaks of ZrSi2  and ZrB2  phases were clearly detected, while C was difficult to identify due to its amorphous state. After sintering at 1350 -1600 ◦ C, besides the primary ZrB2 phase,  the  diffraction  peaks  of  ZrC  and  SiC  appeared  with  the disappearance of ZrSi2  peaks, indicating the occurrence of the in-situ reactions mentioned above. With the sintering temperature increased, the intensity of ZrC and SiC diffraction peaks gradually increased, which implied the in-situ reactions were gradually deepening during 1350 -1600 ◦ C.

3.1. Phase composition and microstructure
In order to understand phase evolution during the in-situ reactive sintering, heat and mass changes of the mixed powders during heating process,  phase  composition  and  microstructure  of  the  as-sintered  Cdoped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics were successively investigated. Fig. 1 shows the curves of heat change vs. temperature and mass change vs. temperature for the mixed C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2  powders. As can be seen in Fig. 1, three distinguished exothermic peaks were observed. The first exothermic peak was found at around 921 ◦ C, which may be caused by the exothermic coverage of the reaction between ZrSi2 and carbon. The second  and  third  exothermic  peaks  were  located  closely  at  around 1311 ◦ C and 1347 ◦ C, which should be related to two different reactions at 1300 -1400 ◦ C. It is well known that ZrSi2 becomes unstable with the temperature up to near its melting temperature. The formation enthalpy of ZrSi2 and ZrSi were GLYPH<0> 60 kJ/mol and GLYPH<0> 90 kJ/mol, respectively [34]. 3.1.1. ZrC-B4C-Si (R1) series
As was shown in our previous work [16], the densification of the ZrC-B4C-Si  powder  mixture  during  hot  pressing  (Fig.  6a)  proceeds through three main stages. The first stage, providing about 30 % of the overall  sample  shrinkage, occurs  during  the sample  heating between 1000 ◦ C and 1300 ◦ C, alongside the precursor phase content decreasing by about 50 % and reaction (1) products (ZrB2 and SiC) appearing. This stage was explained by the reaction-induced powder mobility, arising from the disappearance of the initial phase grains (B4C, ZrC, and Si) alongside  the  formation  of  new  ones  (ZrB2,  SiC,  and  graphite).  The shrinkage stops at approximately 1300 ◦ C, and the sample height remains  constant  up  to  1550 ◦ C.  During  this  second  stage,  called the plateau ,  reaction  (1) is  completed  by  about  90 %,  meaning  that  the precursor phase content is now 10 % as compared to the beginning. As we can see from Fig. 6, presenting the material structure before the start of the first stage (Fig. 6b) and at the end of the second stage (Fig. 6c), the reaction (1) leads to a significant structure refinement with most of the grains after the second stage significantly less than 0.5 µ m. It should be noted that stratification was not observed either after the first or the second stage. The third stage began at 1600 ◦ C, with shrinkage occurring even faster than in the first stage, and continued until 1850 ◦ C. In [16], we suggested that the rapid consolidation at this stage originated from new phase plastic deformation and was facilitated by the rapid growth of nanostructured nuclei, the presence of molten silicon remnants, and the completion  of  reaction  (1).  This  is  the  stage  when  the  stratification occurs.

In order to simultaneously achieve low-temperature preparation and good high-temperature resistance, it is needed to remove the residual ZrSi2 from the final ZrB2-based ceramic. In the previous studies [32,33], we found that introducing carbonaceous material into ZrB2 -ZrSi2  can reduce the amount of residual ZrSi2  in the final materials, due to the reaction between carbonaceous material and sintering additive of ZrSi2. However, the effects of sintering additive transformation on the densification  process,  phase  composition,  microstructure  and  mechanical properties are still unclear.
In the current study, active carbon was introduced into ZrB2 -ZrSi2 to prepare high-temperature-resistant ZrB2-based ceramics at a relatively low temperature via in situ reactive sintering. The C-doped ZrB2 -ZrSi2 powders  were  hot-pressed  at  different  sintering  temperatures.  Phase composition, microstructure, densification behavior and hightemperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs were systematically investigated, in order to clarify the effects of active carbon on the densification and high-temperature properties of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics.

Reaction Path and Synthesis Mechanism
An initial series of sintering experiments examined the reactive path and synthesis mechanism of the Zr-B 4 C-Si system without using SHS reactions to obtain densified products. The processing parameters and final phase compositions corresponding to chemical reaction (10) after a series of pressureless heat treatments were shown in Table 1. Stacked XRD patterns of samples prepared under the different heat treatment conditions were presented in Figure 3. In sample 1, apart from Zr, B 4 C and Si in raw powders, X-ray diffraction also identified cubic ZrC 1-x and ZrO 2 phases. The ZrO 2 phase was attributed to the oxidation of Zr powder in air and ZrO 2 milling balls. The presence of ZrC 1-x indicated that the reaction between Zr and B 4 C commenced at 800 °C and ZrC 1-x formed preferentially, which was inconsistent with the results of thermodynamic calculations (Figure 2). The formation of ZrC as shown above was mainly due to non-stoichiometric ZrC 1-x , although the Gibbs energy of ZrB 2 was lower than that of ZrC. There was no apparent difference between the XRD patterns of samples 1 and 2. However, it was found that the 1 h hold at 800 °C delayed the hot explosion temperature from 980 to 1050 °C under a 10 °C/min heating rate. This phenomenon demonstrated that many more atoms diffused during the temperature hold, especially between Zr/C and Zr/B.

question: Huang et al. 2016 thermally cycle ZrB2-SiC and ZrC-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites over a specified hot-and-cold temperature range simulating low-Earth orbit, while Borrelli et al. 2009 assume an initial temperature at the start of the Nose_2 re-entry trajectory in their FEM model. Which is colder: the low end of Huang's thermal shock range, or Borrelli's assumed initial re-entry temperature of 300 K?
answer: The low end of Huang's thermal shock range is -100 °C (or 173 K) [16], while Borrelli's assumed initial re-entry temperature is 300 K. Therefore, the low end of Huang's thermal shock range is colder.

This study aims to study thermal shock behavior of the coated composites. The results show the degradation of /uniFB02 exural strength without obvious variation, and the fracture mode and pull-out mechanism are also changed. The composition of coatings is characterized by XRD, SEM and XPS. The decrease of /uniFB02 exural strength during the tests is due to the debonding of the coating-matrix interfaces. Compared to uncoated C/SiC composites, the UHTCs based coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.

2.2. Atmospheric Re-entry
Starting at a 120 km altitude and an initial ~ Mach 25 velocity, NASA space shuttles were required to end missions by landing on a runway 91 m wide [33], - a challenge for blunt-shaped bodies. The accuracy of a body during re-entry is related to leading edge geometry. Sharp geometries have higher ballistic coefficients (BC) and lower drag forces/coefficients than blunt geometries. However, as BC increases, so too do maximum heating rates. If a material can withstand ultra-high temperatures, the BC of the vehicle can be increased via sharper, more streamlined geometries that allow several advantages, like significantly altering the altitude of maximum deceleration. The lower the altitude of deceleration, the shorter the amount of time spent in the atmosphere, or the more rapidly the vehicle arrives at the surface of the Earth. The benefits of a high BC compared to a low BC are listed in Table 2.
Opeka et al. indicated the 500 ◦ -2000 ◦ C range as being the necessary materials window for hypersonics [34]. Technically, hypersonic velocity is defined as any speed exceeding the speed of sound, but is typically more  specifically  defined  as  beginning  at  Mach  5,  or  in  the  range 1702-3403 m/s. Hypersonic velocities are less demanding the further from the Earth ' s atmosphere due to decreasing air density. The Space Shuttle re-entering the Earth ' s  atmosphere at nearly Mach 25 in low earth  orbit  is  a  substantially  different  thermal,  aerodynamic,  and oxidation scenario than Mach 25 a few kilometers above the surface.
As discussed earlier, the maximum usage temperature for any SiC based thermal protection system (TPS) is ~1727 ◦ C and for comparative purposes, SiC is viewed as protective up to1727 ◦ C. Over a temperature range of 300-1700 °C, the optimized coating containing 15 vol% TaB2 extended the initial oxidation temperature from 300 °C to 500 °C, reducing dynamic oxidation loss by 75.85% compared to the 60HfB2-40MoSi2 coating. During dynamic oxidation testing from 573 K to 1773 K, the gradient coating demonstrated enhanced mass retention, improving from 62.9% (pure SiC coating) to 97.94%.

The  existing  thermogravimetric  and  wide  temperature  range  oxidation tests have verified the nonlinear relationship between the selfhealing ability of coating oxide films under temperature gradients and temperature. However, the linear temperature change mode is difficult to  reproduce  the  transient  competition  mechanism  of  thermal  shock and oxidation coupling during space reentry, which limits the accuracy of  predicting  the  oxygen  diffusion  path  of  coatings  under  extreme thermal gradients.

THERMAL SHOCK RESISTANCE
After the recent tragic explosion of Space Shuttle Columbia, the findings of the Columbia Accident Investigation Board and the recent Space Exploration Initiative suggest that safety be made the top priority in the design of new reentry vehicles (Wang et al., 2007). During reentry from Low Earth Orbit (LEO), such as from the Space Station or parking orbits, relatively high speeds (on the order of 8 km/s) are reached (Monteverde et al., 2007). External thermal protection systems of reusable space vehicles, in the form of rigid surfaces in areas of ultra-high working temperatures (beyond 1800 °C), must be able to withstand high temperatures, high heat flux and mechanical stresses associated with vibrations at launch and re-entry in Earth's atmosphere (Tang et al., 2007). Most ceramic matrix composites, including ZrB 2 matrix ceramics, have inherently low fracture toughness and are thus susceptible to catastrophic fractures caused by thermal stresses under the above-mentioned conditions. Consequently, the thermal shock resistance of the ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic must be estimated.
In  the  present  work,  the  thermal  shock  resistance  of  the  ZrB 2 -SiC-ZrC ceramic was investigated by the water quenching method, and the microstructure of the specimen before and after water quenching was observed. Furthermore, the temperature and thermal stress distributions in the specimen during instantaneous water quenching were simulated and analyzed in detail by finite element analysis.

1. Introduction
With the increased severity of hypersonic re-entry vehicles ' operating  conditions,  the  propulsion  system  requires  materials  that  can withstand  temperatures > 2000 ◦ C  [1].  Ceramic  matrix  composites (CMCs) such as C/C and C/SiC fibers are the existing materials of choice due  to  their  light-weight,  excellent  mechanical,  and  thermal  shock resistance [2]. However, carbon is susceptible to oxidation above 400 ◦ C and has poor stability in an erosive environment [3,4]. The addition of SiC only improves its stability until 1100 ◦ C due to the volatile nature of SiO2  at higher temperatures [5]. Due to the thermal limitations of C/C and  C/SiC,  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  considered good additives for conventional C/C composites. UHTCs are carbides, nitrides, and borides of early transition metal elements (group IV and V) with  a  combination  of  metal  and  ceramic-like  properties  that  offers excellent  stability  at  temperatures > 2000 ◦ C.  UHTC  is  a  potential candidate for a thermal protection system (TPS) for reusable re-entry vehicles. TPS  is necessary for spacecraft that enter the earth ' s

5. Conclusions
ZrB2-SiC based ultra-high temperature ceramics with addition of ZrO2 were prepared by hot-pressing. By means of the water-quenching, thermal shock behavior of the ceramics was investigated with temperature difference increased from 200 to 800 ◦ C. Results of residual strength and critical temperature difference revealed that ZS10Z exhibited the optimal thermal shock resistance. Simulated analysis with FEM found that ZS10Z provided lowest thermal stress and stress intensity factors during thermal shock, which was in a good agreement with the experimental data. Improved toughness was beneficial for the thermal shock resistance. In addition, phase transformation from t-ZrO2 to m-ZrO2 during thermal shock led to the tougher surface, which was also favorable for the thermal shock resistance. With further addition of ZrO2 to ZrB2-SiC, phase transformation during thermal shock will crack the surface. In conclusion, ZrB2 -10 vol%SiC-10 vol%ZrO2 wasdiscovered to provide the best thermal shock resistance, which should be instructive for the design of ZrB2 -based ultra-high temperature ceramics.

4. Conclusions
In conclusion, ZrB2-20%SiC and ZrB2-20%SiC-10%graphite ultrahigh temperature ceramic were fabricated. A novel method was proposed to characterize thermal shock resistance for ZS. Under the experimental condition in this work, critical temperature at the center of specimen is w 2300 /C14 C for ZS and 2400 /C14 C for ZS10C. By several repeating thermal shock experiments, it implies that ZS10C provides a better repeating thermal shock resistance. It is calculated for thermal stress caused by temperature gradient and thermal expansion mismatch of phases, which is consistent with experiment for ZS.
The method proposed in this paper can be used to characterize UHTC failure with local place heated up quickly, and it may provide help for fabricating UHTC with higher application temperatures.

4. Conclusions
All the UHTCMCs successfully passed the base qualification and cyclic  exposure for three times, achieving temperatures in the order of 2000 -2500 K depending on the material and corresponding emissivity. Larger amounts of SiC were conducive in maintaining the Near InfraRed (NIR) spectral emissivity around 0.6 -0.8, owing to continuous recall of silica-glass to the surface, which all contributed not to exceed 2050 K during the qualification test. On the other hand, when only 10 vol% SiC was  present  in  the  matrix,  the  NIR  emissivity  waved  in  the  0.8 -0.4 range, owing to a prevalence of zirconium oxide on the surface. For these set of composites, regardless the presence of short or long fibre, the temperature  approached  2500 K.  The  reference  Cf/SiC  loaded  with 10 vol%  ZrB2 composite,  featured  by  mostly  silica,  had  a  spectral emissivity as high as 0.8 -0.9 with stable peak temperature of 2100 K. Although such discrepancies in the experienced thermal histories, the new  UHTCMCs  outperformed  the  reference  composite  by  enhanced ablation resistance.
Reusability tests, repeated three times, were first very reproducible assessing the reliability of the method, and then confirmed the trend of the baseline qualification outcomes, verifying the capability of the novel UHTCMCs to maintain their functionality and structural integrity after repeated exposure, making them appealing for future reusable TPS.
Finally, an extremely demanding test condition was selected in order to assess the materials performance under the peak pressure conditions of  an  abort  re-entry  scenario.  This  was  achieved  by  substantially increasing  the  heat  flux  delivered  to  the  surface,  up  to  8.5 MW/m 2 . Despite the considerable erosion and the very high temperature reached (over 3000 K for all UHTCMC compositions), which even led to material melting on the exposed surface, all the UHTCMCs were able to preserve a good  structural  integrity,  without  any  catastrophic  failures,  demonstrating their ability to properly protect the spacecraft even in the most challenging re-entry environment.

1. Introduction
The design of hypersonic vehicles needs leading edge components with sharp profile to improve flight performances like drag reduction, easier manoeuvrability, or larger cross-range options during atmospheric exit and re-entry [1]. At some stage of an hypersonic (re-entry) flight, the aforementioned sharp components are subject of very stressful heat fluxes in corrosive plasmas from atmosphere, and temperatures exceeding 2300 K for several minutes may become for these components a projected service condition [2]. Such extreme conditions limit the spectrum of possible candidates that nowadays are intensively searched within the class of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC). UHTC typically include non-oxides with melting/decomposition temperatures in excess of 3300 K. Diborides of the group IV transition metals like ZrB2 and HfB2 (3518 K and 3653 K of melting point, respectively) are currently the base systems taken in greater consideration to answer this technological challenge. Besides melting points greater than 3500 K [3], transition metal diborides have also thermal conductivity above 50 W/(m K) up to 1500 K [4], giving them a great potential advantage over more traditional materials to operate in extreme environments such as those experienced
⇑ Corresponding author. Tel.: +39 0546 699758; fax: +39 0546 46381. frederic.monteverde@istec.cnr.it (F. Monteverde).

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
Not only the mechanical properties and oxidation resistance, but responses of materials to the conditions of high enthalpy plasma flows that are typically encountered during atmospheric re-entry need to be evaluated. One of the possible ways to investigate such effects, in terms of materials' response to large heat fluxes as well as oxidation resistance under extreme/complex conditions, is Arc-jet testing. A schematic representation of a typical arc-jet testing set-up is presented in Figure 14a. In a recent work, Savino et al. (Savino, 2008) had performed arc-jet testing using a 80 kW plasma torch, operating under in inert gas conditions at mass flow rates up to 5 g/s, on hemispheric shaped HfB 2 - 5 vol.% MoSi 2 and HfC - 5 vol.% MoSi 2 specimens placed at a distance of 6 cm form the exit torch (see Figure 14bd).  The  temperatures of investigation were between 1950 o C and 2400 o C, which revealed that both the  materials  could  be  stable  up  to  temperatures  exceeding  2000 o C. Prior to this work, arc-jet testing were done only on ZrB 2 -  SiC and HfB 2 -  SiC composites (Gasch, 2004). Due to the issues associated with experimental determination of such high temperature properties, a lot of work has focused on theoretical simulation of the behavior via computational fluid dynamics (Borrelli, 2010), FEM based analysis (Borrelli, 2009; Stine, 1968) or various other computational tools. Such models need to simulate the demands for the application under consideration. However, despite the progress with experimental and computational facilities, still lacking  is  a  comprehensive  thermostructural  database  for  the  various  potential UHTCs.

Since  EBS  only  melts  a  single-phase component powder (ZrSi2), it can be assumed that the effect of phase transformations is negligible. 4. Conclusion
As current experiments /uniFB01 nd it di /uniFB03 cult to uncover the nucleation and propagation process of thermal shock cracks when the ceramic material is subjected to thermal shock. In this paper, a constitutive model of the microcracked UHTCs with the used of Huang's energy method has been deduced, and all the material parameters of the model are temperature dependent. The UHTCs, as a type of thermal protection material, work across a wide temperature range and are sensitive to temperature in terms of their thermophysical properties. Therefore, the e /uniFB00 ect of temperature on the crack propagation criterion must be considered. For this reason, this paper has promoted a temperature-dependent critical energy release rate to determine the damage evolution and crack propagation for UHTCs. With the use of the constitutive relation and temperature-dependent energy release rate mentioned above, the thermal shock behaviors of Al2O3 ceramic material have been simulated /uniFB01 rstly with the /uniFB01 nite element method. The results indicated that the crack patterns obtained from simulation are quite similar with the experiments. The nucleation and propagation of thermal shock cracks, multicrack propagation, and regularity of long and short cracks can well be embodied in simulation, further validating the accuracy of the model. Then, the ZrB2-based UHTC has been used as an example to study the e /uniFB00 ects of the temperature dependence of crack propagation criterion on simulation and evaluation of the TSR of UHTCs. Results indicated that the thermal shock crack patterns obtained from the simulated, which assumed that the crack propagation criterion is temperature dependent, are much closer to the actual results. In addition, the cracks caused by the thermal shock lead to discontinuity inside the materials and accordingly a /uniFB00 ect the internal thermal conduction and consequently the temperature /uniFB01 eld.

4. Discussion
The calculation assumes an instantaneous change in surface temperature (infinite heat transfer) and is valid when the core of the body does not heat or cool prior to the surface reaching the temperature of the surrounding environment. Neglecting in first approximation the anisotropy of this composite and using experimental values of E (124 GPa), α 1  (1.8 × 10 GLYPH<0> 6 ◦ C GLYPH<0> 1 ), tensile and bending strength as lower and upper bounds (310 -630 MPa) for σ ,  and 0.13 as Poisson ratio of the composite, the maximum change in temperature that the composite can withstand is in the range 1220 -2470 ◦ C. This huge range needs refinement but is anyway consistent with the experimental results that indicate a 84% of strength retention after the thermal drop of 1500 ◦ C. In the present work it was not possible to raise temperature drops over 1500 ◦ C because of experimental limitation in the maximum furnace temperature, however this rudimental calculation seems to suggest that these composites could withstand even greater thermal shocks, as required by the type of applications envisaged.

Thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-10vol%SiC ultra-high temperature ceramics with addition of ZrO2 was investigated by means of water-quenching as well as the finite element analysis. Optimal residual strength and critical temperature difference were obtained from ZrB2-10 vol%SiC ceramic added by 10vol%ZrO2. Simulated results revealed that ZrB2-10 vol%SiC-10 vol%ZrO2 provided the lowest thermal stress and stress intensity factor during thermal shock, which confirmed the experimental data. Such improvement in thermal shock resistance compared with ZrB2-10vol%SiC was primarily ascribed to the improved toughness by the addition of ZrO2. Besides, appropriate phase transformation of ZrO2 during quenching was further beneficial to resist the thermal shock.

The present paper focused on the thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-SiC based UHTCs with addition of zirconia. By means of traditional water-quenching as well as the finite element method (FEM), influence of ZrO2 on the thermal shock behavior of ZrB2-SiC ceramics was analyzed.

question: Comparing porous YB2C2 ceramics at about 50% porosity fabricated by Yang et al. 2025 with ZrB2-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites after 200 thermal shock cycles fabricated by Huang et al. 2016: which material system reports the higher room-temperature strength value?
answer:
The room-temperature strength value of porous YB2C2 ceramics at about 50% porosity fabricated by Yang et al. 2025 is reported as 76.7 ± 14.7 MPa [16]. However, the room-temperature strength value of ZrB2-SiC coated PIP-C/SiC composites after 200 thermal shock cycles fabricated by Huang et al. 2016 is not available in the provided sources. 

Therefore, the higher room-temperature strength value is reported for the porous YB2C2 ceramics fabricated by Yang et al. 2025.

1. Introduction
However,  excessive  sintering  additives  would  reduce  the  hightemperature properties of the as-sintered ZrB2-based UHTCs [26 -30]. Especially, ZrSi2 would severely reduce the high-temperature strength of ZrB2-based UHTCs, as the fact that the yield stress of ZrSi2 monotonously decreases to less than 50 MPa with the increasing temperature up to 1500 ◦ C [26]. Previous literature [31] reported that 7.4 vol% ZrSi2-assisted sintered ZrB2  ceramics exhibited a strength reduction from 400 MPa at room temperature (RT) to 220 MPa at 1400 ◦ C. Although few ultra-high-temperature strength ( > 1600 ◦ C) of ZrB2 -ZrSi2  ceramic was reported, it is foreseeable that the strength of ZrB2 -ZrSi2 ceramics would decrease  to  an  extremely  low  value  at  ultra-high-temperatures.  In contrast,  SiC  or  ZrC-assisted  sintered  ZrB2 ceramics  have  superior high-temperature mechanical properties. For example, the strength of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrC ceramic sintered at 1900 ◦ C was 348 MPa [15]. The 1800 ◦ C-flexural  strength  of  ZrB2-15  vol%  SiC  ceramics  sintered  at 2100 -2170 ◦ C  could  reach  474  MPa  [13].  Although  the  ZrB2-based UHTCs  without  ZrSi2 sintering  additive  (e.g.  ZrB2 -SiC,  ZrB2 -ZrC, ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC)  exhibit  excellent  high-temperature  strength,  these  ceramics  require  extremely  high  sintering  temperature  (mostly  higher than  1900 ◦ C)  to  prepare  highly  dense  ceramics.
