In recent years, slurry injection technology has demonstrated potential for shortening process cycles in SI by directly introducing high-solid-content ceramic slurries into fiber preforms. 15-18 According to the study by Liu et al., 19 the process was optimized through the injection of ZrB2 -phenolic resin slurry, achieving a density of 2.10 ± 0.05 g/cm 3 and porosity of approximately 40%. However, subsequent densification processes are required after slurry injection into preforms. Venkatachalam et al. 20 fabricated C f -ZrB 2 -Cm composites using slurry injection combined with radio-frequency-assisted CVI, achieving 90% relative density for C f -ZrB 2 within 24 h. Under oxyacetylene ablation, the composites exhibited mass ablation rate of 1.7 mg/s and linear ablation rate of 1.6 µ m/safter 60 s (peak temperature ∼ 2600 K). However, residual 10% porosity in the 2.5D carbon fiber preform, caused by complex pore structures and CVI process limitations, significantly reduced ablation performance. Yang et al. 21 obtained C/ZrB2-SiC with 86% relative density and 215 MPa flexural strength using 30 vol.% ZrB 2 slurry injection combined with 12 PIP cycles. Hu et al. 22 achieved a 286 MPa flexural strength for Cf /ZrC-SiC through eight PIP cycles. Although PIP enables gradient distribution control of matrix phases, its requirement for multiple cycles (typically > 10) extends fabrication time to hundreds of hours. 23 RMI rapidly produces high-density ceramic matrix composites through molten metal reactions with preforms. It is particularly suitable for complex-shaped components, having demonstrated efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and high application potential. 24,25

1. Introduction
ZrB2-based composites have an extremely high melting point (>3000 /C176 C), high thermal and electrical conductivities, low density, and excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [1-4]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramic composites have been considered as potential candidates for application in critical thermal protection systems and other components of future hypersonic aircraft or re-entry vehicles [1-7]. Silicon carbide (SiC) is commonly used as an additive to improve the oxidation resistance and mechanical properties of ZrB2. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC particulate composites (ZS) are regarded as the baseline material in this research. However, unsatisfactory thermal shock resistance remains the obstacle to the development of ZrB2-SiC ceramic on an industrial scale. To avoid or mitigate catastrophic failure of ZrB2SiC ceramics during thermal shock, most current researches focus on composition design to improve the performance by selecting different additives [8-12]. Carbon is also sometimes used as an additive to enhance thermal shock resistance [12-14]. Recently, Levine et al. [13] studied the mechanical properties of ZrB214 vol% SiC-30 vol% C. They concluded that the addition of carbon could reduce strength but increases fracture toughness. Hong et al. [14] fabricated ZrB2-SiC-graphite composite, which exhibits increased thermal shock resistance compared with monolithic ZrB2-SiC. The toughness and hence the reliability and thermal

1. Introduction
Porous ceramics with engineered anisotropy have attracted significant  attention  for  their  unique  combination  of  low  density,  tailored permeability, and direction-dependent mechanical and thermal properties, which are critical for advanced aerospace, energy, and environmental  applications  [1 -3].  Among  ultra-high-temperature  ceramics (UHTCs),  YB2C2  has  emerged  as  a  promising  candidate  owing  to  its exceptional thermal stability above 2500 ◦ C, excellent chemical inertness,  and  remarkable  resistance  to  thermal  shock  and  mechanical damage  [4].  For  instance,  YB2C2 retains  more  than  70 %  of  its room-temperature bending strength at 1300 ◦ C, and has demonstrated excellent chemical compatibility and enhanced mechanical performance

2.2. Characterization
Thermo  gravimetric  and  differential  scanning  calorimetry  experiments were carried out on a thermal analyzer (TG-DSC, STA 449F5, Netzsch, Germany) to determine the thermal debinding temperature of the polymer binders according to the weight loss. The solvent debinding green bodies approximately 2 mg were heated from room temperature to 800 ◦ C at a rate of 10 ◦ C/min under a flowing air atmosphere. The chemical and structural compositions of green bodies and brown bodies were determined using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker, Germany). The phase composition of porous YB2C2  ceramics was analyzed using an X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 advanced, Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) with Cu K α radiation. A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Merlin Compact, Zeiss, Germany) equipped with an energy dispersive  spectroscopy  (EDS)  detector  was  used  to  observe  the morphology of the samples. Compression tests  were performed  on  a universal  testing  machine  (UTM-50kN,  Zhejiang  Dana  Automation Technology Co. LTD., China). The average maximum load at failure of five  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  with  the  size  of  10 × 10 × 10 mm 3 was recorded as the compressive strength under a uniaxial compressive load at a crosshead speed of 0.5 mm/min. The grain structure and crystallographic  texture  orientation  maps  of  the  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  in different directions (the X-Y surface and the X-Z surface) were determined using Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD, Oxford Symmetry 22, Voltage 20 kV, diaphragm 120 μ m). Samples for EBSD observation were subjected to standard metallographic procedures including cutting,  grinding  and  polishing.  Laser  confocal  microscopy  (VK-1000, Japan) was used to test the roughness of the samples, and DIC was used to test changes in the mechanical behavior of the samples.

3.3. Cycle dependence of thermal shock resistance
Fig. 7 shows the /uniFB02 exural strength of the C/C-ZrC composites experiencing di /uniFB00 erent number of thermal shock cycles from 1700 °C to the room temperature. The /uniFB02 exural strength decreases gradually with the increasing number of thermal shock cycles. The residual strength of the composite thermally shocked for 20 cycles is about 130 MPa, 70.3% of the as-produced C/C-ZrC composite. Mass change of the C/C-ZrC composite with di /uniFB00 erent number of thermal shock cycles was also measured and listed in Fig. 7. The mass loss keeps constant without obvious di /uniFB00 erence, which is the same as the composite thermally shocked at di /uniFB00 erent temperatures (< 1900 °C).
Fig. 7. Flexural strength and mass loss of the C/C-ZrC composites subjected to di /uniFB00 erent thermal shock cycles.
Fig. 8 shows the cross section microstructure of the C/C-ZrC composite thermally shocked from 1700 °C to the room temperature for di /uniFB00 erent thermal cycles. The formation of matrix cracks and interfacial debonding can be clearly observed as that shown in Fig. 5. With increasing numbers of thermal cycles, the interfacial debonding becomes more severe. The cross section microstructure of the composite thermally shocked for di /uniFB00 erent numbers is dense without formation of pores, which is consistent with the mass constant listed in Fig. 7. Here, the shocked temperature is merely 1700 °C, which is not high enough to enable the evaporation of the Zr2Si phase. Thus, no obvious weight change was measured.

4. Conclusions
UHTCs based coatings (ZrB2 e SiC coating and ZrC e SiC coating) for PIP-C/SiC composites are deposited by slurry and CVD method, then the thermal shock behavior of the coated samples is studied, and the effect of thermal cycling on their /uniFB02 exural property and microstructures is discussed. The following conclusions have been reached:
- (1) ZrB2 e SiC (ZrC e SiC) coating is fabricated by slurry and CVD method with a thickness of 50 m m.
- (2) The coated samples showed the degradation of the /uniFB02 exural strength properties and no mass variation. Compared with the as-received C/SiC composites, the mode of the fracture behavior of coated C/SiC composites was changed, the samples exhibited an obvious brittle fracture behavior, and the coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.
- (3) ZrB2 e SiC coated C/SiC composites are no longer a singlephase of ZrB2, after thermal cycling tests, SiC and C are detected. The surface of ZrC e SiC coated C/SiC composites is no longer a single-phase of ZrC, SiC and C are also detected either.
- (4) The damage of the UHTCs based coatings caused by tensile stress could in /uniFB02 uence the mechanical properties of PIP-C/SiC composites under thermal cycling tests, and the /uniFB02 exural property of C/SiC composites is reduced.

3.2. Mechanical properties of the composites
Analogous behavior is also observed in the tensile strength of the C/ C-ZiC-ZrC-SiC  composites,  and  the  increase  is  more  obvious.  For example, starting with the C/C composites with the density of 1.00 g/ cm 3 (C/C-10),  after  4  and  7  PIP  cycles,  the  increase  of  the  tensile strength of the composites CZZS-1014 and CZZS-1016 after RMI process is  4.76%  and  9.38%,  respectively  (Table  1  and  Table  2),  which  is significantly higher than that of the composites CZS-10 (-13.64%) with 0 PIP cycles. For the composites with density of 1.20 g/cm 3 (C/C-12), after  2  and  9  PIP  cycles,  the  increase  of  the  tensile  strength  of  the composites CZZS-1214 and CZZS-1218 after RMI process is 20.83% and 56.76%, respectively (Table 1 and Table 2), which is also much higher than that of the composites CZS-12 (4.55%) with 0 PIP cycles. Therefore, the increase of both flexural and tensile strength of the composites after RMI can be strongly improved and optimized by tailoring the thickness of ZiC via PIP cycles and achieve 50.54% and 56.76% after 7 and 9 PIP cycles, respectively.
Due to the introduction of the ZiC, which can protect carbon fibers well  during RMI, thus the prepared C/C-ZiC-ZrC-SiC composites also shows improved mechanical properties compared to most of the composites prepared by similar processes (Table 3).

a b s t r a c t
UHTCs based coatings (ZrB2 e SiC coating and ZrC e SiC coating) are prepared on the surface of PIP-C/SiC composites by slurry and CVD method, and the thermal stability is one of the key issues for C/SiC composites. This study aims to study thermal shock behavior of the coated composites. The results show the degradation of /uniFB02 exural strength without obvious variation, and the fracture mode and pull-out mechanism are also changed. The composition of coatings is characterized by XRD, SEM and XPS. The decrease of /uniFB02 exural strength during the tests is due to the debonding of the coating-matrix interfaces. Compared to uncoated C/SiC composites, the UHTCs based coatings are able to improve the mechanical properties of the composites.

A B S T R A C T
Controlling crystallographic texture and pore architecture in porous ultra-high temperature ceramics is critical for tailoring their performance in extreme environments, yet achieving this dual control remains a formidable challenge.  Here,  we  demonstrate  a  novel  strategy  to  fabricate  architected  porous  YB2C2  ceramics  exhibiting strong crystallographic and mechanical property anisotropy by synergizing powder extrusion printing with in-situ reaction  synthesis.  The  core  of  our  approach  is  a  templated-growth  mechanism,  where  shear  forces  during extrusion  align  sacrificial  graphite  flakes  within  the  green  body.  These  aligned  flakes  subsequently  serve  as heterogeneous  nucleation  and  growth  sites  for  plate-like  YB2C2  grains  during  boro/carbothermal  reduction, effectively  translating  the  macro-scale  printed  path  into  a  micro-scale  crystallographic  texture.  This  process yields a remarkable hierarchical structure, featuring unidirectionally aligned pores and a strong [001] crystallographic texture parallel to the printing direction. The resulting ceramics exhibit a highly tunable porosity (50 -75 vol%) and exceptional compressive strength, reaching 76.7 ± 14.7 MPa at ~50 % porosity. Crucially, this microstructural control imparts a pronounced mechanical anisotropy, a key attribute for designing components with directionally dependent loads. This work establishes a new paradigm for manufacturing ultra-high temperature ceramics, enabling the concurrent design of geometry and crystallographic texture to achieve tailored thermomechanical properties.
when used as a reinforcement phase in Cu-based composites [5,6]. These properties position YB2C2 as an ideal material for structural components in harsh environments such as thermal protection systems, nuclear reactors, and hypersonic vehicle engines [7 -9]. However, realizing the full potential of porous YB2C2  ceramics hinges on the ability to precisely control  their  porosity,  crystallographic  texture,  and  macroscopic  geometry -a challenge that has yet to be fully addressed by conventional fabrication techniques.

3.4. Composite mechanical behaviour
The composite (Cf-YB2C2) was machined into bars and tested under bending to evaluate the strength at room temperature. Compared to a reference material based on a ZrB2/SiC matrix reinforced with continuous carbon fibres (Cf-ZrB2/SiC) with similar carbon fibre content and porosity, the ycBC-Cf composite yielded higher values of strength, which were only matched and surpassed in the case of the Y2O3 doped ZrB2/SiC material, where the YB2C2 is formed during sintering within the ZrB2/ SiC matrix. This highlights the promising mechanical properties of the YB2C2 phase combined with the fibre reinforcement, and confirms the synergistic effect in the ZrB2/SiC/Y2O3 matrix which leads to a decrease of porosity and an increase of the mechanical properties.
From  the  load/displacement  curves  of  the  Cf-YB2C2 composite (Fig.  11  a),  the  behaviour  observed  is  typical  of  other  UHTCMCs previously reported, mostly constituted by an elastic zone, followed by the  progressive  breaking  of  the  layers.  The  fracture  surface  appears rough and complex (Fig. 11 b), and looking at the details of the fractured matrix (Fig. 11 c), fibres appear well anchored to the ceramic matrix and the fibre pull-out is very limited, implying a strong fibre/matrix interface; moreover, the individual lamellae can be seen at different angles in spite of the partial anisotropy imparted by the applied pressure during sintering.  From  Fig.  11  d,  the  individual  YB2C2  lamellae  themselves showed some sliding over each other and some degree of lamellae pullout, which likely contributed to the high strength reported.

[100] fabricated ZrB2-LaB6-SiC coatings  on  graphite  substrates  via  spark  plasma  sintering  (SPS) method.  The  coating  containing  3 wt%  LaB6 demonstrated  optimal oxidation  resistance,  maintaining  structural  integrity  and  97.7%  oxidation protection efficiency through 20 thermal cycles.
Thermal shock and cyclic oxidation testing provide key evaluation criteria  for  the  synergistic  performance  of  coatings  against  thermal shock and oxidation. However, existing methods lack sufficient characterization of the temporal interaction and multi field coupling mechanism of thermal shock and oxidation corrosion, which restricts the life  prediction  and  optimization  design  of  coatings  under  extreme thermal-oxygen alternating conditions in space.

thermal stability, making it suitable for use at temperatures of 1600 ◦ C. 2-4 The ZrB2's impressive resistance to high-temperature structural degradation is attributed to the in situ formation of a thermally stable ZrO 2 -rich oxide scale, which provides additional thermal insulation and enhances its overall performance. 4-6 Incorporating silicon carbide (SiC) as reinforcement into the ZrB 2 matrix enhances its mechanical properties, oxidation, and ablation resistance. 6,7 Furthermore, the SiC reinforcement also inhibits ZrB2 grain growth during sintering, leading to enhanced densification. The composites with 20 vol% SiC reinforcement are found to offer the best combination of mechanical and thermophysical properties, along with high ablation resistance at elevated temperatures. 7-9

A B S T R A C T
The ZrB2 is widely recognized as an ultra-high-temperature ceramic (UHTC) due to its high melting point, high thermal conductivity, and superior  thermal  stability,  making  it  suitable  for  application  at  temperatures ≥ 1400 ◦ C [1 -3]. The ZrB2  exhibits a very remarkable resistance to high-temperature structural degradation, thanks to the in-situ formation of a thermally stable ZrO2-rich oxide scale, further enhancing its overall performance by providing thermal insulation [4 -8]. Furthermore, the addition of SiC as reinforcement in the ZrB2 matrix not only improves its strength and fracture toughness, but also its ability to withstand oxidation and ablation [5,7]. Another advantage of the SiC being present as reinforcement is its ability to impede the growth of ZrB2 grains during the sintering process, resulting in superior densification, leading  to  enhanced  mechanical  properties.  It  has  been  conclusively demonstrated  that  among  the  ZrB2-based  composites  with  different proportions of SiC, the one containing 20 vol % SiC as reinforcement exhibits the most favourable combination of mechanical and thermo-physical properties, along with high resistance to ablation at elevated  temperatures  [9,10].  However,  both  monolithic  ZrB2 and ZrB2 -SiC composites show the same type of brittle catastrophic failure Design  and  optimization  of  the  process  for  fabricating  ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) with continuous carbon fibre (Cf) as a reinforcement has recently garnered significant interest among the researchers globally [12]. The strong interest in this area of research stems from the remarkable improvement in fracture toughness and thermal shock resistance, along with the observation of damage tolerant behaviour in the continuous Cf  reinforced UHTCMCs. Due to the combination of continuous carbon fibre and UHTC matrix, these composites attain the thermal and mechanical properties desirable for  use  in  structural  components  of  hypersonic  vehicles,

1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.

Adding ZrC can be a good trick for compensating such a drop in hardness and flexural strength. Adding ZrC to ZrB2-SiC composites, individually, has also been investigated by several researchers [21-25]. Table 1 summarizes the average values of relative density and mechanical properties for some of ZrB2-based ceramics containing SiC, ZrC and/or Cf additives.

One-dimensional ZrB2 crystals with tube structure synthesized using a two-step heat treatment approach
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

Directionally Solidified Ceramic Eutectics for High-Temperature Applications
where K C stands  for  the  fracture  toughness  (Llorca  &  Orera,  2006).  Thus  the  improvements in strength of DSE ceramics are associated with an increase of the toughness or a reduction of the defect size, which is normally related to the characteristic dimension of the microstructure. At the same time the crack deflection is a well-known toughening mechanism in ceramic composites with columnar or lamellar structures. For instance, crack deflection in fiber-reinforced ceramics or laminated ceramics is achieved through weak fiber-matrix interfaces or by inserting layers with very low toughness, respectively (Pardo, Peña, Merino, Cases, Larrea, & Orera, 2002). In case of DSE ceramics, the fracture toughness of single phase ceramic layers is not so high and it allows to realise crack deflection toughening mechanism. On the other hand, the thermal residual stresses induced by a thermal expansion mismatch between the phases in DSE ceramics could be used to increase the toughness by enhancing the crack deflection (Llorca & Orera, 2006).
Reduction in the domain thickness can also be achieved by adding a third phase to form a ternary eutectic composite. For example, ternary eutectic Al 2 O3 -YAGYSZ showed higher room temperature strength than binary Al 2 O3 -YAG grown at the same rate (Lee, Yoshikawa, Fukuda, & Waku, 2001; Waku, Sakata, Mitani, Shimizu, & Hasebe, 2002). This indicates that the defect size for the Al 2 O3 -YAG binary eutectic was controlled by the lamella thickness. At present the maximum flexural strength (4.6 GPa) was achieved for DSE Al 2 O3 -YAG-YSZ (Oliete, Peña, Larrea, Orera, Llorca, Pastor, Martin, & Segurado, 2007). This is approximately four times higher than the best results measured in bulk non-oxide ceramics and twice

3.1. SiC whisker reinforcement
Another emerging trend regarding whisker reinforced UHTCMCs is laminated  architectures.  Specifically,  set  of  recent  studies  [119,120] have  investigated  the  effect  of  having  porous  UHTC -SiCw  interfaces between  monolithic  UHTC  layers.  Weak  interfaces  such  as  BN  and graphite  have  previously  been  applied  to  induce  crack  deflection  in these lamented structures [121,122]. However, both BN and graphite are prone to oxidation at lower than desirable temperatures ( ≤ 800 ◦ C). Alternatively, a dense SiC layer has the potential to manifest as an undesirably strong interface. A layer composed of a UHTC ceramic with a high  volume  fraction  of  whiskers  could  instead  produce  a  relatively porous  interface  that  can  concomitantly  resist  oxidation  and  induce toughening mechanisms. This was achieved using whiskers of about 18 μ m length and 1.5 μ m diameter. The fabricated architecture is shown in Fig. 5a, where crack deflection and branching are apparent at the SiCw-UHTC  interface.  Fig.  5b  and  c  further  illustrate  how  microscale whisker  pullout  and  bridging  can  enforce  the  macroscale  crack  deflections. The reported flexure strengths were similar to values reported M. Ammendola et al.
Fig. 5. Fracture morphologies of a laminated ZrB2 -SiC composites with SiCw -ZrB2 interfaces, showing (a) macroscale crack deflections, (b) whisker pullout, and (c) whisker bridging after 3-point flexure testing [119]. Reproduced with permission, © 2018 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
Fig. 6. Cross-section microstructure of the laminated ZrB2 -SiC -SiCw  composite after ablation: (a) cross section; (b) molten ZrO2  layer; (c) loose ZrO2  layer; (d) interface layer [120]. Reproduced with permission, © 2021 Elsevier Ltd.

3.2. SiC short fiber reinforcement
Other recent investigations have proposed functionally graded materials (FGM) for embedding SiCf  in UHTC matrices. Silvestroni et al. [147] synthesized ZrB2-based composites where the fiber volume fraction  increases  as  a  function  of  the  material  depth,  where  the  thin, outermost layer of the material contains the lowest fiber fraction. In principle, this outermost skin should possess a high resistance against oxidation and ablation, and it serves to protect the inner layers of the FGM  that  are  embedded  with  higher  volume  fractions  of  SiCf,  The described  architecture  merited  a  composite  with  reasonable  fracture toughness values (~6 MPa m ½ ) while maintaining low overall densities; this  suggested  a  potential  for  lightweight  component  design  using fiber-reinforced FGMs. Furthermore, load-displacements curves (Fig.  9b)  indicated  failure  behavior  that  was  not  completely  brittle, based  on  the  tailed  shape  at  higher  displacements.  The  subsequent investigation by Stricker et al. [148] further developed the ZrB2  FGM approach by refining the buffer layer architecture. In this design, the outermost  layer  of  the  composite  contained  a  MoSi2 additive  for enhanced oxidation protection, followed by a ' buffer layer ' that mixed ZrB2 with Si3N4, SiCN, or SiHfBCN. The buffer layer served to obstruct diffusion of Mo species from the outer skin to the inner composite, where it could detrimentally react with SiCf during sintering.
In terms of mechanical properties, SiCf-reinforced composites have reported  advantages  over  monolithic  ceramics,  specifically  in  higher fracture toughness values and retained flexural strength up to 1200 ◦ C. Nevertheless, reaction between the fibers and the matrix phase during sintering  remains  an  ongoing  issue,  which  severely  limits  effective toughening  by  fiber  pullout.  Next  generation  SiC  fibers  with  higher purities  have  the  potential  to  resist  matrix  interaction  during  high temperature pressing, but these materials have not yet been explored as reinforcements for UHTCMCs [149].

3.3. Correlating interfaces with the mechanical properties
In summary, a correlation of the microstructural features with enhancement in the mechanical properties is established for ZrB2-SiCCNT composites and schematically presented in Fig. 7. A comparison of the mechanical properties of ZrB2-SiC-CNT composites (see Fig. 7 a ), unequivocally, establishes the synergy of SiC and CNT reinforcement in enhancing the properties of ZrB2. The enhancement in the fracture strength, fracture toughness, elastic modulus and reinforcement factor was found to synergistically increase with both SiC and CNT addition (in Z20S10C when compared with monolithic ZrB2). Further, based on the microstructural evolution and enhancement in the fracture toughness, the toughening mechanism with SiC and CNT reinforcement has been delineated, schematically shown in Fig. 7 b . It was found that the strong interfacing between the reinforcement and the matrix, and phenomenon like crack de /uniFB02 ection, branching and bridging (shown in Figs. 3, 5 and 6) attributes in enhancing the strength and toughness of ZrB2-SiC-CNT composites. The result revealed that the enhanced mechanical performance of Z20S10C get an edge over other ZrB2based composites making it a suitable candidate structural material for high damage tolerance.

3.3. Mechanical properties
Fig. 8 shows the variation of hardness of ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites with the reinforcement content. The average hardness of SPS sintered, with relative density of ∼ 85%, was found to be 16.64 GPa in this investigation. The average hardness of the ZrB2-SiC composites increased with increasing SiC reinforcement content, reaching ∼ 22.71 GPa for composites with 40% SiC. The higher hardness for the ZrB2-SiC composites compared to ZrB2 seems to be due to combination of effects such as higher relative density and presence of high volume fraction of harder reinforcement (SiC) particles. As all the ZrB2-SiC composites (10-40%
Fig. 9. Flexural strength of (a) ZrB2-SiC, and (b) ZrB2-CNT composites as a function of reinforcement content.

3.3. Mechanical properties
sintered in this investigation seems primarily due to lower relative density ( ∼ 85%). While the SiC reinforcement resulted in the improvement in fracture toughness of ZrB2, the average fracture toughness values of all the ZrB2-SiC composites remained in the range of 2-2.7 MPa m 1/2 . Among ZrB2-SiC composites, the composites reinforced with 20% SiC exhibited highest fracture of 2.66 ± 0.34 MPa m 1/2 . For ZrB2-CNT composites, the fracture toughness also increased with increasing CNT reinforcement content and was in the range of 1.5-3.5 MPa m 1/2 . The ZrB2-CNT composites reinforced with 6% CNTs exhibited highest fracture toughness of 3.53 ± 0.47 MPa m 1/2 . Fig. 11 shows the morphologies of indentation cracks propagated through the SPS sintered ZrB2, ZrB2-SiC, and ZrB2-CNT composites. In ZrB2 samples, the crack front was relatively straight without significant bending or deflection (Fig. 11(a)). However, the crack seems to propagate along boundaries of smaller grains (1-2 𝛍 m). The fracture surface of ZrB2 samples indicated predominantly intergranular fracture features with distributed porosity (Fig. 3(a)). For ZrB2-SiC composites, the indentation crack interacts with the reinforced SiC particles causing shear of the finer particles and crack getting deflected at the coarser particles. The fracture surface of the ZrB2-SiC composites also shows predominantly intergranular fracture features with distributed depressions due to grain pull-outs (Fig. 3(b-c)). The relatively higher fracture toughness of these ZrB2-SiC composites compared to ZrB2 samples seems to be due to better densification and additional toughening mechanisms (SiC grain pull-outs and crack deflection).

3. Results and discussion
The bulk density of the ZrB2-20vol.% SiCW composites hotpressed at 2000 ◦ C was 5.44g/cm 3 , which corresponds to 98.6% of the theoretical density estimated with the rule of mixture. The flexural strength and fracture toughness of ZrB2-SiCW composites at room temperature are 510 ± 25MPa and 4.05 ± 0.20MPam 1/2 , respectively. However, the flexural strength and the fracture toughnessofSiCwhisker-reinforcedZrB2 werenotsignificantlyincreased compared with those without reinforcements. The values of flexural strength and the fracture toughness are similar to those of SiC particle reinforced ZrB2 composites. Amazingly, SiC whiskers have been degenerated into particles (Fig. 2). Furthermore, the microstructure of the SiC whisker reinforced ZrB2 composite is very similar to that of the SiC particle reinforced (not shown).

Fabrication, Microstructure, and Properties of Zirconium Diboride Matrix = Ceramic.............................................................................................................. 354. Fabrication, Microstructure, and Properties of Zirconium Diboride Matrix = Zhi Wang, Dalian University of Technology, China. Fabrication, Microstructure, and Properties of Zirconium Diboride Matrix = Zhanjun Wu, Dalian University of Technology, China
The crystal structure, synthesis, and densification of zirconium diboride (ZrB2) are summarized in detail. In this chapter, ZrB2-ZrC-SiC ceramic was synthesized by reactive hot pressing a mixture of Zr, B4C, and Si powders. The thermal shock resistance of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic was estimated by the water-quenching method and was significantly greater than that of a ZrB2-15vol.% SiC ceramic. The isothermal oxidation of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic was carried out in static air at constant temperatures of 1000±15, 1200±15, and 1400±15 ºC for different amounts of time at each temperature. The mechanism of strength increase for the oxidized specimen indicated that the strength increased with the reaction rate, which was related to the rate of change in volume induced by reaction, initial crack geometry, elastic modulus, and surface free energy. The formation of oxide layers resulted in (I) repair of surface flaws, (II) increase in flexural strength, (III) appearance of a compressive stress zone beneath the surface oxide layers, (IV) decrease in thermal stress, and (V) consumption of thermal stress. These five aspects were favorable to the improvement of the thermal shock resistance of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic. The isothermal oxidation of the ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ceramic was carried out in static air at 1600±15 ºC. In the different oxidation stages, quantitative models were proposed for predicting oxidation kinetics.

3.2. Microstructure of the as-sintered material
As example, the typical microstructure (cross-section) of as-prepared C/ZrB2-SiC composites (BAR and UBR samples) is shown in Fig. 2.
The low magnification micrographs (Fig. 2a,b) evidence the layered structure  in  a  0/90 ◦ and  0/0 ◦ configurations  with  homogeneously distributed carbon fibers in a nearly fully dense ZrB2/SiC matrix with no delamination. The volumetric fiber content amounted to about 50 vol% as ascertained by image analysis, while the matrix consisted of about 45 vol% ZrB2  and 5 vol% SiC (Fig. 2c). Interlocking among fibers, SiC and ZrB2 particles is well highlighted in Fig. 2d,e. During the sintering treatment, the high chemical compatibility of ZrB2  with carbon led to enhanced reactivity between fiber and matrix, resulting in strong interfaces.  The  proposed  thermal  treatment  at  temperature  close  to 2000 ◦ C and pressure of tens of MPa for few minutes time followed by a rapid  cooling  guaranteed  a  strong  fiber/matrix  interface  without causing critical fiber damage.
As  can  be  seen  in  high  magnification  micrograph  in  Fig.  2f,  the presence  of  SiC  particles  (dark  grey  regions)  at  grain  boundaries inhibited  the  excessive  grain  growth  of  bright  contrast  ZrB2 (small variations in color are due to the different grain orientations) [18,19]. As a result, the microstructure was very fine-grained, with a mean ZrB2 grain size of 2.2 µ m. Similar microstructure was found for UD sample.

Zhang et al. [24] selected pressureless sintering process for producing ZrB2 -SiC composites, which for them nominated SiC volume percentage included 10, 20 and 30%. Sintering occurred at 2000 °C for 3 h. This research revealed that SiC content prevents grain growth, while changing such mechanical properties as /uniFB02 exural strength and toughness to 492 MPa from 404 and to 3.5 MPa m 1/2 from 3, respectively.
Emami et al. [28] produced ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC composites through mechanical activation spark plasma synthesis (MASPSyn) process. For this purpose, selected SiC content was about 31.6 vol% and ZrC content varied from 7.5 to 19.5 vol% and what remained was balanced by ZrB2 as the matrix. The best results for /uniFB02 exural strength and toughness were obtained for 15 vol% of ZrC content by measuring 620 MPa and 5.7 MPa m 1/2 , respectively.
Tsuchida et al. [58] used MA-SHS process to obtain ZrB2 and ZrC from elemental raw materials to produce ZrB2 -ZrC composites through the SPS process. The mechanical characteristics of the composites, estimated by Vickers indentation technique, showed the fracture toughness of 2.9 -5.1 MPa m 1/2 and Vickers hardness of 13.6 -17.8 GPa, depending on the molar ratio of ZrB2/ZrC.

question: What does the negative Seebeck coefficient observed in Dubey 2022 for HfB2-ZrB2 composites indicate about the type of mobile charge carriers responsible for electrical transport?
answer: The negative Seebeck coefficient observed in Dubey 2022 for HfB2-ZrB2 composites indicates that electrons are the dominant charge carriers responsible for electrical transport [1].

A B S T R A C T
As the total thermal conduction in diborides comprises both electronic and phononic component,   electrical conductivity ( σ e) at room temperature  has  also  been  assessed.  The  electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck coefficient (S) of the HfB2-ZrB2-based composites was measured in the He atmosphere at room temperature using ZEM-3M10 apparatus (ULVAC-RICO Inc.). The figure of  merit  ( ZT = S 2 σ eT κ )  was  calculated, Table  2,  from  thermoelectric  parameters  (electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck  coefficient).  Seebeck  coefficient  is  observed  to  be  negative, suggesting n-type semiconductor behavior, with electrons to be dominant charge carriers in all the composites, and following similar trend of electrical conductivity at room temperature with maximum of -3.57 V/K observed for HZ20S6C. The power factor is ~6 × 10 -5 Wm -1 K 2 at room temperature for Z20S, HZ20S and HZ20S6C composites (Table 2) except for H20S (where the presence of HfB2  phase dominates). On the other hand, ZT value for Z20S is ~43 times to that of H20S. After incorporation of CNT, all thermoelectric parameters in HZ20S6C are observed to increase ~4 -6% as compared to that of HZ20S. Low ZT value in ~1 × 10 -4 Wm -1 K -1 range (due to high κ ) for these composites is not sufficient for thermoelectric applications at room temperature.

A B S T R A C T
of phonon transport which reduces drastically in presence of impurities such  as  WC  introduced  during  milling,  thus,  lowering  the κ of  the diboride based ceramic [11]. The room temperature κ of ZrB2  also decreases by ~40% on introduction of HfB2  in the matrix without much difference in charge carrier density (similar electronic structure, AlB2) but rather, due to difference in phonon scattering and formation of solid solution of (Hf-Zr)B2  [12]. CNT, on the other hand, not only improves densification  but  works  synergistically  to  enhance  the  thermal  and electrical transport in diboride based composites [10,13,14]. The addition of 10 vol% CNT to ZrB2 + 20 vol% SiC has elicited marginal increase in high temperature (1200 ◦ C) thermal conductivity to ~52.3 Wm -1 K -1 [10].
The spark plasma sintering (SPS) of (Hf,Zr)B2-SiC-CNT system (at 1850 ◦ C,  30  MPa for  10 min hold time, vacuum ~6 Pa) is reported earlier highlighting the solid solution, and eliciting enhanced mechanical  (H~28.1 GPa, KIC~10.2 MPa.m 1/2 )  [15]  and tribological performance  [16]  with  synergistic  superior  mechanical  properties in HZ20S6C (37 vol.% HfB2, 37 vol.% ZrB2, 20 vol.% SiC and 6 vol.% CNT) compared to that of HZ20S.
Fig. 1i(a-i) [15] present the microstructural morphology of ZrB2-HfB2-based composites, and elemental spectrum of starting phase pure SiC, HfB2 and ZrB2 powders. Further, marginal increase in oxidation resistance (~6%)   is depicted by HZ20S6C due to the synergistic solid solution, presence of CNT and SiO2 formation on oxidation of SiC phase [17,18].

10. Challenges, emerging trends, and conclusions
UHTCs  including  ZrB2,  HfB2,  TiB2,  TaC,  HfC,  TiC,  etc.  possess admirable  properties;  however,  ZrB2  can  be  considered  as  the  most suitable UHTC material in its monolithic form due to its lower density (6.10  gcm GLYPH<0> 3 ),  chemical  inertness,  high  thermal  conductivity  (56 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 ), high melting temperature and thermal conductivity of its oxide  (~2660 ◦ C  and  ~2  Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 respectively)  proving  strong candidature of ZrB2 for TPS of hypersonic vehicles. It may be highlighted that the starting powder purity and sintered densification play a very significant role in dictating the structural and high temperature oxidation properties, and merely extrapolated prediction of porous composites may not do justice to with that of phase-pure and dense ZrB2-HfB2SiC based composites. Pressure-assisted sintering techniques such as SPS and HP can provide remarkable properties. Among the second phase sintering additives, SiC results in enhancing the densification, mechanical and oxidative performance of ZrB2. On one hand where > 20% SiC content provides an enhanced fracture strength and low temperature oxidation resistance, whereas on the other hand < 20% SiC content assists high temperature performance of ZrB2-based UHTCs. Further, the finer size of SiC ( < 5 μ m) with optimum content (20 vol %) may impart superior combination of thermo-mechanical properties in ZrB2. However,  incorporation  of  HfB2 may  further  limit  the  oxygen  diffusion through the formation of HfO2 and enhance the oxidation resistance of ZrB2 -SiC-based UHTCs. Further, with higher thermal conductivity of HfB2 (~105 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 ), its incorporation has provided ~30% enhanced oxidation  resistance  in

3.1.1. Theoretical calculation of diffusion coefficient
The average diffusion coefficient for Hf in ZrB2  was observed to be higher  (i.e.,  ~10.2 × 10 GLYPH<0> 13 m 2 /s)  than  that  of  Zr  into  HfB2 (i.e., ~2.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 13 m 2 /s) which is also supported by higher diffusion depth of Hf into ZrB2. Diffusion coefficient calculated in the present work is in accordance with the lattice diffusion of hot pressed ZrB2 at 2100 ◦ C [44], but diffusion coefficient alone cannot justify the mechanism. Hassan et. al. [35] reported the grain boundary diffusion to be dominating mechanism at temperatures < 1950 ◦ C for pure ZrB2  phase, however, with 20 vol%  SiC  addition,  lattice  diffusion  dominates  at  temperatures > 1850 ◦ C, which is very close to the joining temperature used in the present work. Furthermore, recent study by Dubey et. al. [45] also reported the transition from grain boundary to lattice diffusion at 1850 ◦ C for pure HfB2, which might shift to lower values with SiC reinforcement. Therefore,  during  joining  of  ZS-HS  composite  via  SPS  at  1800 ◦ C, 40 MPa pressure, the diffusion mechanism is dominated by the grain boundary diffusion along with some transition to the lattice diffusion as suggested by the calculated diffusion coefficient value (i.e., 2.7 × 10 GLYPH<0> 9 m 2 /s).

SEM micrographs of the fractured surface after 4-point bending test of (e) ZS-ZS and (f) ZS-HS joints revealing intergranular and trans granular fracture along with the presence of step like cleavage planes.

4. Discussion
Thermal transport in solid materials is a consequence of electron and phonon conduction. Therefore, thermal conductivity is explained in terms of an electronic contribution and a phonon contribution. The phonon contribution depends on number of carriers, phonon velocity and phonon mean free path, which are all constant at temperatures above the Debye temperature. Debye temperatures for ZrB2, HfB2, SiC and La2O3 are 750 K, 30 565 K, 2 1080 K 31 and 160 K, 28 respectively. Therefore, the phonon contribution to conductivity should be constant at temperatures well above the Debye ( ∼ 1000 ◦ C) temperature as has been highlighted for pure ZrB2 and HfB2. 27 Grain boundaries are resistive elements for thermal transport leading to so-called Kapitza resistance. Therefore, thermal conductivity decreases with reduced grain size. 32 The intrinsic Kapitza resistance should be relatively temperature insensitive above the Debye temperature unless physical changes take place with increasing temperature. 18 The dominant part of thermal conductivity of monolithic ZrB2 and HfB2 is the electron contribution which could represent up to 90% of the thermal conductivity in ZrB2 and HfB2 ceramics. 17 SiC addition is known to increase the phonon contribution to thermal conductivity. 17 However, data on the effect of SiC on UHTC thermal conductivity are contradictory. Due to its high thermal conductivity 33 compared with ZrB2 and HfB2, SiC should increase thermal conductivity and the greater the amount of SiC added, the higher the thermal conductivity as a better connected internal network is formed. 13,14,18,34 However, the presence of SiC-ZrB2 or SiC-HfB2 in addition to ZrB2-ZrB2 or HfB2-HfB2 interfaces can lower thermal conductivity below monolithic thermal conductivities values 15,27 as a consequence of Kapitza resistance.

question: What is the chamber pressure in millibar for Case D in Table 1 of the Kim et al. 2024 vacuum plasma spray study on ZrC powder coatings?

However, it is noted that the chamber pressure was increased to 150 mbar in conditions E, F, and G [6]. The specific pressure for Case D is not available in the corpus.

3. Results and discussion
Fig. 4 shows the cross-sectional images of FE-SEM and EDS analysis corresponding each process condition from Cases A to G. Cases A and B show the specimens coated with ZrC-49 powder, which presents the severely damaged surfaces due to the impact of high velocity un-melted large particles during the coating process. In Case A, as seen in Fig. 4(a), the underlying C/C composite was exposed and damaged. The reduced damages in Case B [Fig. 4(b)] can be attributed to the increased power and addition of He gas, resulting in decrease of un-melted powder in the plasma flame. However, since the plasma condition might not be sufficient to melt the whole injected particles, the surface damages were still observed  and  the  thin  ZrC  layer  was  partly  coated.  To  mitigate  the damages and to increase coating thickness, a relatively small powder (ZrC-42) was introduced with different current and pressure conditions as described as Cases C, D and E in Table 1. In Case C, it was confirmed that  the  SiC  layer  was  partially  damaged,  while  the  underlying  C/C composite was found to be relatively intact. However, as seen in Fig. 4 (c),  it  was  hard  to  increase  the  ZrC  layer  coating  thickness  only  by reducing the powder size under the same processing conditions. To increase the contact time of particles with the plasma, the gas flow rate was reduced but the chamber pressure was increased for Cases D and E. Relatively thick ZrC layers of 12.5 μ m (Case D) and 25 μ m (Case E) were obtained, which indicated that not only the smaller size of powder but also their sufficient residence time in the plasma were crucial to ensure complete  melting  and  to  form  a  thick  coating  layer.  Therefore,  the coating processes under high power (54 kW) and high pressure (150 mbar) conditions was performed with smaller-sized powders, ZrC-33 (Case  F)  and  ZrC-10  (Case  G). In this work, therefore, we propose a multi-step ZrC coating process on C/C composites to form a homogeneous ZrC multi-layer protecting the  base  material  under  high-temperature  oxidation  conditions.  The structural properties of the ZrC layers formed through multi-step coatings  were  investigated  using  field  emission  scanning  electron  microscopy (FE-SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), a Vickers hardness tester, and a  surface  roughness tester.  The  performance  of  the  ZrC  layers  under high-temperature  oxidation  conditions  was  evaluated  using  a  highvelocity  oxygen  fuel  (HVOF)  facility.  The  results  were  compared  to those of layers obtained by a conventional single-step coating process.

Notably,  the  Case  F exhibited the highest thickness (50 μ m) and very smooth roughness (16 μ m) of ZrC coating layer, which was attributed to the well formation of splats on the surface by increased droplets of melted powders due to the use of smaller powder size (D50 = 33.85 μ m), higher pressure and higher power conditions. In Case G, the coated surface was extremely flat (Sa = 3 μ m) because the smallest particles were completely melted and coated as  the  form  of  splats  even  though  the  thickness  of  ZrC  layer  was decreased due to lower feeding rate and reduction of some powders by vaporization in the plasma.
From the result, it was found that the formation of powder droplets via  complete  melting  was  crucial  for  a  smooth  and  thick  ZrC  layer coating on the rough surface of C/C composite [14,15]. The complete melting of powders could be achieved with the use of smaller powder and with the sufficient contact time of particles in the plasma, which was related to the velocity and residence time of the particles in the plasma. Fig. 7(a) presents a particle image captured for a duration of 5 μ s just before the collision of ZrC powder with the substrate during the VPS process. Given the large number of images obtained using a high-speed camera,  image  processing  was  conducted  using  MATLAB  [16]  to differentiate  particles  within  the  image,  as  depicted  in  Fig.  7(b). Following the completion of image processing, the velocity of ZrC particles was calculated using the acquired data.
Fig. 7(c) displays the measured particle velocity and calculated gas residence time in chamber [17]. Gas residence time means the time the process gas stays in the chamber considering filling the entire chamber and is calculated using the chamber pressure, volume, and total input gas flow. The particles velocity tends to decrease as the gas residence time  increases,  which  means  that  although  the  particle  flight  time ( = spray distance/calculated particle speed) is very short, less than 1 ms, H.S. 2.2. Vacuum plasma spray coating process
The VPS system mainly consists of a plasma spray gun (F4VB, Metco) mounted on a 6-axis robot (IRB1600, ABB) in the cylindrical vacuum chamber (diameter = 1.8 m, length = 2.35 m) as illustrated in Fig. 3(a). The  plasma  with  a  DC  power  (Max = 55  kW)  is  produced  within  a pressure  range  of  30  mbar -900 mbar by introducing argon gas. The plasma gas flows between the electrode and nozzle inside the spray gun, and raw powders are fed into the plasma with the argon carrier gas from a powder feeder. Fig. 3(b) shows the path of the robot during the coating process,  and  the  coating  process  is  carried  out  along  this  path  in  a H.S. Kim et al.
Fig. 3. (a) Schematic illustration of vacuum plasma spray coating system and (b) a path of one cycle to coat whole substrate surface.
reciprocating manner. During the coating process, the spray distance between the spray gun to the substrate is about 300 mm. Table 1 presents the detailed plasma operating conditions such as current, power, injecting  gases  and  chamber  pressure  for  different  cases  with  four different sized powders.

1. Introduction
Recently, carbon-carbon (C/C) composites have been widely employed in various application fields due to their exceptional properties, but their oxidation at relatively low temperature around 500 ◦ C poses a challenge to be used in high-temperature conditions. To address the drawback, the coating of ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) layer on the C/C composites has been suggested as one of promising solutions. However, the quality of UHTC coating layers obtained through vacuum plasma spraying (VPS) is heavily influenced by the size of the introducing raw powders to coat the layers. Therefore, we investigated the size effects of ZrC powders in the range of (10 -50) μ m, one of UHTCs, to optimize the coating quality. By modulating process conditions, the coating quality with thick ZrC layer having lower porosity was obtained when using the powder around 34 μ m. From the results, we found the gas residence time related to the particle velocity in the plasma flame is one of key parameters to be modulated by VPS operational parameters, which could improve the quality of the coating layer even for large powder cases.

the time the particles stay in the plasma flame also increases. Since the severe substrate damage occurred in Case A, we increased plasma power and He gas flow ate in Cases B and C to mitigate the damage. In Cases B and C, although substrate damage was reduced, partial damage was still observed. To address the issue of substrate damage, the amount of argon gas was reduced and the chamber pressure was increased. These adjustments effectively prevented  substrate damage,  resulting in a decrease in particle velocity by approximately 45 m/s. Furthermore, it was confirmed that an increase in chamber pressure and a decrease in the total input gas flow resulted in approximately a twofold increase in gas residence time. To facilitate the formation of the ZrC coating layer, the chamber pressure was increased to 150 mbar in conditions E, F, and G. Consequently, the particle velocity was determined to be 325 m/s, representing a reduction of approximately 40 m/s. Additionally, it was confirmed that the gas residence time increased by about 200 s.

4. Summary
The size of raw powders introduced for vacuum plasma spray coating is one of important parameters for the quality of coated layers. In order to study the powder size effects, ZrC layers with various powder sizes were coated and compared. When a large-sized powder (D50 = 49 μ m) was employed, it was found the substrate damages due to un-melted powders. The large-sized powders were well melted to form droplets in the plasma by increasing power and helium gas addition. On the other hand, it was also shown that the smaller powders (D50 = 33.85 μ m) was beneficial  to  coat  thick  ZrC  layers.  On  the  other  hand,  the  coating thickness rather decreased by use of smallest powder (D50 = 10.45 μ m)
H.S. Kim et al.
Fig. 6. Surface roughness (Sa), and thickness of ZrC coating layers for different coating conditions.

3. Results and discussion
Fig. 3(a) shows the SEM image of the uncoated SiC -C/C composite substrate ' s  cross-section,  while  Fig.  3(b) -(f)  include  the  ZrC  layers coated under different VPS coating cycles and steps as described in Fig. 2 (c). The ZrC layer was coated on SiC-covered C/C composite at the same coating parameters. It is widely acknowledged that interlayer with an intermediate thermal expansion coefficient of UHTC and the C/C composite is required for better adhesion of UHTC [18]. As expected, for the samples coated in single-step processes, coating thickness increased as the number of cycles increased. Increasing the cycle numbers from 4 [Fig. 3(b), Case Z1] to 20 [Fig. 3(d), Case Z3], increased the ZrC layer ' s thickness from 32.3 μ m to 116.2 μ m. This implied that the coating efficiency  per  cycle  was  not  constant  but  linearly  decreased  from  8.1 μ m/cycle to 5.8 μ m/cycle as the number of coating cycles increased. On the other hand, the layer thicknesses for the samples coated in multi-step coating processes [Fig. 3(e) and (f)] were close to the sum of the coating thickness obtained during the corresponding cycles of each single-step coating. For example, Case Z4 (which underwent double-step coating of 10 cycles) was 136.3 μ m thick, almost twice the thickness of 70.6 μ m obtained from 10-cycle single-step coating. Case Z5 (which underwent the quintuple-step coating of 4 cycles) was 145.5 μ m thick, close to five times the thickness of 32.3 μ m obtained at 4 cycles of single-step coating. Although the total coating cycles in all three cases [Fig. 3(d) -(f)] were the same at 20, the thicker layer was coated in the case of more steps with fewer cycles per step.

4. Conclusions

sample, forming roughly equiaxial 2 μ m inclusions.
The  hardness  of  the  composite  with  carbon  black  monotonically degraded from 22.5 GPa for the pure TiB2-SiC matrix to 16 GPa for the sample with 7.4 wt% of the carbon black. Carbon black addition did not visibly affect fracture toughness and thermal conductivity within the margin  of  error,  which  remained  at  the  TiB2-SiC  ceramic  level  of 6.1 MPa · m 1/2  and 45 W/m · K, respectively.
The rGO addition significantly improved the mechanical and thermal properties. The improvement was non-monotonical, with a pronounced maximum from 2 to 3.8 wt% of rGO. The composite hardness enhanced by 16% to HV = 26.1 GPa, toughness by 20% to K1 C = 7.4 MPa · m 1/2 , and thermal conductivity by 53% to k = 69 W/m · K.  The  properties enhancement corresponded to the structure when graphene was well distributed in the matrix as separate sheets. High rGO concentration failed  to  improve  composite  properties  due  to  the  abovementioned agglomeration.

3.3. Characterization of sintered materials
There  are  a  number  of  papers  presenting  research  on  graphene ' s influence on TiB2-SiC [31,33,38,39]. Koval ˇ cíkov ´ a et al. [33] presented the mechanical properties of TiB2-SiC-based materials containing 1, 2, 5, and 10 wt% of graphene. TiB2-SiC matrix in [33] had slightly higher hardness and lower toughness. The toughness dependence on graphene content dependence presented in Fig. 10 for comparison is similar to that for rGO in the current work. In contradiction, the material hardness in [33] showed a monotonical decrease resembling carbon black-containing composites.
Fig. 7. SEM micrographs of the fracture surfaces of TiB2-SiC-based composites: a) reference (TS00); b,d) 3.8 wt% rGO (TSG03); c,e) 3.8 wt% carbon black (TSC03). The marked red areas in b) and c) are shown in d) and e), respectively. Carbon sheets are indicated with red arrows.
Fig. 8. SEM micrograph of the fracture surface of the 7.4 wt% rGO composite (TSG04), showing rGO agglomerates.
The carbon black presence seems to have minimal impact on the thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC matrix (see Fig. 11). The rGO additive, however, leads up to a 50% thermal conductivity coefficient rise at  3.8 wt%  as  a  peak  value.  At  higher  rGO  concentrations,  thermal conductivity slumps. The latter, again, could be attributed to the rGO sheet agglomeration at higher carbon contents.

A B S T R A C T
The most crucial goal is the improvement of Ultra High Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) fracture toughness without negatively impacting the ceramic high-temperature properties. Carbon nanostructures in UHTCs appear to be one of the most promising ways forward. The influence of reduced graphene oxide vs. carbon black on microstructure and properties of TiB2-SiC ceramics is studied. The composite ceramic samples with additive contents up to 7.4 wt% were manufactured via the reactive hot pressing of B4C-TiC-Si-reduced graphene oxide/carbon black precursors at 1850 ◦ С and 30 MPa for 4 min. The sintered materials composition and structure were analysed  with  Raman  spectroscopy,  XRD,  SEM,  and  TEM.  Reduced  graphene  oxide  (rGO)  survives  the  hotpressing procedure and, at lower concentrations, distributes inside the TiB2-SiC matrix in the form of separate sheets. However, at 7.4 wt%, the rGO was shown to agglomerate. The rGO improved the hardness, fracture toughness, and thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC by up to 16%, 20%, and 53%, respectively. The composite characteristics reached the values of HV = 26.1 GPa, K1 C = 7.4 MPa · m 1/2 , and k = 69 W/m · K. Carbon black additive formed roughly equiaxial two micrometre inclusions and was not observed to impact the investigated properties.
the TiB2 matrix resulted in a material hardness of 19.4 GPa, toughness of 6.9 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 , and flexural strength of 797 MPa. Zhou et al. [10] showed an enhancement of SiC high-temperature tribological properties with an addition  of  20%  of  TiB2.  Sintering  to  high  density  is  still  very  challenging. We previously showed that reactive hot pressing produces high composite density at lowered temperatures and shorter processing times (see references below).

3.3. Characterization of sintered materials
TiB2-25 vol%  SiC  ceramics  presented  in  [39]  showed  a  thermal conductivity coefficient of 50 W/mK, which is close to the TS00 reference sample. However, adding 2 wt% of graphene led to an 8% value reduction. The dissimilarity of the data with the results of the current work shows the necessity of additional research on the mechanisms of carbon  additives ' influence  on  ceramic  matrix  composite  thermal properties.
In summary, we can conclude that rGO addition improves mechanical  properties  and  thermal  conductivity  compared  to  pure  TiB2-SiC ceramic or composites with carbon black as an additive. The improved properties could have multiple explanations and contributing factors. There is a higher oxygen content in the reference than in the different composite structures of the sintered materials. Carbon additives themselves can potentially contribute to toughness, hardness, and thermal properties. Since the samples containing carbon black showed similar or worse  mechanical  properties  and  thermal  conductivity,  it  can  be assumed that the reduced oxygen content is not the determining factor. The  larger  areas  of  continuous  SiC  or  TiB2 observed  in  the  rGOcontaining sample could be an explanation for the higher thermal conductivity because of fewer grain boundaries. It should also be noted that the most essential property improvements in rGO-contained composites coincide with the rGO existing in the form of separate sheets. The sheet agglomeration  at  higher  additive  concentrations  results  in  property similarity for graphite/rGO-containing materials. Therefore, it can be assumed that the rGO sheets that remain in the matrix after sintering contribute to the mechanical and thermal properties with specific rGOinherent mechanisms.

question: How do NbC coatings modified with 75 mol% HfC perform under oxyacetylene flame ablation testing for 180 seconds, and what is the effective ablation time compared to the 50 mol% HfC composition?
answer: The sources do not provide information on the performance of NbC coatings modified with 75 mol% HfC under oxyacetylene flame ablation testing for 180 seconds. Additionally, the effective ablation time of the 75 mol% HfC composition compared to the 50 mol% HfC composition is not covered in the provided sources. However, it is mentioned that the effective ablation time of the NbC coating mixed with 50 mol% HfC was approximately 120 s, which is more than twice that of the pure NbC coating [1].

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
In this work, NbC coatings modified with different contents (0, 25 and 50 mol%) of HfC were deposited on SiCcoated C/C composites via supersonic atmospheric plasma spraying to study the effect of HfC on the ablationresistance  of  the  NbC  coatings  under  an  oxyacetylene  torch.  The  phase  composition  and  chemical  states  of Nb2O5 -66.67  mol%  HfO2  plates  heated  up  to  2100 ◦ C  in  an  Ar  atmosphere  were  investigated.  The  results confirmed that the effective ablation time of the NbC-coating mixed with 50 mol% HfC was approximately 120 s, which  is  more  than  twice  that  of  the  pure  NbC  coating.  During  air  quenching,  NbO  and  NbO2  ultimately transformed  into  Nb2O5,  producing  compressive  stress  that  stabilized  the  t-HfO2 crystals.  Furthermore,  the formation of hafnium oxide significantly improved the thermal stability and viscosity of the Nb -O film, and the niobium  suboxide-based  solid  solution,  possessing  an  attractive  self-healing  performance,  increased  the compactness of the HfO2-based solid solution layer are responsible for the superior ablation performance of the NbC-HfC coating.

3.1. Ablation property
Fig. 2 illustrates the macro-morphology of the coated samples after the oxyacetylene test. A pit is observed in the central ablation region of the NbC coating after ablation for 60 s. However, a white glassy phase that is able to resist the infiltration of O2 and heat forms in the NbC-HfC coatings after ablation for 90 s. In addition, the film of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating is exfoliated, possibly due to the mismatch of the thermal stresses in the outer oxide layers [19]. After a period of 120 s, severe damage with several pores occurs in the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating, which can be attributed to the denudation of NbO ss .  However, in the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating, only a porous oxide skeleton is observed in the  central  ablation  region,  indicating  a  significant  improvement  in ablation-resistance behaviour.
The temperature variations of the coatings in the oxyacetylene test are  plotted  in  Fig.  3.  The  temperature  of  the  NbC  coating  increases rapidly at the initial stage and then decreases due to the presence of gaseous products. However, the temperature of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating increases after 80 s, suggesting the formation of a smooth and dense oxide layer. Furthermore, due to the absence of a smooth oxide film of the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating, the temperature of the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating is lower than that of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating, and  stabilizes  after  75  s.  Additionally,  compared  to  the  pure  NbC coating, each of the NbC-HfC coatings, without temperature reduction at the final stage, achieves a better thermal protection for the SiC transition layer.

3.1. Ablation property
The linear and mass ablation rates of the specimens are listed in Table 3. The mass ablation rates of the NbC-HfC coatings are all negative. In contrast, after ablation for 60 s, the NbC coating has a positive result  with  the  highest  thickness  reduction,  indicating  a  significant consumption of NbC coating by mechanical denudation. Moreover, the net  increments  of  mass  and  thickness  of  the  NbC-HfC  coatings  after ablation for 120 s are less than those after ablation for 90 s, suggesting that the coatings are mechanically eroded by the oxyacetylene flame before 120 s. In addition, at the same ablation time, the net increments of the mass and thickness of the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating are more R. He et al.
Fig. 4. Phase composition of the NbC-HfC coatings after ablation for 120 s and heated Nb2O5 -66.67 mol% HfO2 plates at 2100 ◦ C in Ar atmosphere for 2 h. (a) X-ray diffractograms; (b) enlarged view of (a). ( 160 ◦ C :  the  ball-milled Nb2O5 -66.67 mol% HfO2 slurry was dried at 160 ◦ C before cold pressing for Nb2O5 -66.67 mol% HfO2 plates; dots, pink dashes, and grey lines represent the raw data, fitted curve, and baseline, respectively.)

4. Conclusions
To summarize, NbC coatings mixed with various contents of HfC were  successfully  synthesized  on  the  surface  of  SiC-coated  C/Cs  via SAPS.  A  test  of  the  ablation-resistance  of  the  coated  samples  and investigation of the phase composition of the heat-treated Nb2O5 -66.67 mol% HfO2  plates were conducted. The findings  of the  study are  as follows: By increasing the HfC content of NbC-HfC coating from 0 to 50 mol%, the effective ablation time increased by over 100% to 120 s. This outstanding ablation-resistance is attributed to a synergistic effect that the  HfO2  greatly  improves  the  thermal  stability  and  viscosity  of  the Nb -O film, and the NbO ss possessing an excellent self-healing performance increases the compactness and mechanical strength of the HfO2, ss layer.  Furthermore,  Nb2O5 stabilized  the  t-HfO2 crystals  after  the oxyacetylene test of the NbC-HfC coatings, while the crystal structures of HfO2 after the heat-treatment all remained monoclinic; the reasons are that the ionic radius of Nb 5 + is much smaller than that of Hf 4 + ;  and during the air quenching, the niobium suboxides are re-oxidized and eventually  transformed  into  Nb2O5  with  a  volume  expansion  which produces compressive stress on the t-HfO2 crystals. Thus, Nb2O5  stabilized t-HfO2. While, the niobium suboxides without phase change in Ar atmosphere have no effect on the phase transformation as a function of the temperature of HfO2.

3.1. Ablation property
than those of the NbC-25 mol% HfC coating, indicating that the NbC-50 mol% HfC coating is more resistant to erosion. Therefore, modification with  HfC  significantly  improves  the  ablation-resistance  of  the  NbC coating,  with  the  NbC-50  mol%  HfC  coating  displaying  the  best performance.

A B S T R A C T
fluidity. HfC-30 mol% NbC coating forms a ' role-reversal honeycomb ' structure comprising HfO2 columnar crystals bonded by niobium oxide under an oxyacetylene flame at 2.4 MW/m 2 , exhibiting excellent ablation resistance [16]. Before the HfC -NbC coatings are applied in thermal  protection,  their  ablation  resistance  limits  need  to  be  studied, particularly at service temperatures exceeding 2500 ◦ C (oxyacetylene flame at approximately 4.2 MW/m 2 ). However, the density, thickness, and  bonding  strength  with  substrate  of  the  HfC -NbC  coatings  are limited. This results in coating transfer under the oxyacetylene flame before stable oxides form on the surface, complicating the analysis of physicochemical changes in the Hf -C -Nb system. To address these challenges,  vacuum hot-pressed sintering  (VHPS) technology [17,18] was  employed  to  synthesise  NbC-modified  HfC  (Hf1x Nb x C)  UHTCs capable of successfully completing ablation tests under an oxyacetylene flame at 4.2 MW/m 2 . Furthermore, to investigate the wide-temperature range protection performance of Hf1x Nb x C, the anti-oxidation property of Hf1x Nb x C at 1700 ◦ C should be studied.

3.3. Ablation behavior of the coatings
Ablation performance of the coatings was measured under oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame. After being ablated for 90 s, the mass ablation rate of pure ZrB2-SiC/SiC coated C/C sample is 0.20 mg/s. While, the mass ablation rate of the coated sample with HfC nanowires is positive ( -0.12 mg/s). During ablation, on the one hand, the coating would gain weight because of its oxidation; on the other hand, mechanical denudation and volatilization of oxide layer would lead to weight loss. By incorporating nanowires, the weight gain of the sample is predominant, so the mass ablation rate is minus. Above results imply that HfC nanowires could inhibit the oxidized layer being denudated, which could reduce the mass loss of the oxide layer.
XRDpattern of the coating with HfC nanowires after ablation for 90 s is shown in Fig. 8. After ablation, ZrO2 phase emerges and the ZrB2 and SiC phases disappear. In ablation environment, ZrB2 would be oxidized to ZrO2 and B2O3, and SiC would be oxidized to SiO2, SiO, CO and CO2. After ablation, only ZrO2 remained on the sample surface owing to its high melting point (2953 K). SiO2, SiO, B2O3 and other gaseous products wouldbeevaporated /uniFB01 ercely in ablation environment, so they could not be found in the XRD pattern.

2.2. Ablation test and heat-treatment

3.3. Ablation resistance of Hf1-xNbxC
Fig. 7 shows optical images of Hf1x Nb x C after ablation. At 2.4 MW/ m 2 , the surface of HfC fractures; however, the oxide layer of Hf0.9Nb0.1C remains  dense,  indicating  that  doping  with  NbC  alleviates  the  stress mismatch.  Hf0.8Nb0.2C  develops  a  dense  filmy  structure  that  significantly  mitigates  the  pulverised  oxidation  of  HfC.  Hf0.7Nb0.3C  and Hf0.6Nb0.4C form continuous oxide films in central ablation region, and the increased NbC content intensifies the transfer of the Nb -O liquids. At 4.2 MW/m 2 , HfC fractures owing to the stress mismatch during cyclic ablation. A dense oxide film forms after the introduction of 10 mol% NbC. The surface of Hf0.8Nb0.2C is rough, and the residual oxide film in border ablation region exhibits peeling at the centre. The transfer of Nb -O liquids intensifies, causing Hf0.7Nb0.3C to form an ablation pit. As the NbC content increases to 40 mol%, the ablation pit deepens.

2.2. Ablation test and heat-treatment
The ablation properties of the NbC/SiC and (NbC-HfC)/SiC doublelayer coated C/Cs were tested in an oxyacetylene torch system according to the National Standard of Ablation. The technological details are given  in  Table  2.  The  samples  were  vertically  placed  under  the oxyacetylene flame. The inner diameter of the oxyacetylene gun tip was 2 mm, and the distance between the specimens and the gun tip was 10 mm. The surface  temperature  of  the  samples  was  detected  using  an infrared thermometer (RaytekMR1SCSF). The linear and mass ablation rates of the specimens were calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively, below. The final ablation rates of the specimens were the average ablation rates of the three samples.

question: How does the viscosity and thermal stability of the oxide film formed on NbC-ZrC coatings change when subjected to peak temperatures of 2500°C instead of 2100°C during oxyacetylene testing?
answer:
The provided sources do not contain specific information regarding how the viscosity and thermal stability of the oxide film formed on NbC-ZrC coatings change when subjected to peak temperatures of 2500°C instead of 2100°C during oxyacetylene testing. However, based on the available information:

- The Nb -O film, with a liquidus temperature of approximately 2000°C, primarily composed of niobium suboxides (such as NbO and NbO2) at superhigh temperatures [3].
- Zr0.9Nb0.1C developed a 'free-standing' single-phase ZrO2 film without surface defects under an oxyacetylene flame at 4.2 MW/m² for 40 s × 6, exhibiting excellent high-temperature and long-term ablation resistance [10].
- The increasing duration of exposure improves the diffusion of Zr element, which increases the sintering degree of ZrO2, resulting in further densification of the 'free-standing' film [10].

These points suggest that the NbC-ZrC coatings can form a stable and dense oxide film at high temperatures, but the specific changes in viscosity and thermal stability at 2500°C compared to 2100°C are not covered in the provided sources.

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
subjected to an oxyacetylene flame at 2.4 MW/m 2 for 300 s, ZrC-20 mol % NbC coating develops  a ' role-reversal  honeycomb ' structure  consisting  of  ZrO2  columnar crystals healed by niobium oxide [22] and exhibits  outstanding  ablation  resistance.  To  study  their  potential  for thermal protection applications, it is crucial to investigate the wide-temperature  range  erosion  performance  of  ZrC -NbC  coatings, particularly their ablation resistance near 2500 ◦ C (oxyacetylene flame at approximately 4.2 MW/m 2 ) and their oxidation resistance up to 1700 ◦ C. However, analysing the physiochemical changes of ZrC -NbC coatings [22] remains challenging owing to their insufficient thickness, low density, and poor bonding strength with the substrate, which cause them to peel off before the formation of stable oxides. To address these issues, this  study  employs  vacuum  hot-pressed  sintering  (VHPS)  technology [23 -25] to prepare dense and size controllable Zr1x Nb x C UHTCs with uniform elemental distributions.
One of the primary challenges in elucidating the oxidation-ablation mechanism of Zr1x Nb x C lies in identifying the phase evolution on its surface. Investigations into the phase composition of Zr -O -Nb systems have been limited to temperatures up to 1950 ◦ C, and the reported solid compounds formed by Nb2O5 and ZrO2, as well as their decomposition points, show inconsistences [26,27]. To address this, ZrO2 -Nb2O5 ceramics were heat-treated in Ar ranging from 1300 to 2100 º C, and the phase  composition  at  2100 º C  was  accurately  determined  [22].

A B S T R A C T
fluidity. HfC-30 mol% NbC coating forms a ' role-reversal honeycomb ' structure comprising HfO2 columnar crystals bonded by niobium oxide under an oxyacetylene flame at 2.4 MW/m 2 , exhibiting excellent ablation resistance [16]. Before the HfC -NbC coatings are applied in thermal  protection,  their  ablation  resistance  limits  need  to  be  studied, particularly at service temperatures exceeding 2500 ◦ C (oxyacetylene flame at approximately 4.2 MW/m 2 ). However, the density, thickness, and  bonding  strength  with  substrate  of  the  HfC -NbC  coatings  are limited. This results in coating transfer under the oxyacetylene flame before stable oxides form on the surface, complicating the analysis of physicochemical changes in the Hf -C -Nb system. To address these challenges,  vacuum hot-pressed sintering  (VHPS) technology [17,18] was  employed  to  synthesise  NbC-modified  HfC  (Hf1x Nb x C)  UHTCs capable of successfully completing ablation tests under an oxyacetylene flame at 4.2 MW/m 2 . Furthermore, to investigate the wide-temperature range protection performance of Hf1x Nb x C, the anti-oxidation property of Hf1x Nb x C at 1700 ◦ C should be studied.

1. Introduction
To  improve  the  use  of  carbon/carbon  composites  (C/Cs)  in  newgeneration  aircrafts,  coating  technology  is  employed  to  optimize  the anti-oxidation property and ablation-resistance of C/Cs [1 -2]. Coatings consisting  of  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  have  been proven to effectively protect SiC-coated C/Cs [3 -6]. However, a single UHTC coating forms a porous oxide layer due to its pulverized-oxidation behaviour,  thus  limiting  the  thermal  protection  performance.  Recent studies have reported that liquid SiO2 can heal the cracks formed on SiCmodified  UHTC  coatings,  enabling  the  construction  of  a  dense  and smooth oxide layer at high temperatures and improving their ablation behaviour [7 -10]. However, SiO2  volatilizes at 1650 ◦ C [11] and SiO sublimates above 1727 ◦ C [12], these reduce the compactness of the oxide film. Moreover, SiC (Tm ≈ 2700 ◦ C) decreases the melting temperature of the UHTC coating. These drawbacks limit the applications of SiC-modified UHTC coatings.
In a previous study, we reported that among several UHTC coatings, only the NbC coating acquired a smooth and dense oxide layer in the central ablation region after the oxyacetylene test [13]. 1. Introduction
ZrC, a key ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC), has been widely recognized for its capability to provide substantial thermal protection for aerospace vehicles operating in extreme environments [1,2]. However, its performance is hindered by the extremely high melting point (T m ≈ 2710 ◦ C [3]) and detrimental phase-transformation stress [4] of ZrO2, resulting in pulverised oxidation [5,6] and limiting its ability to inhibit  oxygen  diffusion.  Doping  with  Si-based  ceramics  [7,8] forms Si -O glass that can heal surface defects; however, SiO2  evaporates at 1650 ◦ C [9] and SiO sublimates at 1727 ◦ C [10], leading to a porous oxide film. Rare-earth oxides such as La2O3 [11], Yb2O3 [12], and Y2O3 [13] can  stabilise  tetragonal  ZrO2  (t-ZrO2),  thereby  mitigating  stress mismatches; however, these materials also increase the concentration of oxygen vacancies. High-T m components, including TiC [14], W [15], and TaC [16], help maintain the high liquidus temperature of ZrC; however, the thermal stabilities of their oxides are insufficient [17 -19], resulting in discontinuous oxide films. To date, no public reports have demonstrated the formation of dense single-phase ZrO2 films on the surface of ZrC coatings after ablation.
NbC oxidises to a liquid state at 1500 ◦ C in air [20] and forms a dense filmy structure at 2000 -2100 ◦ C under an oxyacetylene flame [21], demonstrating  excellent  wide-temperature  range  flowability.  When
