1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), particularly HfC, effectively protect aerospace vehicles in extreme environments [1,2]. However, the high melting point (T m ≈ 2810 ◦ C [3]) and phase-transformation stress [4] of HfO2 result in pulverised oxidation of HfC  coating  [5,6],  limiting  its  ability  to  inhibit  oxygen  diffusion. Component modification of HfC coatings has been extensively studied. For instance, under doping with SiC [7], SiO2 evaporates at 1650 ◦ C [8] and SiO sublimates at 1710 ◦ C [9], resulting in a porous oxide film. Introduction of TaC [7], the low T m of Ta2O5 [10] leads to a discontinuous  oxide  film.  For  HfO2 [11],  although  the  formation  of  gaseous products is suppressed, the porous structure of the HfO2 layer does not significantly densify. Furthermore, Y2Hf2O7 coating [12] forms a dense oxide film under an oxyacetylene flame at 2.4 MW/m 2 ; however, Y 3 + increases the concentration of oxygen vacancies [13]. To date, no public reports have documented the formation of a dense single-phase HfO2 film on the surface of HfC coating after ablation.
NbC oxidises into a liquid state at 1500 º C in air [14] and produces a dense  filmy  structure  under  an  oxyacetylene  flame  in  the  range  of 2000 -2100 º C [15],  indicating  its  excellent  wide-temperature  range

A B S T R A C T
Static oxidation and oxyacetylene ablation tests were conducted on vacuum hot-pressed NbC-modified ZrC (Zr1x Nb x C) ultra-high temperature ceramics. For revealing the oxidation mechanism, the phase composition of heattreated ZrO2 -Nb2O5 ceramics in Ar was identified. The results demonstrated that the stable phases of the ZrO250 mol% NbO2.5  ceramic heat-treated at 1700 -1900 ◦ C were Nb2Zr6O17  and NbO2. The oxidation resistant temperature limit (T l ) for Zr1x Nb x C was 1650 ˂ T l ˂ 1700 ◦ C. At 1650 ◦ C for 30 h, Zr0.7Nb0.3C formed a dense oxide  film  comprising  bulky  ZrO2 bonded  by  lathy  Nb2Zr6O17,  exhibiting  optimal  oxidation  resistance. Zr0.9Nb0.1C developed a dense ' free-standing ' single-phase ZrO2  film at 4.2 MW/m 2 for 40 s × 6, achieving a modification effect on the ablation morphology of ZrC nearing 100%. Furthermore, the thermostability mechanism of ZrO2  to Nb2O5  and the formation mechanism of the ' free-standing ' structure were analysed.

5. Conclusions

- (iii)  Enduring  an  oxyacetylene  flame  at  2.4 MW/m 2 for 120 s, Zr0.8Nb0.2C formed an oxide film that consisted of t-ZrO2  with traces of m/o-ZrO2 and Nb2Zr8O21 and had the highest compactness, achieving outstanding ablation resistance. Increasing the NbC content (0 → 30 mol%) promoted the growth of ZrO2 grains and densification of the GBs, improving the filmforming ability of ZrO2.
- (iv)  Zr0.9Nb0.1C developed a ' free-standing ' single-phase ZrO2  film without surface defects under an oxyacetylene flame at 4.2 MW/ m 2 for 40 s × 6, exhibiting excellent high-temperature and longterm ablation resistance. Furthermore, the Nb -O liquids facilitated the growth of ZrO2  grains, forming ZrO2  flakes, and the fusion  of  ZrO2 deformed  grains  and  flakes  produced  ZrO2 columnar  crystals.  With  the  melting  of  the  outer  ends  of  the columnar crystals into an oxide film, the ' free-standing ' structure formed.

3.3. Behaviour of Zr1-xNbxC under ablative conditions
Fig. 7 shows optical images of Zr1x Nb x C after ablation. At 2.4 MW/ m 2 for  120 s  (Fig.  7a),  the  oxide  layer  of  Zr0.9Nb0.1C  becomes  more intact  compared  with  that  of  ZrC,  and  Zr0.8Nb0.2C  develops  a  filmy structure in central ablation region, indicating that doping with NbC improves the sintering degree. The oxide film of Zr0.7Nb0.3C expands; however, the surface becomes rough, indicating insufficient viscosity. At 4.2 MW/m 2 for  40 s × 3 (Fig.  7a),  the  oxide  layer  of  ZrC  fragments; however, Zr0.9Nb0.1C generates a filmy structure. With the transfer of the liquids, the surface roughness increases. Additionally, molten oxides transfer from the central to transition ablation regions of Zr0.9Nb0.1C after ablation at 4.2 MW/m 2 for 40 s × 6 (Fig. 7b); however, the oxide film  remains  dense,  exhibiting  excellent  high-temperature  and  longterm ablation resistance.

4.4. Formation mechanism of the ' free-standing ' ZrO2  film

Additionally, at Zones II/III interface, the compositional and structural transitions are gradual (Fig. 12c), causing the residual Nb -O liquids  to  adhere  to  Zone  II,  and  thereby  producing  a  downward contraction force (F s ) (Fig. 14c). In contrast, at Zones III/IV interface, abrupt  changes  in  the  composition  and  structure,  coupled  with  the presence  of  numerous  pores  that  reduced  the  contact  area  between Zones  III  and  IV  (Figs.  12d  and  12e),  weaken  the  bonding  strength. Consequently,  the ' free-standing ' film  detaches  near  Zones  III/IV interface in Zone III (Figs. 12d and 12e) under the combined action of F w , F g , and F s , resulting in a critical thickness (h c ) (Fig. 14c) that is not conducive to long-term ablation. However, the increasing duration improves the diffusion of Zr element, which increases the sintering degree of  ZrO2,  resulting  in  further  densification  of  the ' free-standing ' film (Figs. 11m, 11k, and 11n); moreover, the ' free-standing ' film comprises high-purity  ZrO2 (Figs.  9d,  10j  and  10 k),  ensuring  stable  physiochemical  changes.  Therefore,  Zr0.9Nb0.1C  exhibits  excellent  hightemperature and long-term ablation resistance. Comparing ZrC doped with SiC [7], La2O3 [11] and CY2 [13], which forms simple oxide layer with more defects, NbC-modified ZrC develops a ' free-standing ' single phase ZrO2  film without surface defects due to the wide-temperature range  flowability  of  Nb -O  system,  which  facilitates  the  growth  of ZrO2  grains,  formation  of  a  temperature  gradient,  and  fusion  of  the surface, enabling the thermal protection up to 2500 º C.

A B S T R A C T
In this work, NbC coatings modified with different contents (0, 25 and 50 mol%) of HfC were deposited on SiCcoated C/C composites via supersonic atmospheric plasma spraying to study the effect of HfC on the ablationresistance  of  the  NbC  coatings  under  an  oxyacetylene  torch.  The  phase  composition  and  chemical  states  of Nb2O5 -66.67  mol%  HfO2  plates  heated  up  to  2100 ◦ C  in  an  Ar  atmosphere  were  investigated.  The  results confirmed that the effective ablation time of the NbC-coating mixed with 50 mol% HfC was approximately 120 s, which  is  more  than  twice  that  of  the  pure  NbC  coating.  During  air  quenching,  NbO  and  NbO2  ultimately transformed  into  Nb2O5,  producing  compressive  stress  that  stabilized  the  t-HfO2 crystals.  Furthermore,  the formation of hafnium oxide significantly improved the thermal stability and viscosity of the Nb -O film, and the niobium  suboxide-based  solid  solution,  possessing  an  attractive  self-healing  performance,  increased  the compactness of the HfO2-based solid solution layer are responsible for the superior ablation performance of the NbC-HfC coating.

3.1. Results of one time heating up experiments
As shown from Fig. 4a, residual strength of ZS begins to decrease when the central temperature reaches w 2000 /C14 C, which can be attributed to high thermal stress caused by temperature gradient and thermal expansion mismatch between SiC and ZrB2. The specimen is failed when center temperature reaches w 2300 /C14 C.
Visible from Fig. 4b, residual strength of ZS10C changes little with increasing center temperature from 1800 /C14 C to 2200 /C14 C at 1 or 5 times repeated heating. Although interface damage between SiC and ZrB2 began to form when the center of specimen heated up to > 2000 /C14 C, additives of graphite improved crack resistance of ZS10C [7]. The specimen was suddenly failed when the center temperature reaches w 2400 /C14 C.
After 10 times thermal shock, residual strengths of both the two materials decreased clearly, and residual strength of ZS remained about 30% when center temperature reaches 2200 /C14 C, and it is relatively lower than that of ZS10C (40%). It implies that ZS10C has a better resistance for repeating thermal shock.

3.2. Phase quantification of surface oxide layer

Similarly, among the two Hf-containing samples, the content of HfO2 in ternary is more than quaternary is due to the high content of HfC in (50 vol%) in ternary,  which  also  led  to  the  segregation  of  the  phase  along  with complete solid-solution phase in the sample [7]. The content of unary oxide phases reduced from 82.7% in the binary compound to 21.5% in ternary and 12.9% in the quaternary compound. Hf-Ta oxide complex phases follow most Ti-Ta-rich oxide at the surface in quaternary UHTC while Hf-Ta complex oxide phase in the ternary system and unary oxide of Ta-Nb in binary UHTC. The majority of the oxide phase in the quaternary  system  is  rich  in  Ti-based  oxide.  This  could  be  due  to  the segregation of the TiC phase (up to 3 wt%, see Fig. 3) near the grain boundaries, which forms additional complex mixed oxide of Ti during plasma oxidation. Also, Ti is the smallest, lightest, and oxidation-prone among  the  metallic  elements  of  (Ta,Nb,Hf,Ti)C  [12].  It  is  worth mentioning that the high-resolution SEM on this sample reported in the recent study by authors could not detect TiC segregation [7] due to the smaller amount of undissolved TiC (~3 wt%) in the sample. EBSD, a more precise technique, could visualize the distribution of phases and quantify it qualitatively (see Fig. 3).

A B S T R A C T
Multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has garnered attention due to their improved thermo-mechanical and oxidation properties. MC-UHTC was progressively formed by adding a UHTC component at  each  step,  producing  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary  UHTC  systems,  processed  by  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS). We investigate the role of multi-elements and thermochemical stability of the surface oxide layer formed during plasma arc jet exposure ( T > 2500 ◦ C) of MC-UHTCs using core-level X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) correlated with the structural investigation. The results indicate that adding a UHTC component improves oxidation resistance due to the assemblage of refractory phases forming complex mixed oxides via preferential oxidation. XPS spectra of Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, and Hf4f showed that the nature of the chemical bonds switched from metal carbides to metal oxides (and metal oxy-carbides) in the binary system with retained metal carbides in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. The absence of a metal carbide peak in the high-resolution C1s scan of the binary system also supports this finding. The principal complex mixed oxide phases emanated in the exiguous oxidation of MC-UHTCs are quantified using Rietveld analysis. Overall, this study suggests that MC-UHTC is a promising material for possible application in thermal protection systems (TPS).

These materials have gathered quick attention because of their remarkable properties, which include enhanced mechanical properties [9 -12], improved fracture toughness [11,13,14],  tribological  performance  [15 -17],  creep  resistance  [18], and low thermal conductivity [11,19 -21].

4. Summary
The MC-UHTCs showed remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance during plasma exposure testing compared to binary UHTCs. This has been attributed to the preferential oxidation and a reduction in low melting temperature unary oxide content in the surface oxide scale from > 82% in binary UHTC to < 13% in MC-UHTC. A thermally stable complex protective oxide layer of Hf6Ta2O17 (a primary phase) in MCUHTCs has further suppressed the oxide formation. As a result of the relative thermal stability of transition metals and components in the MCUHTCs, preferential oxidation occurs in MC-UHTCs, i.e., Hf in ternary and Ti in quaternary UHTC system, while both are forming the protective mixed complex oxide layer to remarkably improve ( > 40 times) the oxidation resistance over binary and unary UHTC systems. Both XRD and XPS analysis showed retention of metal carbide phase only in MCUHTCs. The study suggests that MC-UHTCs designed to form a mixed complex protective oxide layer of group IV element under service conditions elicit extended potential in extreme space vehicle applications.

3.3. Surface oxidation state
Fig. 5 e-g shows the high-resolution elemental scan for Ta, Hf, Nb, and  Ti  metal  cations  in  quaternary  UHTC  system.  The  peaks  corresponding to Ta, Hf, and Nb were similar to those obtained in the ternary UHTC system, albeit with slight shift ( < 5%) due to the addition of TiC in the quaternary system. Similar to ternary UHTC, two additional minor peaks of Ta4f7/2 and Ta4f5/2 with binding energies 24.4 eV, and 26.2 eV, respectively, correspond to the carbide peak of Ta with a shift towards the higher energy due to the solid-solution formation in a four component system. The shift in the M GLYPH<0> C towards higher energy could be due to the formation of oxy-carbide. The substitution of C by O in the M GLYPH<0> C bond leading to the formation of oxy-carbide has shown to have a higher binding energy of 1 -2 eV [28]. The intensity ratio of Ti2p1/2 and Ti2p3/2 is constrained by 1:2, which is maintained in the present case. As per the literature, Ti peaks corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sets at 453.74 eV, and 459.79 eV while Ti(IV) oxide (TiO2) peaks corresponding to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sets at 458.59 and 464.31 eV [46]. The observed peak position of  Ti2p,  which  corresponds  to  2p3/2,  is  at  458.8  eV,  confirming  the presence of the rutile TiO2 phase and a shoulder peak at 457.32 eV. This shoulder peak can be due to Ti ' s other complex mixed oxide phases, as observed in the XRD analysis (see Fig. 2, Table 2). Hence, in the quaternary system, the major oxidation by-products comprises of two mixed complex oxide phases of Ta-Ti-O and Hf-Ta-O (and not TiO2  alone), analogous  to  the  results  obtained  from  Rietveld  analysis  (Fig.  2, Table 2).

3.3. Surface oxidation state
Table 3 summarizes the Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, Hf4f, O1s, and C1s peaks from XPS spectra collected for the plasma exposed binary, ternary, and quaternary UHTCs along with the literature peaks for carbides and oxides. The relative intensity of elements ' peaks in XPS spectra can provide insight into oxidation thermodynamic and kinetic theories. This can be well  understood by comparing the relative intensities of various elements in a particular sample. The Gibbs free energy of oxide formation for Group V (Ta,Nb) is lower than that of Group IV (Hf,Ti), other things being equal [28]. Hence, the preferential oxide growth of Ti and Hf are more favorable once the oxidation process is activated [26], as observed from the XRD analysis (see Table 2). 5. Conclusions
The objective of this work was to experimentally test the predictions of the analytical and computational thermodynamic approach outlined in a companion publication. This methodology was applied to the prediction of substrate and oxide composition after oxidation of a high entropy carbide and boride containing Hf, Zr, Ti, Ta and Nb in equimolar amounts. The experimentally determined oxide and substrate compositions were found to be in good agreement with the thermodynamic predictions, which describe well the local equilibrium at the reaction interface for both the carbide and the diboride case. In general, the oxidation of high entropy materials studied here occurred by preferential oxidation of each component according to the relative thermodynamic favorabilities of their respective oxides. Group IV oxides, being most favorable, formed preferentially. The experimental results further elucidated that within each group, preferential oxidation can also occur according to relative thermodynamic stability: Hf and Zr preferentially oxidize over Ti as shown in the boride case, while Ta preferentially oxidizes over Nb as shown in the carbide case. The substrate composition of group V metal-rich carbides and borides, which are or have the potential to form phases with different crystal structures from the original ceramic, was accurately predicted. Whereas complex oxides were observed experimentally, this is not a significant limitation to the prediction of preferential oxidation, as the formation of these complex oxides was dominated by predicted oxide constituents.
This work shows that the approach outlined in Part I provides insight into the thermodynamics driving the oxidation behavior of high entropy materials and can be used as a tool for the design of high entropy materials for oxidation resistance.

4.1. Carbides
(HfZrTiTaNb)C, (HfZrTiTaMo)C and (HfZrTiMoW)C oxidize preferentially according to the relative thermodynamic favorabilities [8], with group IV oxides dominating in the oxide scales, and Ta, Mo and W (in their  respective  materials)  following.  This  is  not  to  say  that  at  these conditions, the group V and VI elements did not oxidize. Evidence for Ta2O5 is seen in XRD data as well as evidence of Mo and W oxides (See Supplement).  (HfZrTiMoW)C  exhibited  lower  material  consumption compared to the other high entropy compositions. While the surface on (HfZrTiMoW)C exhibits a mostly dense scale (Fig. 11), examination in cross-section  reveals  some  porosity  just  below  the  surface  and  the presence of fine particles of group VI (Mo,W) carbide phase. Oxidation of this phase results in volatile oxides that leave the system [5], which was expected. Both Mo and W were observed in the deposits in the cooler regions of the experimental set-up, e.g., chamber walls and clips holding the sample, suggesting that both group VI elements had at least begun oxidizing (we still see remnants of Mo and W containing carbide on the surface). More Mo is observed in these downstream deposits, which may be an indication of Mo oxides being more volatile than W oxides as proposed through thermochemical analyses [43]. W additions are also known to promote densification of the group IV oxides [34,36], and these may explain the mostly dense appearance of the group IV-rich oxide, particularly around the group VI-rich carbide grains. Overall, this behavior led to a more uniform attack and may be partially responsible for the lower material consumption observed for (HfZrTiMoW)C.
Although all samples exhibited intergranular oxidation, the group V

4. Carbides

Below 1800 ◦ C, kinetics were attributed to O2, CO, and CO2 gaseous transport controlled oxidation, as temperature was found to have little impact on oxidation rate -rates of gaseous diffusion are not heavily influenced by temperature. The small increase in rate below 1800 ◦ C was attributed to the oxide becoming more ductile and the pore structure being able to sinter and collapse. Courtright et al. concluded the transition in mechanism  may  be  due  to  the  oxide  becoming  increasingly  dense  with temperature.

a b s t r a c t
Refractory high entropy materials have garnered significant research interest due to their potential ability to fill a need in high temperature structural applications. However, challenges remain with respect to designing for oxidation resistance. A knowledge gap exists with respect to a rigorous understanding of the mechanisms driving oxidation processes unique to high entropy materials. This work provides an experimental complement to a companion publication, which outlines analytical and computational thermodynamic approaches that are envisioned to aid the design of refractory high entropy materials containing group IV (Hf, Zr, Ti) and group V (Ta, Nb) constituents. In this work, (Hf 0.2 Zr 0.2 Ti 0.2 Ta 0.2 Nb 0.2 ) carbide and diboride specimens were exposed at 1700 ° C in 1% O 2 for 5 min. Experimental results show good agreement with the computational predictions for the same temperature, despite differences in the overall morphology of the oxidized regions. The carbide formed porous oxides, while the diboride formed a denser external scale. Oxidation products are dominated by group IV oxides, depleting the underlying materials, which were found to consist of primarily group V carbides and borides respectively. The results provide a first look at the oxidation of high entropy UHTCs at ultra-high temperatures and validate the preferential nature of high entropy material oxidation predicted by the computational approach developed for the study of this new class of materials.

Post-exposure of the samples showed that 50HO-50TO survived the plasma exposure even up to 3 min, with some apparent cracks on the sample surface and a slight change in sample color (white) due to the removal of diffused C, see Fig. 7a. However, the 70HO-30TO sample couldn ' t sustain the thermal shock associated with plasma exposure and broke into two pieces. 3.2. Phase quantification of surface oxide layer
To determine the exact amount of phases present on the surface of each sample after plasma arc jet exposure, Rietveld refinement is performed over the XRD pattern, presented in Fig. 2. It is evident from Fig. 2 that  the  major  oxide  peaks  correspond  to  the  simple  oxide  phase  of Ta2O5 in a binary UHTC, which then changes to complex mixed oxides of (Hf-Ta) and (Ti-Ta), respectively in ternary and quaternary UHTC systems. The percentage of various phases present in the oxidized samples is tabulated in Table 2. The phases on the oxide scale have been quantified  based  on  the  prospective  oxide  phases  mentioned  in  Table  1. However, insufficient thermodynamic data on complex oxide in the case of a ternary and quaternary system limits their exact quantification. The overlap in the peak position of the multiple oxide phases in the XRD pattern  of  the  surface  oxide  layer  in  each  sample  makes  the  exact quantification of phases arduous. However, the XRD pattern was only fitted with the phases considering the peak intensity, and its position as a good match.
The XRD pattern on the plasma exposed sample surface shows the complete oxidation of the solid-solution phase in binary UHTC system while it was retained in MC-UHTC systems. It is also evident from the thicker oxide scale seen in Fig. 1 a . It is also to be mentioned here that the solid solution phase still exists in the unaffected region of the binary UHTC system.

The formation of a porous scale upon oxidation of the carbide also results in higher oxygen partial pressures at the reaction interface in the carbide, which allows for the further oxidation of Ta over Nb (Ta 2 O 5 is more stable than Nb 2 O 5 ). Fig. 7 and the EDS maps in Fig. 11 show that Ta is present in some of the oxygen rich regions, but almost no Nb. XRD matches for Nb-rich carbides are consistent with this interpretation and calculated predictions. In contrast, the dense surface scale formed on the diboride and filled pores likely reduces the oxygen partial pressure at the reaction interface. This explains not only the predicted low tantalum content in the oxide formed on the diborides, but also the titanium retained in the boride phase in Figs. 11 and 12 . Hf and Zr oxides are significantly more thermodynamically stable than TiO 2 (see Ellingham diagram in companion paper [12] ); Hf and Zr oxidize preferentially from the boride, leading to Ti 'lagging' the other group IV elements in extent of oxidation. The depletion zone in the Ti map ( Fig. 15 ) indicates that some Ti is still retained in the diboride. The Ta 3 B 4 boride phase indicated by the XRD results is consistent with a tantalum rich composition, an analog to Nb rich substoichiometric carbide in the high entropy carbide case.

4.3. Design of oxidation-resistant HE-UHTCs
as Hf6Ta2O17, which may promote oxidation resistance. However, while there is evidence for the presence of such complex oxides, the results discussed here and for the oxidation of (HfZrTiTaNb)C [9], as well as in a more recent HfC-TaC oxidation study [11], show that excess Ta additions in materials that oxidize intergranularly (such as UHTC carbides) result in liquid oxide formation, which in turn promotes ' premature ' densification of the intergranular oxides (before an external scale can form). In the cases of Ta additions, oxygen transport towards the center of the grains in the oxidation-affected zone is slowed due to the densified intergranular oxide, while intergranular oxygen transport continues to occur into the unoxidized material.
The presence of group VI oxides in the thermally grown scale show that porosity due to volatile oxides is observed on the surface of the scale (Figs. 13 and 15), but the mode of attack was more uniform, despite evidence of preferential oxidation. The pores observed were just below the surface and smaller than that seen in the cross sections of group V containing compositions (e.g., Fig. 16). Any detrimental effects due to the non-group IV elements present in the oxide phase, in this case Mo and W, was minimized due to (a) their oxides having limited solubility in group IV oxides, being volatile and leaving the system, and (b) their preferential retention as a carbide phase. The material consumption data confirms that at the conditions tested, group VI additions to group IV base-material are preferable over group V additions. This is especially true for conditions that likely prevail during hypersonic flight. Thus, the group IV + VI elemental combination is a promising route for the design of HE-UHTCs.

The  objectives  of  this  study  were  to  (a)  determine  the  oxidation resistance  of  HfZrTiTaMo  and  HfZrTiMoW  carbides  and  borides  by measuring material consumption after tests in similar conditions, and then comparing these to the group IV + V compounds (Hf, Zr, Ti, Ta and Nb), the results of which were previously published [9]; (b) explore the compositional effects on the oxidation resistance as described by the periodic trends previously observed; (c) determine whether the preferential oxidation behavior is also observed in other compositions.

A B S T R A C T
Our  earlier  studies  [18,19]  revealed  that  the  gradual  addition  of UHTC  components  to  form  binary,  ternary,  and  quaternary  UHTCs improved their mechanical properties and oxidation resistance remarkably. The resistance to indentation damage proved up to 72 % at an applied load of 200 N, while the formation of protective complex mixed  oxides  with  Hf6Ta2O17 (as  the  primary  phase)  improves  the oxidation  resistance  by > 40  times  in  MC-UHTCs.  Hf6Ta2O17 has  a melting temperature > 2400 ◦ C, low thermal conductivity, and oxygen diffusion, and no phase transformation from RT up to the melting point. In  addition,  the  author  highlighted  the  crack  patching  behavior  in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. This finding was similar to the plasticity of the (HfTaZrNb)C grains during the micro-pillar compression along {110}, < 110 > slip systems [20]. Additionally, Ab initio molecular dynamics simulations validated by experimental fracture testing show that the ductility, plasticity, and fracture resistance of MC-UHTCs improves with increased valence electron concentration (VEC) [21,22].

As we add more components to the UHTC system, Eq (1a) becomes complex on the product side. Transition metal cations form a complex binary,  ternary  mixed  oxide  phases,  and  oxy-carbide  phases.  The retention  of  solid-solution  carbide  phase  in  ternary  and  quaternary UHTC systems was 27.7% and 17.0%. It is worth mentioning that the more the oxide scale thickness, the lesser is the retention of the solidsolution carbide phase in the sample, schematically shown in Fig. 4. This trend can be observed in the present samples as well. The ternary UHTC system with the least oxide scale thickness ( < 3 μ m) showed the highest amount of retained sample phase (~27.7%) post oxidation. The

According  to  our  previous  research  [21],  the  introduction  of  Zr element  into  HfO2-based  oxide  scale  in  the  (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C  ceramic showed  improved  ablation  property  than  that  of  (Hf-Ta-Nb)C  and (Hf-Ta)C,  resulting  from  the  regulatory  effect  of  Zr  element  on  the melting points of surface oxides. 4. Summary
The MC-UHTCs showed remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance during plasma exposure testing compared to binary UHTCs. This has been attributed to the preferential oxidation and a reduction in low melting temperature unary oxide content in the surface oxide scale from > 82% in binary UHTC to < 13% in MC-UHTC. A thermally stable complex protective oxide layer of Hf6Ta2O17 (a primary phase) in MCUHTCs has further suppressed the oxide formation. As a result of the relative thermal stability of transition metals and components in the MCUHTCs, preferential oxidation occurs in MC-UHTCs, i.e., Hf in ternary and Ti in quaternary UHTC system, while both are forming the protective mixed complex oxide layer to remarkably improve ( > 40 times) the oxidation resistance over binary and unary UHTC systems. Both XRD and XPS analysis showed retention of metal carbide phase only in MCUHTCs. The study suggests that MC-UHTCs designed to form a mixed complex protective oxide layer of group IV element under service conditions elicit extended potential in extreme space vehicle applications.

A B S T R A C T
Multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has garnered attention due to their improved thermo-mechanical and oxidation properties. MC-UHTC was progressively formed by adding a UHTC component at  each  step,  producing  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary  UHTC  systems,  processed  by  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS). We investigate the role of multi-elements and thermochemical stability of the surface oxide layer formed during plasma arc jet exposure ( T > 2500 ◦ C) of MC-UHTCs using core-level X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) correlated with the structural investigation. The results indicate that adding a UHTC component improves oxidation resistance due to the assemblage of refractory phases forming complex mixed oxides via preferential oxidation. XPS spectra of Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, and Hf4f showed that the nature of the chemical bonds switched from metal carbides to metal oxides (and metal oxy-carbides) in the binary system with retained metal carbides in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. The absence of a metal carbide peak in the high-resolution C1s scan of the binary system also supports this finding. The principal complex mixed oxide phases emanated in the exiguous oxidation of MC-UHTCs are quantified using Rietveld analysis. Overall, this study suggests that MC-UHTC is a promising material for possible application in thermal protection systems (TPS).
sharp leading edges in re-entry vehicles. These materials have gathered quick attention because of their remarkable properties, which include enhanced mechanical properties [9 -12], improved fracture toughness [11,13,14],  tribological  performance  [15 -17],  creep  resistance  [18], and low thermal conductivity [11,19 -21].

4.  Potential pathways to address challenges involved with HEUHTCs
Based on the limited available data in the literature [13,30,46,58], a comparison  of  the  mechanical  and  oxidation  resistance  of  mono, binary,  ternary,  and  multi-component  UHTC  carbides  (referred  to  as HE-carbide) is summarized and plotted in Fig. 7. The improvement in the hardness (see Fig. 7) has mainly been attributed to the solid-solutioning in the binary, ternary, and HE-UHTCs. The onset temperature at which the oxide starts forming has remarkably improved from 900 °C in monolith UHTCs to > 1100 °C for HE-UHTCs (Fig. 7 ) . This is possibly due to the formation of the complex, layered structure as well as the composition of the oxide scale in a multi-component system. The innermost oxide layer near the unoxidized sample usually has a dense structure with lesser pores (all filled with molten particles) followed by a porous oxide structure with lesser pores (see Fig. 5). Also, the complex composition of the mixed oxides due to multi-constituents restricts the inward  diffusion  of  O2 providing  oxidation  resistance,  discussed  in section 3.2. However, whether all components in a solid-solution of the HE-UHTC  system  react  with  oxygen  simultaneously  or  preferential oxidation, still needs to be looked upon in both isothermal and highspeed  plasma  jet  conditions.  Also,  this  brings  up  a  question  of  how many  constituents  (four,  five,  six,  or  more)  in  a  multi-component system would be sufficient for enhancement in the properties in the absence of entropy quantification. To get an insight into it, a study on gradually raising the constituents in UHTCs from mono, binary and so on is required along with the delineation of the entropy contributions to the properties.

In the cases of Ta additions, oxygen transport towards the center of the grains in the oxidation-affected zone is slowed due to the densified intergranular oxide, while intergranular oxygen transport continues to occur into the unoxidized material.
The presence of group VI oxides in the thermally grown scale show that porosity due to volatile oxides is observed on the surface of the scale (Figs. 13 and 15), but the mode of attack was more uniform, despite evidence of preferential oxidation. The pores observed were just below the surface and smaller than that seen in the cross sections of group V containing compositions (e.g., Fig. 16). Any detrimental effects due to the non-group IV elements present in the oxide phase, in this case Mo and W, was minimized due to (a) their oxides having limited solubility in group IV oxides, being volatile and leaving the system, and (b) their preferential retention as a carbide phase. This is especially true for conditions that likely prevail during hypersonic flight. Thus, the group IV + VI elemental combination is a promising route for the design of HE-UHTCs.

Applied Surface Science
A companion  publication [7]  revealed  that  MC-UHTCs  have remarkable oxidation resistance compared to unary and binary UHTC attributed to mixed complex oxide phase formation with Hf6Ta2O17 as a major phase. The present work focuses on qualitatively and quantitatively investigating the content and chemical composition of the oxide scale in the binary and MC-UHTCs using core level spectroscopy correlated with phase quantification via Rietveld analysis. Based on the review of various available phase diagrams of a binary and ternary system, the possible combination of oxide phases in the chosen chemistry for the present work is summarized in Table 1.
Fig. 2. XRD pattern of the surface oxide layer formed on the plasma exposed binary and MC-UHTC systems. The colored dots in each XRD pattern represents the experimental raw data, while the lines denote calculated data after Rietveld refinement.
The present study explains how preferential oxidation based on the components added in the UHTC system can improve or deteriorate its oxidation resistance. These findings can enable oxidation-resistant designs for non-oxide multi-component UHTCs.

3.3. Plasma arc jet exposure
Further,  the  cross-sectional  SEM  of  samples  was  conducted  to quantify the thickness of the oxide film formation on the sample surface due to the melting of the Ta2O5  phase, see Fig. 9c and d. The minor cracking in the 50HO-50TO sample vs. wide crack opening in the 70HO30TO sample in the unaffected region elicits that the intense plasma exposure was enough to create thermal stresses, analogous to the strain analysis in Table 1. The thickness of melted and then resolidified oxide film is 210 nm and 297 nm in 50HO-50TO and 70HO-30TO samples. Although there is no significant change in the thickness of melted oxide in both the samples, there are significantly more apparent melted regions in the 70HO-30TO sample. Hence, the 50HO-50TO sample has better  thermal  stability  than  the  70HO-30TO  sample.  In  terms  of improving the oxidation resistance of solid-solution binary UHTCs or MC-UHTCs, it can be anticipated that a composition with equal or higher content of TaC will have a higher probability of Hf6Ta2O17  phase formation during plasma exposure. This complex mixed oxide phase act as a protective layer that suppresses further oxidation. Also, the protective layer in UHTC components is formed due to the preferential oxidation of various  components  at  service  temperature  [16].  The  present  study shows that the complex mixed oxide formation during service ( > 2500 ◦ C) of solid-solution binary UHTCs and MC-UHTCs improves its oxidation resistance due to the thermal stability and mechanical integrity of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase. UHTC composition, thus designed to form a protective and thermally stable Hf6Ta2O17 phase under extreme conditions, will provide new directions to the materials for reusable hypersonic applications.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have shown aspiration to overcome challenges in the thermal protection system (TPS) by designing new materials referred to as multi-component UHTCs (MC-UHTCs) in the compositional space. MC-UHTCs have shown remarkable improvement in oxidation resistance due to the formation of the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure during plasma exposure. Herein, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure is synthesized via a solid-state reaction between HfO2 and Ta2O5 powder mixtures during spark plasma sintering (SPS). The compositions chosen are 50 vol% of HfO2 -50 vol% of Ta2O5 (50HO-50TO) and 70 vol% of HfO2 -30 vol% of Ta2O5 (70HO-30TO). The phase quantification via Rietveld analysis showed Hf6Ta2O17 as a principal phase with some residual Ta2O5  phase in both the samples. The high-temperature thermal stability of the samples was evaluated using high-velocity plasma jet exposure for up to 3 min. 50HO-50TO was able to withstand the intense plasma condition, which is attributed to the higher content of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase (~84%) and lower strain in the Ta2O5 phase. The augmentation in the Hf6Ta2O17 phase to 94.7% (in 50HO-50TO) post plasma exposure has been attributed to the invariant transformation from a liquid state to Hf6Ta2O17 at temperatures > 2500 ◦ C during testing. The mechanical integrity is elucidated from the insignificant change in the hardness ~13.3 GPa before and 11.2 GPa after plasma exposure of the 50HO-50TO sample. As a result, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure ' s thermo-mechanical stability suggests developing novel oxidation-resistant MC-UHTCs in compositional space for reusable space vehicle applications.

[10] showed oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-graphene nanoplatelets (1 to 5 vol%) (GNP) composites processed at temperature of 1850 ◦ C under high temperature plasma flow of 30 kW power for 60 s and showed that addition of GNP suppresses formation of oxide layer up to 60%. However, high temperature thermal conductivity for TaC based composites is not available in literature, which is one of the important attributes to survive high temperature exposure.
Improvement of fracture toughness with SiC and CNT addition in TaC-based UHTCs is demonstrated elsewhere [39]. Hence, the scope of the present work is to develop an understanding of the oxidation mechanisms of TaC-SiC-CNT composites to predict the lifetime performance of UHTCs under operational conditions. Oxidation behaviour is analysed in terms of the oxide layer formation during testing. To supplement plasma arc jet testing, thermogravimetric analysis is carried out for 30 min. at 1500 ◦ C in oxygen environment. Thermal conductivity of these materials have been measured using laser flash diffusivity technique and, accordingly, correlation of the heat transfer mechanism during plasma arc jet testing is established. An attempt has been made to associate the synergistic effect of SiC and CNT reinforcement on the performance of TaC-based UHTCs through these investigations.

Post-exposure of the samples showed that 50HO-50TO survived the plasma exposure even up to 3 min, with some apparent cracks on the sample surface and a slight change in sample color (white) due to the removal of diffused C, see Fig. 7a. However, the 70HO-30TO sample couldn ' t sustain the thermal shock associated with plasma exposure and broke into two pieces. The higher thermal stability of the 50HO-50TO sample has been attributed to the higher content of Hf6Ta2O17  phase formation during SPS. This indicates that the oxide composition is important in providing oxidation resistance  to  UHTCs. 1.1. Expected oxidation behavior of group IV compounds with group V and VI additions
Group V additions to group IV ceramics in various forms, mainly as Ta compounds, have resulted in some limited improvement in oxidation behavior, whether through liquid phase assisted sintering of the group IV oxides, the doping of group IV oxides with higher valence ions to limit oxygen transport through oxygen vacancies [13], or the formation of ordered compounds such as A6B2O17 (A = Hf, Zr; B = Ta, Nb) [13,22] (Fig. 1(a)). On the other hand, liquid oxide formation at more aggressive conditions  and  ultra-high  temperatures  has  been  shown  to  be  detrimental to the oxidation behavior of UHTCs. Results discussed in previous work [9] for the group IV + V combination shows that the oxidation behavior of the solid solution closely matches the design considerations [5] from the constituent elements, i.e., the formation of group V oxides, for example, promoted the formation of oxides with low melting temperatures  that  resulted  in  a  decrease  in  oxidation  resistance  of  the material.

A B S T R A C T
High entropy ultra-high temperature ceramics (HE-UHTCs) have garnered intense research interest due to the potential for optimized oxidation and mechanical properties for extreme environment applications. HE-UHTCs are  expected to oxidize according to the thermodynamic favorability of their respective oxidation reactions, which varies according to the periodic grouping. Based on this, the oxidation resistance of equimolar (metalsbasis) group IV + V (HfZrTiTaNb), group IV + V + VI (HfZrTiTaMo) and group IV + VI (HfZrTiMoW) carbides and borides were evaluated at 1700 ◦ C in 1 mol% O2  for 5 min and compared. Group IV elements oxidized preferentially in all three compositions. Group V element-containing carbides exhibited the lowest oxidation resistance, attributed to the formation of intergranular liquid oxides. (HfZrTiMoW)C exhibited the best resistance among the carbides. The diborides exhibited similar material consumption, reinforcing the hypothesis that the oxidation behavior under these conditions is controlled by the presence of boria. These findings provide direction for HE-UHTC composition design for oxidation resistance.

4. Conclusions
Oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-SiC-CNT composites was evaluated through plasma arc jet exposure under heat flux of 2.5 MW/m 2 . Oxidation behaviour is characterised in terms of oxide layer formation, its thickness and residual stresses. After exposure to harsh aerothermal conditions, the structural integrity of these UHTC composites were measured in terms of critical energy release rate ( G IC ) which increases up to 2.5 times ( ∼ 58 to 139 Jm -2 ) with synergistic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in TaC. The protective SiO2 formation with SiC reinforcement in TaC suggested an extension of stability domain which resulted in an improved oxidation resistance. It is elicited that CNT addition improves oxidation resistance of TaC by grain sealing mechanism. Raman spectroscopic analysis elicited the presence of carbonaceous material even after plasma exposure, further supplemented by TEM analysis of T15S15C. In addition, enhanced thermal conductivity with temperature for all composites provides an indication of improved oxidation resistance via heat dissipation. Thermal studies up to 1500 ◦ C have confirmed that the temperature of oxide formation shifts towards higher temperature (from 735 ◦ C to 901 ◦ C) and decrease in the enthalpy of oxide formation (from 4.5 to 3.5 kJ/g) with SiC and CNT reinforcement. Such an observation insinuates synergetic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in enhancing the thermal stability and oxidation resistance of TaC-based UHTC composites.

3.2. Phase quantification of surface oxide layer
In the view of kinetic theory, multi-element oxide formation is highly favorable due to the comparable diffusivity of Group IV and V oxides [39].  Based  on  the  thermal  stability  of  formed  oxides,  preferential oxidation  occurs  governing  the  quantity  of  the  multi-element  oxides formed [26,28]. Among all refractory elements, oxides from group IV elements (Hf, Ti) owing to the highest melting point (Ta2O5 ~ 1872 ◦ C; Nb2O5  ~ 1915 ◦ C;  TiO2  ~ 1843 ◦ C;  HfO2  and other mixed complex oxides > 2400 ◦ C  [2,7,29])  are  most  thermodynamically  favored. Hence, ternary UHTC has oxide scale rich in Hf while quaternary rich in Ti (see Table 2). To further supplement the result obtained from XRD analysis,  core-level  spectroscopy  using  the  XPS  technique  has  been carried out.
